[A21D 2/00] Treatment of flour or dough by adding materials thereto before or during baking -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ingredients for bakery products.  Special chemical leavening composition (e.g. encapsulated chemical leavening composition)  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Batters, dough, or mixtures before baking -> A21D10/00
[A21D 2/02] by adding inorganic substances -- A21D 2/02 covers processes that treat flour or dough for baking by adding inorganic substances, such as salts, leavening agents, anti-caking or fortifying compounds, before or during the baking preparation. The aim is to modify the dough's properties, texture or nutritional profile.
[A21D 2/04] Oxygen -- A21D 2/04 "Oxygen" covers the addition of oxygen or oxygen-containing agents to flour or dough as a treatment step before or during baking, aimed at modifying dough chemistry, rise, or texture. It is a specific subcategory of the broader CPC group for treating flour or dough for baking.
[A21D 2/06] Reducing agents -- A21D 2/06 covers the use of reducing agents added to flour or dough to modify its properties during preparation or baking. Such agents lower oxidation, improve dough strength, and can enhance crumb softness or volume in the final baked product.
[A21D 2/08] by adding organic substances -- A21D 2/08 covers methods of treating flour or dough by adding organic substances before or during baking, including sugars, oils, enzymes, flavors, preservatives or other natural additives that alter the dough's properties or the final baked product.
[A21D 2/10] Hydrocarbons -- The A21D 2/10 subclass relates to the use of hydrocarbons-such as oils, fats, waxes or petroleum-derived agents-in the treatment of flour or dough. It covers adding these substances before or during baking to act as lubricants, anti-stick agents, or texture modifiers, thereby influencing the properties of the final baked product.
[A21D 2/12] Halohydrocarbons -- A21D 2/12 refers to the use of halohydrocarbons as additives in the treatment of flour or dough, applied before or during baking to modify texture, stability or shelf-life, such as dough conditioners, preservatives or leavening agents. This subclass falls under the broader category of foodstuff processing in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[A21D 2/14] Organic oxygen compounds -- A21D 2/14 refers to the use of organic oxygen compounds-such as acids, enzymes, oxidizing agents or other oxygen-containing molecules-in the treatment of flour or dough before or during baking. The classification covers additives that modify the dough's properties, improve leavening, enhance texture, or extend shelf-life by chemical interaction with the dough constituents.
[A21D 2/145] Acids, anhydrides or salts thereof -- A21D 2/145 covers the use of acids, their anhydrides or salts as additives in the treatment of flour or dough before or during baking, falling under the CPC hierarchy of Human Necessities → Foodstuffs → Treatment of flour or dough for baking by addition of materials. This code classifies formulations that modify dough properties such as pH, texture or rise through acidic or basic agents.
[A21D 2/16] Fatty acid esters -- The CPC subclass A21D 2/16 covers the use of fatty-acid esters as additives in flour or dough, applied before or during baking to modify properties such as texture, crumb structure, shelf-life, or flavor. It includes emulsifiers, oils, or other esterified fatty compounds that affect dough handling or the quality of the final baked product.
[A21D 2/165] Triglycerides -- A21D 2/165 relates to the use of triglycerides as additives in the treatment of flour or dough for baking. It covers the incorporation of triglyceride ingredients to improve dough properties such as texture, stability, or shelf-life before or during baking.
[A21D 2/18] Carbohydrates -- The A21D 2/18 subclass concerns the addition of carbohydrate substances to flour or dough to modify its properties during baking, such as texture, moisture retention, browning or nutritional value. It includes starches, sugars, fibers and other carbohydrate-based additives applied before or during the baking process.
[A21D 2/181] Sugars or sugar alcohols  honey  A21D2/34 -- A21D 2/181 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers the addition of sugars, sugar alcohols, and honey to flour or dough before or during baking. This entry is part of a broader category that deals with the treatment of foodstuffs for human consumption.
[A21D 2/183] Natural gums -- A21D 2/183 relates to the addition of natural gums to flour or dough during preparation for baking. It covers the use of plant-derived polysaccharide gums to modify texture, retain moisture, and improve stability of baked goods.
[A21D 2/185] Biosynthetic gums -- A21D 2/185 relates to the addition of biosynthetic gums to flour or dough as a treatment before or during baking. These artificially produced polymers are used to modify texture, retain moisture and enhance the stability of baked products.
[A21D 2/186] Starches; Derivatives thereof -- A21D 2/186 covers starches and their derivatives that are added to flour or dough for baking, including native, chemically or enzymatically modified starches used to improve texture, stability or shelf-life during processing.
[A21D 2/188] Cellulose; Derivatives thereof -- The A21D 2/188 subclass covers the use of cellulose and its chemical derivatives as additives in flour or dough. It includes methods and compositions that incorporate these materials before or during baking to modify texture, water retention, volume, stability or shelf-life of the finished product.
[A21D 2/20] Peroxides -- A21D 2/20 covers the use of peroxide compounds added to flour or dough before or during baking, typically as bleaching or oxidising agents that improve colour, texture or gluten development. This sub-class falls under treatment of flour or dough for baking within the foodstuffs section of the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[A21D 2/22] Ascorbic acid -- A21D 2/22 covers the use of ascorbic acid as an additive in the treatment of flour or dough for baking. The code pertains to adding this antioxidant or dough conditioner before or during baking to improve dough strength, volume, or crumb structure.
[A21D 2/24] Organic nitrogen compounds -- It covers the addition of organic nitrogen compounds such as amino acids, proteins, urea or their derivatives to flour or dough before or during baking to modify nutritional value, dough rheology, leavening or texture.
[A21D 2/245] Amino acids, nucleic acids -- A21D 2/245 covers the addition of amino acids or nucleic acids to flour or dough as a treatment step before or during baking, aiming to modify nutritional content, dough handling or final product qualities. This subclass includes any process where such biomolecules are incorporated as functional additives in baked-goods manufacturing.
[A21D 2/26] Proteins -- A21D 2/26 covers the use of protein additives in flour or dough during baking. It includes introducing sources such as gluten, soy, whey, egg or other isolated proteins to modify dough strength, elasticity, nutritional value or other functional properties before or while the product is baked.
[A21D 2/261] Animal proteins -- A21D 2/261 covers the addition of animal-derived proteins to flour or dough to modify its baking properties, such as elasticity, water retention, or nutritional value. It includes proteins from meat, milk, eggs, fish and other animal sources used before or during the baking process.
[A21D 2/262] from eggs -- A21D 2/262 pertains to the treatment of flour or dough by adding egg products, enhancing texture and nutritional value before or during the baking process. This classification is part of a broader category that includes various methods of treating flour or dough with different materials.
[A21D 2/263] from dairy products -- A21D 2/263 concerns the treatment of flour or dough for baking by adding dairy products such as milk, butter, cheese, yogurt or whey. The addition is made before or during baking to modify texture, flavor, nutritional value or shelf life of the baked goods.
[A21D 2/264] Vegetable proteins -- A21D 2/264 refers to the use of vegetable proteins as additives in flour or dough to modify its properties before or during baking, encompassing preparations that improve texture, nutritional value or functionality of baked goods.
[A21D 2/265] from cereals, flour, bran -- The A21D 2/265 subclass covers the use of ingredients obtained from cereals, flour or bran as additives for treating flour or dough prior to or during baking, such as nutritional enhancers, dough conditioners or functional powders.
[A21D 2/266] from leguminous or other vegetable seeds; from press-cake or oil bearing seeds -- A21D 2/266 pertains to the treatment of flour or dough for baking by adding ingredients that originate from leguminous or other vegetable seeds, including press-cake or oil-bearing seed extracts, to modify the dough's characteristics before or during baking.
[A21D 2/267] Microbial proteins -- A21D 2/267 refers to the use of proteins produced by microorganisms as additives in flour or dough preparations for baking. It covers incorporation of microbial protein isolates or extracts to modify dough properties, nutritional value, or leavening performance.
[A21D 2/268] Hydrolysates from proteins  hydrolysis of proteins  A23J3/30 -- A21D 2/268 pertains to the addition of protein hydrolysates to flour or dough before or during baking, involving the process of protein hydrolysis as detailed in classification A23J3/30. This entry is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the EPO and USPTO.
[A21D 2/28] Organic sulfur compounds -- A21D 2/28 covers the addition of organic sulfur compounds to flour or dough during preparation for baking, aiming to modify dough characteristics, enhance gluten development, improve texture or extend shelf life.
[A21D 2/30] Organic phosphorus compounds -- A21D 2/30 refers to the use of organic phosphorus compounds as additives for treating flour or dough, typically applied before or during baking to improve texture, stability or shelf-life. The classification covers phosphorus-based dough conditioners, enzymes, or preservatives that modify dough properties in the baking process.
[A21D 2/32] Phosphatides -- A21D 2/32 pertains to the use of phosphatides, such as lecithin, as additives in flour or dough to improve handling, stability and baking performance. These compounds are incorporated before or during baking to modify texture, reduce stickiness and extend shelf-life.
[A21D 2/34] Animal material -- This subclass covers the addition of animal-derived ingredients such as meat, fat, gelatin, milk, eggs or other animal products to flour or dough before or during baking to modify texture, flavor or nutritional value.
[A21D 2/36] Vegetable material -- A21D 2/36 covers the use of vegetable material added to flour or dough to modify its properties before or during baking, such as improving texture, flavor, or nutritional value. The classification applies to any vegetable-derived additives incorporated in the treatment of dough for baked products.
[A21D 2/362] Leguminous plants -- A21D 2/362 covers the use of leguminous plants-such as beans, peas, lentils or soy-in the treatment of flour or dough before or during baking, including their addition as protein-rich or functional ingredients to improve texture, nutritional value, or leavening performance.
[A21D 2/364] Nuts, e.g. cocoa -- A21D 2/364 relates to the addition of nuts, for example cocoa, to flour or dough during preparation for baking
[A21D 2/366] Tubers, roots -- A21D 2/366 pertains to the use of tubers and root vegetables as added ingredients in flour or dough, describing their incorporation or treatment during preparation or baking to modify texture, flavor, nutritional value or functional properties.
[A21D 2/368] Fermentation by-products, e.g. grapes, hops -- A21D 2/368 covers the addition of fermentation by-products such as grapes, hops or similar residues to flour or dough during baking. It relates to treating the dough with these materials to modify flavor, texture, or preserving qualities.
[A21D 2/38] Seed germs -- A21D 2/38 covers the incorporation of seed germs into flour or dough as a treatment applied before or during baking, aiming to improve nutritional value or functional properties. It is classified under foodstuffs for the treatment of flour or dough by adding materials.
[A21D 2/40] Apparatus for the chemical treatment of flour or dough -- A21D 2/40 covers machines and devices designed to chemically treat flour or dough, such as mixers, injectors or dosing equipment that add additives before or during baking to modify the dough's properties. The apparatus may incorporate systems for precise material handling, dosing control and uniform distribution within the dough mass.

[A22] BUTCHERING -- A22 BUTCHERING covers the methods, tools and apparatus used to slaughter, eviscerate, split, debone and otherwise process livestock and other animals for human consumption, including apparatus for handling carcasses, meat-cutting machines, and related sanitation steps. It also includes techniques for preserving meat during the butchering process.

[A22B 3/086] Stunning devices specially adapted for poultry -- A22B 3/086 covers devices designed to stun poultry prior to slaughter, including electric, gas or mechanical systems specifically adapted for birds. [A22B 5/0088] Electric stimulation of carcasses -- The A22B 5/0088 entry covers methods and devices for electrically stimulating carcasses after slaughter to accelerate rigor mortis, improve meat tenderness and colour, and reduce microbial growth. [A22B 5/0094] Cutting or blocking the rectum of a carcass, e.g. for avoiding fecal contamination -- This classification covers devices or methods that cut or seal a carcass's rectum during or after slaughter to prevent fecal contamination of the meat. It includes accessories such as clamps, sutures or sealing tools specifically designed for hygienic handling of the gastrointestinal tract in butchering operations.

[A22B 7/002] Devices for hanging animal carcasses while being conveyed or stored, e.g. gambrels, hooks -- A22B 7/002 refers to devices that hang animal carcasses while they are conveyed or stored in slaughterhouse settings, including gambrels, hooks and similar apparatus designed to support carcasses during handling.

The classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the EPO and USPTO.

[A22B 7/007] Means containing information relative to the carcass that can be attached to or are embedded in the conveying means -- This subclass covers devices that carry carcass data and are fixed to or integrated within the transport equipment in a slaughterhouse, such as tags, sensors, or displays that convey information about the animal's carcass during processing.

[A22C 11/02] Sausage filling or stuffing machines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sausage filling or stuffing machines, parts thereof.

[A22C 11/0218] with multiple interchangeable stuffing horns, e.g. magazine arrangements -- A22C 11/0218 covers sausage-making apparatus that employs two or more interchangeable stuffing horns, allowing the operator to switch horn diameters rapidly, often via a magazine-type arrangement that holds multiple horns in ready position for quick replacement. This design streamlines changing the product size without disassembling the machine.

It includes the handling, thawing, conditioning and preparation steps required to turn stored raw material into a finished sausage product.

This entry falls under the broader categories of human necessities, butchering, processing meat, poultry or fish, and specifically sausage making.
[A22C 11/0272] for casing depletion -- A22C 11/0272 covers techniques and equipment used in sausage making to remove air, moisture or other contents from natural or artificial casings, ensuring uniform filling and preventing rupture. It falls within the broader class of processing meat, poultry or fish, specifically under butchering and meat-product preparation.

[A22C 11/06] with piston operated by liquid or gaseous means -- A22C 11/06 covers sausage-making devices in which a piston is driven by hydraulic or pneumatic power to extrude, shape or fill casings. The classification applies to equipment that uses liquid- or gas-pressured pistons to form meat, poultry or fish products into sausages.
[A22C 11/08] with pressing-worm or other rotary-mounted pressing-members -- This CPC subclass deals with sausage-making processes that employ a pressing-worm or other rotary-mounted pressing members to compress and shape meat mixtures. It covers equipment where the pressing elements rotate, such as a worm or similar rotary press used in sausage production.
[A22C 11/10] Apparatus for twisting sausages -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Subdividing a continuous tubular sausage casing into a strand of linked individual sausages.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Subdividing filled tubes by sealing or securing -> B65B9/12
[A22C 11/104] by means of shear or blade elements -- A22C 11/104 pertains to the method of sausage making that involves cutting or chopping meat using shear or blade elements, ensuring precise and efficient processing in the food industry.
[A22C 11/105] The sheer or blade elements being displaceable parallel to the sausage string in order to create a narrow point free of meat -- A22C 11/105 covers sausage-making apparatus in which the cutting or sheer blades can shift parallel to the casing string to form a narrow, meat-free tip at the end of the sausage. The movable blade element creates a tapered point without removing meat from the main mass, facilitating uniform linking or packaging. This sub-group falls under processing meat, poultry or fish within the broader human necessities category.
[A22C 11/107] A string passing between two rotary members comprising dividing elements cooperating with each other -- The code A22C 11/107 covers a device in which a string or strap runs between two rotating members that contain cooperating dividing elements, enabling the simultaneous cutting and shaping of meat mixtures during sausage production.
[A22C 11/108] with dividing elements located on the surface of a single rotary member -- A22C 11/108 covers sausage-making apparatus in which the cutting or dividing elements are mounted on the surface of a single rotary member, such as a rotating drum or cylinder, so that the meat or meat-protein mass is divided while it is conveyed around the rotating surface.

The process involves tying or knotting the product to create a segmented sausage. This sub-class is part of the broader category for processing meat, poultry or fish in the CPC system.
[A22C 11/125] by clipping; Removal of clips -- A22C 11/125 concerns the removal of clips that have been used to secure sausage casings during the clipping stage. It covers methods and apparatus for detaching or extracting these clips after the sausages have been formed.

It includes devices and steps that form, secure, or shape the loop in the meat matrix.

The casings are derived from animal or recombinant collagen and serve to encase meat products during cooking or storage.
[A22C 13/0026] Chemical treatment of natural gut -- A22C 13/0026 covers the chemical treatment of natural gut used as sausage casings, including methods for cleaning, preserving, or modifying the gut to improve its suitability for meat, poultry or fish processing.
[A22C 13/02] Shirring of sausage casings -- A22C 13/02 covers the shirring of sausage casings, a technique that gathers or pleats the casing to create a decorative pattern, improve handling, or aid in cooking. It is a specific step within the broader processing of meat, poultry, or fish for human consumption.

[A22C 17/0086] Calculating cutting patterns based on visual recognition -- A22C 17/0086 covers computer-based or electronic systems that use visual recognition-such as cameras or imaging sensors-to analyze a carcass or cut and automatically calculate optimal cutting patterns for meat, poultry or fish, improving yield and efficiency in butchering operations.
[A22C 17/0093] Handling, transporting or packaging pieces of meat -- This CPC subclass A22C 17/0093 covers equipment and methods for handling, transporting or packaging individual pieces of meat after they have been separated from the carcass. [A22C 18/00] Plants, factories, or the like for processing meat -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Complete integral processes for processing meat and plants for their implementation  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Processing poultry -> A22C21/00 - Processing fish -> A22C25/00
[A22C 2011/101] for pinching and twisting -- A22C 2011/101 covers hand-operated tools and devices whose principal function is to pinch and twist objects, such as pliers, tweezers, torque-applying implements and similar apparatuses used for gripping, bending, rotating or otherwise deforming workpieces in manufacturing, medical or maintenance operations.
[A22C 2011/102] and twisting in opposite directions -- A22C 2011/102 falls within the animal-feeding subclass of the CPC and relates to devices or methods that employ simultaneous twisting in opposite directions, such as counter-rotating shafts or rollers, to mix or process feed more uniformly. It addresses the mechanical action of opposite-direction twist for improving homogenisation, size reduction or texture in animal-feed preparation.
[A22C 2013/002] made by extrusion -- A22C 2013/002 classifies foodstuffs that are produced by extrusion, such as pasta, noodles, snack items and other extruded edible products, together with the apparatus and methods used for the extrusion process.
[A22C 2013/0023] coextruded together with the food product -- A22C 2013/0023 covers processes in which a food product is co-extruded together with another material, forming a combined structure where the food and an extruded layer (such as a coating, film or barrier) are produced simultaneously. The classification includes apparatus and methods for applying such co-extruded layers to improve protection, handling or convenience of the food.
[A22C 2013/003] with peeling aids, e.g. reducing adhesion between casing and product -- A22C 2013/003 pertains to food or other product containers that incorporate peeling aids to lessen the adhesion between the inner surface of the casing and the contained product, enabling easier removal of the product from the package. This subclass covers designs, materials or treatments applied to the container wall that facilitate peeling or release of the product.
[A22C 2013/0033] with at least one layer of a fat impermeable material -- A22C 2013/0033 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the European and United States patent offices, refers to a specific type of packaging or wrapping for meat products that includes at least one layer designed to prevent fat penetration. This classification helps in organizing patents related to innovative meat processing techniques and materials.
[A22C 2013/0036] with features allowing an easy opening and/or removal of the casing -- A22C 2013/0036 classifies packaging for fruit or vegetables that incorporates design elements-such as hinged sections, perforated lines, pull tabs, or snap-fit seams-that enable the casing to be opened or removed with minimal effort, improving user convenience and product access. This subgroup is used for inventions that focus on easy-opening or easy-removal features of the outer package.
[A22C 2013/004] with at least one layer of a gas, e.g. oxygen, water vapour, impermeable material -- A22C 2013/004 covers articles or devices that incorporate at least one gas-filled layer-such as oxygen, water vapour, or an impermeable barrier-to create or preserve a specific atmosphere around a product, commonly used in packaging, storage chambers or protective liners.
[A22C 2013/0043] with at least one layer of a water impermeable material -- A22C 2013/0043 covers protective articles for animal or plant products that incorporate at least one water-impermeable layer, such as films, laminates or coatings that block moisture penetration to preserve freshness, prevent spoilage, and extend shelf life. This feature is applied in packaging, coverings or wraps used in the food and agricultural sectors.
[A22C 2013/0046] suitable for impregnation with flavouring substances, e.g. caramel, liquid smoke, spices -- The code A22C 2013/0046 designates food items that are suitable for being impregnated with flavouring substances such as caramel, liquid smoke or spices, typically through processes that inject, spray or coat the product to enhance taste. This classification covers the preparation and treatment steps that enable the absorption or adhesion of these flavour additives.
[A22C 2013/005] monolayer casings -- A22C deals with casings used for food or other products, describing their material and construction. The sub-entry 2013/005 specifies monolayer casings, which consist of a single continuous film or sheet rather than multiple layers. Such casings are employed for items like sausages, cheese sticks or confectionery where a simple, uniform barrier is sufficient.
[A22C 2013/0053] multilayer casings -- Multilayer casings are composite packaging structures formed by two or more layers of material, each selected for a specific function such as gas-barrier, moisture resistance, mechanical strength, or printability. The layers are typically bonded through co-extrusion or lamination to create a flexible yet protective sheath used for foods like sausages, cheeses or cured meats, enhancing shelf life while allowing processing and handling advantages.
[A22C 2013/0056] nets and similar open structures to hold sausages and meat in general -- A22C 2013/0056 covers open-work netting and comparable structures designed to hold sausages, cured meats or other meat products during processing, storage or display. The classification includes woven, knitted or perforated nets, baskets and mesh trays that provide ventilation while maintaining the shape and positioning of the meat items. It excludes rigid containers or closed packaging that fully enclose the product.
[A22C 2013/0059] thermoplastic casings, casings with at least one layer of thermoplastic material -- The CPC subgroup A22C 2013/0059 covers protective casings primarily composed of thermoplastic material, including structures that consist of a single thermoplastic layer or combine thermoplastic with other layers. These casings are designed to enclose, protect, and possibly seal equipment or components, leveraging the durability, moldability and chemical resistance of thermoplastic polymers.
[A22C 2013/0063] containing polyamide, e.g. nylon, aramide -- A22C 2013/0063 designates inventions where the food, feed or related product includes polyamide polymers such as nylon or aramid, typically for structural reinforcement, barrier properties or functional additives. The code is used to classify items that incorporate these synthetic fibers or resins as integral components of the consumable material.
[A22C 2013/0066] casings according to the presence or absence of seams -- A22C 2013/0066 is the CPC subgroup for casings classified according to the presence or absence of seams, distinguishing sealed, seamless or partially sealed containers used in packaging, such as food or pharmaceutical casings.
[A22C 2013/0069] seamed casings, casings with at least one longitudinal seam -- A22C 2013/0069 covers seamed casings, meaning flexible tubes or sleeves used to encase food products that incorporate at least one longitudinal seam along their length. The seam may be formed during manufacturing or added later to join the edges of the casing, providing a closed or partially closed tube for stuffing meat, fish, cheese or similar items. This classification is used for inventions related to the design, production, or treatment of such casings.
[A22C 2013/0073] seamless casings made out of a continuous seamless tubular sheet -- A22C 2013/0073 relates to the manufacture of continuous seamless tubular sheets that are formed into casings without any seams, chiefly for food products such as sausages. It includes processes such as extrusion, casting or similar forming methods and any subsequent treatments to achieve the required thickness, strength or surface characteristics.
[A22C 2013/0076] treated in order to be conferred a rough appearance -- A22C 2013/0076 covers processes and treatments applied to materials or products that intentionally create a rough or textured surface, such as sanding, brushing, shot blasting, embossing or chemical etching, whereby the final appearance is deliberately non-smooth for functional or aesthetic purposes.
[A22C 2013/0079] printable sausage casings or sausage casings showing any kind of indicia -- The A22C 2013/0079 entry concerns sausage casings that are manufactured to carry printed or otherwise applied indicia, such as logos, branding, nutritional information, or decorative designs. It includes both edible and non-edible casings that can be printed before or after forming, and covers methods for applying or fixing such markings onto the casings.
[A22C 2013/0083] biaxially oriented -- A22C 2013/0083 covers sheet or film materials that have been biaxially oriented, meaning they are stretched simultaneously in two perpendicular directions to improve strength, clarity, or barrier properties. The classification includes processes for producing such oriented sheets and the resulting products, typically polymer films, paper, or similar flexible substrates.
[A22C 2013/0086] shrinkable casings -- A22C 2013/0086 relates to shrinkable casings, which are flexible film or polymer sleeves that contract when heated to tightly surround food or other items. The classification covers their design, material composition and methods of applying heat to achieve a sealed, protective layer for products such as sausages, cheese, fish or fruit.
[A22C 2013/0089] smokable casings, e.g. permeable to liquid smoke or phenol -- The CPC subclass A22C 2013/0089 covers casings intended for smoking that are engineered to allow liquids such as liquid smoke or phenol to pass through them. These permeable structures are used in tobacco or related products to deliver flavor or processing agents during combustion.
[A22C 2013/0093] textile casings, casings with at least one layer of textile material -- A22C 2013/0093 pertains to textile casings, specifically casings that incorporate at least one layer of textile material. These are used for products such as food items, medical devices or industrial components where a flexible yet strong covering is required.
[A22C 2013/0096] cellulosic -- A22C covers food, foodstuffs and tobacco and their processing, focusing on cellulosic materials such as cellulose, celluloses, and related derivatives used as additives, dietary fibers, texture agents or functional ingredients in food products and related manufacturing processes.
[A22C 2013/021] with pressurised air -- A22C 2013/021 is a CPC subclass that deals with animal-feed preparation techniques employing pressurised air, such as aerating, mixing, or conveying feed material to affect texture, granulation or microbial control during manufacturing. This entry covers both apparatuses and processes that use compressed air as a functional element in feed production.
[A22C 2013/025] Severing the shired food casing -- A22C 2013/025 relates to methods and apparatus for cutting or removing a protective skin, sheath or casing from food products during processing, such as peeling, uncasing or de-hulling of fish, crustaceans, molluscs or similar items. It falls under the broader CPC group for processing of aquatic foodstuffs.
[A22C 2013/026] Shirring netting onto a tube -- A22C 2013/026 relates to the manufacturing of netting by shirring, where a flexible net is gathered and attached around a tubular form to create a cylindrical or conical shape. The process is used for producing items such as hose reinforcement, flexible tubing, or decorative sleeves, and includes steps for securing the net to the tube and adjusting its tension.
[A22C 2013/028] Supplying and shirring casings -- A22C 2013/028 covers equipment and processes for supplying and shirring casings used in sausage and similar meat product manufacture. It includes devices that load, transport, and compress (shirring) natural or artificial casings to prepare them for stuffing, as well as the associated control systems.
[A22C 21/00] Processing poultry -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All types of machinery and operations performed specifically on poultry:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Killing poultry -> A22B3/086 - Eviscerating carcasses -> A22B5/0005 - Deboning carcasses in general -> A22B5/0035 - Slaughterhouse gambrels and hooks -> A22B7/001  **Glossary:** - Trussing: tying and/or restraining a poultry carcass with twine, cord or other elements so that it can be further processed (e.g. in order to facilitate packaging or enable even cooking) - Plucking: pulling the feathers from a bird's carcass
[A22C 21/0007] Poultry shackles -- A22C 21/0007 covers poultry shackles, which are restraint devices used during the butchering and processing of chickens, turkeys and other fowl. These shackles secure the bird by the legs or neck to simplify bleeding, evisceration and further meat handling, and are designed for integration into automated or manual processing lines.
[A22C 21/0015] Killing poultry entering the processing machine  slaughtering pliers, slaughtering shears  A22B3/08 -- A22C 21/0015 covers devices that kill poultry as they enter a processing line, specifically mechanical pliers or shears used to grasp and slaughter the birds before further handling. The code includes slaughtering tools integrated with or attached to the feeding mechanism to ensure rapid humane killing at the point-of-entry.
[A22C 21/0023] Dividing poultry -- The A22C 21/0023 entry covers techniques and apparatus for dividing poultry carcasses into portions such as breasts, thighs, wings, and other cuts. It includes methods of separating bone-in-meat pieces for further processing, packaging or cooking, and relates to the broader category of meat and fish processing within human necessities.
[A22C 21/003] Filleting poultry, i.e. extracting, cutting or shaping poultry fillets -- A22C 21/003 covers techniques for filleting poultry, that is the extraction, cutting and shaping of poultry fillets. It falls under the processing poultry subgroup of the butchering category within the human necessities section of the CPC system. This entry specifies methods and apparatuses used to produce uniform poultry fillets for further processing or consumption.
[A22C 21/0038] Trussing poultry -- The CPC subclass A22C 21/0038 covers trussing poultry, i.e., binding the carcass of a bird with string, twine or a device to keep its shape during cooking, cooling or storage. This
[A22C 21/0046] Support devices -- A22C 21/0046 concerns support devices used when processing poultry, including racks, hooks, frames or other apparatus that hold, suspend or stabilize birds during slaughter, evisceration, cutting or other preparation steps. These tools facilitate safe, efficient handling and positioning of the poultry throughout the butchering process.
[A22C 21/0053] Transferring or conveying devices for poultry -- A22C 21/0053 covers devices that move or convey poultry within processing operations, such as conveyors, elevators, transfer tables and other mechanical systems that transport birds between stages of slaughter, e-visceration, chilling or packaging. These devices are designed to handle live or carcass poultry efficiently while maintaining hygiene and minimizing damage.
[A22C 21/0061] Cleaning or disinfecting poultry -- The CPC subclass A22C 21/0061 covers methods and apparatus for cleaning or disinfecting poultry during processing, including washing, spraying, immersion or other treatments that use chemical or physical agents to reduce microbial contamination before or after slaughter. It addresses hygiene steps that are part of poultry preparation within the broader butchering and meat-processing categories.
[A22C 21/0069] Deboning poultry or parts of poultry -- A22C 21/0069 covers techniques and equipment for deboning poultry or specific poultry parts, including the removal of bones from meat, wings or other sections to facilitate further processing or consumption.
[A22C 21/0076] Deboning poultry legs and drumsticks -- This CPC subclass covers methods and apparatus for removing the bone from poultry legs and drumsticks, including techniques for separating meat from the femur and tibia while preserving meat integrity. It is used to classify inventions related to deboning processes in poultry processing operations.
[A22C 21/0084] Deboning poultry wings -- A22C 21/0084 covers techniques and apparatus for deboning poultry wings, describing methods to separate bone from meat and the tools designed for that purpose in the processing of poultry within the butchering sector.
[A22C 21/0092] Skinning poultry or parts of poultry -- A22C 21/0092 covers methods for removing the skin from whole poultry or individual parts, usually performed before further processing such as cutting, cooking or packaging. It falls under the Cooperative Patent Classification for human necessities, specifically the butchering and processing meat, poultry, or fish subclass.
[A22C 21/02] Plucking mechanisms for poultry -- This CPC subclass A22C21/02 covers devices and systems used to remove feathers from poultry, including mechanical, pneumatic or combined plucking mechanisms integrated into processing lines. It falls under processing poultry within meat and fish processing.
[A22C 21/022] with fingers -- A22C 21/022 covers methods of processing poultry where the operator uses fingers to handle or separate parts, such as manual de-boning, skinning, or portioning. This
[A22C 21/024] Hand tools for plucking poultry -- A22C 21/024 covers hand-operated tools for removing feathers from poultry during processing, including manual pluckers, comb-type devices and plucking boards that rely on human effort rather than powered mechanisms.
[A22C 21/026] with rollers -- A22C 21/026 covers apparatus and methods for processing poultry that employ rollers as a principal element. It includes devices where rollers are used to transport, flatten, shape, or separate poultry parts during cutting, deboning, or further preparation. The classification is part of the CPC hierarchy for meat, poultry and fish processing under the broader human necessities and butchering categories.
[A22C 21/028] with discs -- A22C 21/028 covers poultry-processing apparatus that utilizes rotating or stationary discs to separate, trim or shape meat parts. The disc element may be employed for deboning, filleting or removing skin, often in conjunction with conveyors or other handling equipment. This sub-class falls under processing meat, poultry, or fish, specifically under the "Processing poultry" group.
[A22C 21/04] Scalding, singeing, waxing, or dewaxing poultry -- A22C 21/04 covers methods applied to poultry after slaughter, such as scalding, singeing, waxing or dewaxing. These treatments prepare the bird for subsequent steps like plucking, chilling or packaging.
[A22C 21/06] Eviscerating devices for poultry -- This subclass covers machines and tools designed to remove internal organs from poultry during processing, including automated and hand-operated eviscerators and related components used on slaughter lines.
[A22C 21/063] Hand tools for eviscerating poultry -- A22C 21/063 covers hand-operated devices used to remove internal organs from poultry during processing. The tools are designed for manual evisceration, assisting butchers in extracting entrails efficiently while maintaining meat quality. This subclass falls under the broader categories of human necessities, butchering, and processing meat, poultry, or fish.
[A22C 21/066] Extracting or removing the tendons from the legs of poultry -- The A22C 21/066 class covers methods for extracting or removing tendons from poultry legs. It pertains to the processing stage where the tendons are separated from the meat to improve edibility or facilitate further preparation. This step is part of poultry processing within the broader butchering and meat-processing hierarchy.
[A22C 25/00] Processing fish, including shellfish -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All types of machinery and operation performed specifically on fish, also processing cephalopods (octopus, squid, cuttlefish...):  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cutting or shaping meat -> A22C17/0006 - Slaughtering fish -> A22B3/083 - Fish powder, agglomerates or flakes -> A23L17/10 - Investigating fish -> G01N33/12  **Glossary:** - Roe: the mass of eggs contained in the ovaries of a female fish or shellfish, especially when ripe and used as food

These tools are classified under Human Necessities → Butchering → Processing meat, poultry or fish → Processing fish, including shellfish.
[A22C 25/04] Sorting fish -- A22C 25/04 covers methods and apparatus for sorting fish, including separating species, sizes or quality grades during processing. It is situated in the processing fish and shellfish subgroup of the butchering division under human necessities.

The classification applies to tables equipped with features such as drainage, refrigeration, adjustable height or surface treatments that facilitate hygienic and efficient fish processing. It falls under Human Necessities → Butchering → Processing meat, poultry, or fish → Processing fish, including shellfish.
[A22C 25/08] Holding, guiding, or conveying fish before, during or after its preparation -- A22C 25/08 covers devices that hold, guide or transport fish-fresh, frozen or shelled-through any stage of its preparation, from initial handling to final processing. [A22C 25/10] Devices for threading fish on strings or the like -- A22C 25/10 covers apparatus for threading fish onto cords, strings or similar supports during processing operations such as hanging, drying or smoking. The devices include mechanisms that automate or aid the insertion of fish onto lines, facilitating handling of whole fish or shellfish in butchering and preparation.
[A22C 25/12] Arranging fish, e.g. according to the position of head and tail -- A22C 25/12 covers methods and apparatus for arranging fish after capture or processing, such as aligning them by head-to-tail orientation to facilitate further handling, packaging or storage. [A22C 25/14] Beheading, eviscerating, or cleaning fish -- A22C 25/14 covers the removal of heads, internal organs and other parts of fish during processing, including cleaning and eviscerating steps for whole fish or fillets. It applies to methods used in the butchering stage of fish and shellfish preparation.
[A22C 25/142] Beheading fish -- A22C 25/142 - Beheading fish refers to inventions for removing the head of fish during processing, typically as an early step before filleting or cleaning. [A22C 25/145] Eviscerating fish -- A22C 25/145 covers methods and apparatus for eviscerating fish, that is removing the internal organs during fish processing. [A22C 25/147] Eviscerating by means of vacuum or suction devices -- A22C 25/147 covers the evisceration of fish and shellfish by using vacuum or suction devices, describing techniques that remove internal organs through negative pressure rather than manual cutting. The entry falls under human necessities, butchering, and processing of meat, poultry, or fish.
[A22C 25/16] Removing fish-bones -- A22C 25/16 covers methods for removing bones from fish and shellfish during processing, including mechanical, chemical or pneumatic techniques that improve edibility and appearance. It is part of the hierarchy under human necessities, butchering, processing meat, poultry or fish, specifically processing fish.
[A22C 25/163] Removing the fins -- A22C 25/163 covers methods and equipment for removing the fins of fish during processing, including cutting, trimming or separating fins to prepare fillets or whole fish for consumption. [A22C 25/166] Removing loose pin bones, e.g. from fish fillets -- A22C 25/166 covers methods and apparatus for extracting loose pin bones from fish fillets, including techniques for locating and removing small skeletal elements in processed fish or shellfish

This sub-class deals with techniques, equipment and methods specifically designed to separate the skin without damaging the underlying flesh. It is part of the broader category for processing meat, poultry or fish within the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[A22C 25/18] Cutting fish into portions -- A22C 25/18 covers the step of cutting fish into portions after it has been processed. It includes methods and apparatus for dividing whole fish or fillets into serving-size pieces, applicable to both fresh and frozen fish, including shellfish.

It falls under processing fish in the CPC hierarchy.

[A22C 25/22] Fish-rolling apparatus -- A22C 25/22 covers devices used to roll whole fish or fillets during processing, typically to shape or prepare them for further handling such as stuffing, portioning or packaging. The apparatus may include rollers, belts or rotating drums that hold the fish while it is turned, ensuring uniform curvature and minimizing damage. This technology is part of the broader category of fish-rolling equipment within meat, poultry and fish processing.

[A22C 29/005] Grading or classifying shellfish or bivalves -- A22C 29/005 covers methods and apparatus for grading or classifying shellfish and bivalves, such as oysters, mussels, clams or lobsters, based on size, weight, species, quality or other physical characteristics, typically for sorting them for market or processing use.
[A22C 29/02] Processing shrimps, lobsters or the like -- A22C 29/02 covers methods and apparatus for processing shellfish such as shrimp, lobster and similar crustaceans, including cleaning, peeling, cooking, freezing or packaging. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Human Necessities → Butchering → Processing meat, poultry or fish → Processing shellfish. This subclass is used to classify inventions that improve the handling and preparation of these seafood products.

It falls within the broader categories for processing meat, poultry or fish under the Human Necessities section.
[A22C 29/022] Deveining shellfish -- A22C 29/022 covers the removal of veins and other internal tissues from shellfish such as oysters and lobsters during processing. It falls under the broader category of butchering and the specific sub-group for processing meat, poultry, fish, and shellfish.
[A22C 29/023] Conveying, feeding or aligning shellfish -- A22C 29/023 covers apparatus and methods for conveying, feeding or aligning shellfish such as oysters and lobsters during processing. It addresses the handling steps that move or position shellfish on production lines to aid cleaning, trimming or further preparation.
[A22C 29/024] Opening, shelling or peeling shellfish -- A22C 29/024 covers methods and apparatus for opening, shelling or peeling shellfish such as oysters, lobsters and similar marine products. It falls within the human necessities group, under butchering and the processing of meat, poultry, fish and specifically the processing of shellfish. This subclass deals with techniques that separate the edible flesh from the shell.
[A22C 29/025] Mechanically opening and shelling crabs, lobsters or other hard-shelled crustaceans -- The entry covers machines and methods that automatically crack, open and remove the shells of hard-shelled crustaceans such as crabs, lobsters, oysters or other shellfish for meat extraction. It includes apparatuses that feed, split and separate the shell from the edible portion in an industrial processing line.
[A22C 29/026] Mechanically peeling and shelling shrimps, prawns or other soft-shelled crustaceans -- The code A22C 29/026 covers mechanical methods for peeling and shelling shrimp, prawns and other soft-shelled crustaceans, such as oysters or lobsters, in an automated or semi-automated process. It is located in the Cooperative Patent Classification hierarchy Human Necessities → Butchering → Processing meat, poultry or fish → Processing shellfish.
[A22C 29/027] Hand tools for shelling shellfish -- A22C 29/027 covers hand-operated implements specifically shaped to open or extract the edible portions of shellfish such as oysters, lobsters and similar species. These tools are used in the processing stage of shellfish preparation within the broader butchering and food-handling categories.
[A22C 29/028] Beheading shellfish -- A22C 29/028 relates to processes and equipment for beheading shellfish such as oysters, lobsters and similar species during processing. It covers methods of removing the head to facilitate cleaning, handling or preparation for further culinary treatment.
[A22C 29/04] Processing bivalves, e.g. oysters -- The subclass A22C 29/04 covers methods and apparatus for processing bivalve mollusks such as oysters, clams, mussels or lobsters, including cleaning, shucking, de-shelling and preparing them for consumption or further treatment. It is situated under the broader groups of human necessities, butchering and processing meat, poultry or fish.
[A22C 29/043] Cleaning operations on bivalves, e.g. evisceration, brushing, separation of meat and shell material -- A22C 29/043 pertains to the cleaning processes for bivalves such as oysters and clams, including specific operations like evisceration, brushing, and separating the meat from the shell. This classification is part of a broader category that deals with the processing of meat, poultry, or fish under human necessities.
[A22C 29/046] Opening or shucking bivalves -- A22C 29/046 covers methods and apparatus for opening or shucking bivalve molluscs such as oysters, clams, and mussels. The entry details steps to separate the shell halves and extract the edible meat, including tools, mechanical actions and handling techniques used in shellfish processing.
[A22C 5/00] Processing meat -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Meat mixers meant for obtaining a meat mixture for its further processing
[A22C 7/00] Apparatus for pounding, forming, or pressing meat, sausage-meat, or meat products -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatuses for pressing and moulding meat.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Ham boilers -> A47J27/20 - Meat patty moulding devices for domestic use -> A47J43/20
[A22C 7/0007] specially adapted for making multi-layered meat products -- The A22C 7/0007 subclass covers devices that pound, shape or compress meat, sausage-meat or other meat products and are specifically designed to produce multi-layered meat items, such as stacked or layered meat blocks, hams or loaves. These machines typically include multiple forming sections or adjustable presses that create distinct layers within a single product.
[A22C 7/0015] specially adapted for making meat-balls -- A22C 7/0015 covers machines designed specifically for forming meat-balls, including devices that shape, compress or bind minced meat into spherical portions. The apparatus may incorporate molds, rollers or press mechanisms to produce uniform meat-ball sizes efficiently during meat processing.
[A22C 7/0023] Pressing means -- A22C 7/0023 covers pressing means, i.e. devices that apply pressure to meat, sausage-meat or similar products to shape, compact or tenderise them. The classification applies to mechanisms such as pistons, rollers or plates integrated in equipment for butchering
[A22C 7/003] Meat-moulds -- Meat-moulds are devices used in butchering to shape, form or press meat, sausage-meat and other meat products into specific cross-sections or patterns, often before cooking or curing. The apparatus may include cavities, pistons or pressing plates that impart a uniform shape and density, facilitating portion control and product consistency. This classification covers tools that pound, form or press meat into moulded forms.
[A22C 7/0038] Demoulding means -- A22C 7/0038 covers demoulding means, devices that release shaped meat, sausage or fish products from molds or forming cavities in processing equipment. These mechanisms may be mechanical, pneumatic or hydraulic aids that push, lift or invert the product to facilitate removal without damage, and are used in apparatus for pounding, forming, or pressing meat.
[A22C 7/0046] Containers in which meat is pressed and moulded -- A22C 7/0046 pertains to specialized containers designed for the process of pressing and molding meat. These containers are integral in shaping meat products during processing, ensuring uniformity and proper form.
[A22C 7/0053] Stackable containers -- Stackable containers are food-grade vessels used in meat-pounding or forming equipment to hold, transport, and temporarily store meat, sausage-meat or fish pieces during processing. Their design allows multiple units to be stacked securely, optimizing space and facilitating cleaning and handling in industrial butchering lines.
[A22C 7/0061] Containers for moulding and pressing ham -- A22C 7/0061 covers containers designed for moulding and pressing ham during meat processing operations. The class belongs to apparatus for pounding, forming, or pressing meat, sausage-meat, or meat products within the butchering subgroup of human necessities.
[A22C 7/0069] Pressing and moulding by means of a drum -- The A22C 7/0069 class covers apparatus that shape and compress meat, sausage-meat, or other meat products using a rotating drum. It includes drums that press, mould or pound the product to achieve a uniform texture or form.
[A22C 7/0076] Devices for making meat patties -- This CPC subgroup covers apparatus specifically designed to shape ground meat into individual patties, such as burger or cutlet moulds, using pressing, forming or pounding mechanisms. It is classified under Human Necessities → Butchering → Processing meat, poultry or fish, focusing on devices that produce meat patties.
[A22C 7/0084] comprising a reciprocating plate -- This subclass covers equipment for processing meat, poultry or fish that uses a plate moving back-and-forth to pound, shape or compress the product. The reciprocating plate contacts the meat while a stationary support holds it, providing consistent forming or tenderising for items such as sausage-meat, patties and other shaped meat products.
[A22C 7/0092] with worms or other rotary mounted members  A22C7/0007  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass covers machines that pound or shape meat, sausage-meat or similar products using worm gears or other rotary-mounted components. It falls under Human Necessities → Butchering → Processing meat, poultry, or fish → Apparatus for pounding, forming, or pressing meat. The code is A22C 7/0092 and it is subordinate to A22C 7
[A22C 9/00] Apparatus for tenderising meat, e.g. ham -- **Definition:** This place covers:  For treating, tenderising or flavouring meat.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Processing meat by injection -> A22C17/0053 - Tenderising chemically -> A23L13/00 - Tenderised or flavoured meat pieces e.g. by injecting -> A23L13/70
[A22C 9/001] by injection -- A22C 9/001 covers apparatus that tenderise meat by injecting fluids such as brine, enzymes or other tenderising solutions into the product. The devices inject the liquid under pressure to improve texture and juiciness, typically for ham, poultry or fish. This subclass falls within the broader group of meat-processing equipment.
[A22C 9/002] by electric treatment -- The CPC subclass A22C 9/002 covers apparatus for tenderising meat, such as ham, that use an electric treatment to soften the product. It is situated within the hierarchy of human necessities → butchering → processing meat, poultry or fish.
[A22C 9/004] by massaging -- This subclass covers apparatus that tenderises meat, poultry or fish by massaging, using rollers, kneading or similar mechanisms to improve texture during processing.
[A22C 9/005] Tumblers and rotating drums for massaging meat in their interior -- A22C 9/005 covers tumblers and rotating drums designed to massage meat inside the container during processing. These devices rotate or tumble the product to improve tenderness and uniformity, typically used in industrial ham or poultry processing lines.
[A22C 9/007] by beating -- A22C 9/007 refers to machines that tenderise meat by beating, such as mechanical hammers or mallets used in butchering to soften muscle fibres of meat, poultry or fish. These devices apply repeated impacts to improve texture before cooking or further processing.
[A22C 9/008] by piercing -- A22C 9/008 covers meat-tenderising apparatus that works by piercing the product with needles, pins or similar elements to disrupt muscle fibers. The devices are typically used for ham or other meats to improve texture and enable better absorption of brine, seasoning or curing agents.
[A23] FOODS OR FOODSTUFFS -- Attention is drawn to the following places: C08B Polysaccharides, derivatives thereof C11 Animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes C12 Biochemistry, beer, spirits, wine, vinegar C13 Sugar industry
[A23B] PRESERVATION OF FOODS, FOODSTUFFS OR NON-ALCOHOLIC BEVERAGES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes or apparatus for the preservation of foods or foodstuffs in general, e.g. by pasteurising, sterilising, heating, freezing, drying, pressurising, irradiation or treatment with chemicals, specially adapted for foods or foodstuffs and where the nature of the product is not relevant or specified.  Processes for preserving meat, sausages, fish, fish products, eggs, egg products, fruit, vegetables (preservation of raw potatoes), edible seeds, milk, dairy products, edible oils or fats, flour, dough before baking, finished bakery products, non-alcoholic beverages, beverages from legumes, cocoa or cocoa products, tea, tea substitutes, herbal tea, tea containing beverages, coffee, coffee substitutes or coffee containing beverages by heating, drying, freezing, irradiating, smoking, using chemicals or microorganisms and compositions therefor.  Apparatus for preserving meat, sausages, fish, fish products, eggs, egg products, fruit, vegetables or edible seeds using liquids, solids or gases.  Apparatus, processes and compositions for coating meat, sausages, fish, fish products, eggs, egg products, fruit, vegetables or edible seeds with a protective layer.  Apparatus for compressing sauerkraut.  Chemical ripening of fruit or vegetables with liquids, solids or gases.  Preserved, ripened or canned products (i.e. meat, sausages, fish, fish products, eggs, egg products, fruit, vegetables, edible seed, milk, dairy products, edible oils or fats, flour, dough before baking, finished bakery products, non-alcoholic beverages, beverages from legumes, cocoa or cocoa products, tea, tea substitutes, herbal tea, tea containing beverages, coffee, coffee substitutes or coffee containing beverages), per se.  **Glossary:** - coffee: stimulant product or beverage made from roasted seeds that are commonly called coffee beans and come from a plant of the genus Coffea, e.g. Coffea arabica or Coffea canephora - coffee substitutes: coffee substitutes (solid or liquid) are non-coffee products normally without caffeine, used to substitute coffee while preserving its taste. Grain coffee and other substitutes can be made by roasting or decocting various organic substances. Common ingredients include acorn, barley and malt, beech nut, chicory root, corn, fig or rye. - edible oil: edible or cooking oil that is a fat of plant, animal or microbial origin, is liquid at room temperature and is suitable for food use. Some of the many different kinds of edible vegetable oils include: olive oil, palm oil, soybean oil, canola oil, pumpkin seed oil, corn oil, sunflower oil, safflower oil, peanut oil, grape seed oil, sesame oil, argan oil and rice bran oil. Many other types of vegetable oil are also used in cooking. The generic expression "vegetable oil", when used to characterise an oil-based cooking product, refers to a blend of different oils, often based on palm, corn, soy or sunflower oils. Fish oil is an example of an edible oil of animal origin. Microbial oils are also included in this category. - egg product: all or a portion of the contents found inside eggs that have been separated from the shell. They may be pasteurised in a food processing plant, with or without added ingredients and are intended for human consumption, such as dried, frozen or liquid eggs. Egg products do not include food that contains eggs only in a relatively small proportion, such as cake mixes. - fat: unctuous substance of little consistency that melts easily, known in chemistry as body fat and present in various parts of the human or animal body. Fats consist of a wide group of compounds that are generally soluble in organic solvents and largely insoluble in water. Chemically, fats are generally triesters of glycerol and fatty acids. Fats may be either solid or liquid at normal room temperature, depending on their structure and composition. Although the terms "oils", "fats" and "lipids" are all used to refer to fats, "oils" is usually used to refer to fats that are liquids at normal room temperature, while "fats" is usually used to refer to fats that are solids at normal room temperature. The term "lipids" is used to refer to both liquid and solid fats, along with other related substances. Fats form a category of lipids that are distinguished from other lipids by their chemical structure and physical properties. This category of molecules is important for many forms of life, serving both structural and metabolic functions. They are an important part of the diet of most heterotrophs (including humans). Fats or lipids are broken down in the body by enzymes called lipases produced in the pancreas. - fish: any fish, including shellfish, crustaceans and marine animals (marine being defined as "of, found in or produced by the sea"), and any parts, products or by-products thereof - fish product: product containing a predominant amount of fish - oil: substance that is in a viscous liquid state ("oily") at ambient temperatures or slightly warmer and is both hydrophobic (immiscible with water) and lipophilic (miscible with other oils). This general definition includes compound classes with otherwise unrelated chemical structures, properties and uses, including vegetable oils, petrochemical oils and volatile essential oils. Oil is a nonpolar substance. The term "oil" is used for any substance that does not mix with water and has a greasy feel, such as petroleum (or crude oil) and heating oil, regardless of its chemical structure. - herbal tea: any Camellia sinensis-free product or beverage prepared by hot water infusion of plant material, usually leaves but also including fruits, flowers and possibly even bark or other parts. Herbal teas are belonging to the group of tea substitutes. - tea: stimulant product or beverage made from the leaves of the traditional tea plant (Camellia sinensis) - tea substitutes: tea substitutes (solid or liquid) are non-tea products, i.e. not coming from the traditional tea plant (Camellia sinensis, e.g. mate (Ilex paraguariensis), lemon balm (Melissa officinalis) or rose hips (Rosa canina); cf. "herbal tea"

[A23B 11/10] Preservation of milk or milk preparations -- Group A23B11/10 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group A23C9/00 . Groups A23C9/00 and A23B11/10 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[A23B 11/102] Storing or packaging in a vacuum or in inert or sterile gaseous atmosphere  impregnation with inert gases  A23C9/1524 -- The A23B 11/102 classification covers methods for preserving milk or dairy products by storing or packaging them under vacuum, in an inert or sterile gaseous atmosphere, or by impregnating the product with inert gases, ensuring extended shelf-life and protection against microbial spoilage.
[A23B 11/12] by heating -- A23B 11/12 covers methods for preserving milk and other dairy products by heating, such as pasteurisation or sterilisation, to extend shelf life and ensure microbial safety. It lies within the CPC hierarchy for food preservation under human necessities.
[A23B 11/123] in packages -- A23B 11/123 concerns the preservation of milk or other dairy products by means of packaging, covering containers, sealing methods and packaging materials designed to extend shelf life and protect the product during storage and distribution.
[A23B 11/127] progressively transported through the apparatus -- The code A23B 11/127 covers apparatuses for preserving milk or dairy products in which the material is moved progressively through successive zones or stages within the machine, allowing each step-such as heating, cooling, homogenising, or adding preservatives-to be performed in sequence as the product travels through the equipment. This staged transport improves control over processing conditions and product quality.
[A23B 11/13] the materials being loose unpacked -- A23B 11/13 relates to the loose, unpacked materials employed in preserving milk and other dairy products, covering packaging, containers, and ancillary substances that are not pre-formed or sealed. The entry addresses methods and compositions for handling these bulk materials during storage or transport to maintain product quality.
[A23B 11/1303] Apparatus through which the material is transported non progressively; Temperature-maintaining holding tanks or vats with discontinuous filling or discharge -- The code A23B 11/1303 covers equipment that transports milk or other dairy products in a non-progressive manner, typically involving temperature-controlled holding tanks or vats that are filled or discharged in batches rather than continuously. Such apparatuses are used for preserving milk and dairy items during processing or storage.
[A23B 11/133] and progressively transported through the apparatus -- A23B 11/133 relates to apparatus for preserving milk or dairy products in which the product is moved progressively through the equipment, such as continuous-flow heating, cooling or other treatment stages. The subclass covers designs that transport the dairy material stepwise during the preservation process.
[A23B 11/1332] in contact with multiple heating plates -- This classification covers processes for preserving milk or dairy products using equipment where the product contacts several heating plates simultaneously, typically for pasteurisation or sterilisation. It falls under the CPC A23B 11/13 series dealing with heat treatment of dairy.
[A23B 11/1334] the milk being heated by electrical or mechanical means, e.g. by friction -- A23B 11/1334 pertains to processes and devices that heat milk or dairy products by applying electrical or mechanical energy, including friction-generated heat, as a means of preserving the food.
[A23B 11/1336] the milk flowing through with indirect heat exchange, containing rotating elements, e.g. for improving the heat exchange -- A23B 11/1336 covers apparatus in which milk or dairy products flow through a heat-exchange zone that uses indirect heating or cooling, incorporating rotating elements such as paddles or impellers to enhance turbulence and improve the efficiency of the heat transfer.
[A23B 11/137] in direct contact with the heating medium, e.g. steam -- A23B 11/137 covers processes or apparatus for preserving milk or dairy products where the product is placed in direct contact with the heating medium, such as steam, to achieve pasteurisation, sterilisation or other heat-treatment effects. It is a sub-class of preservation of foods, specifically milk.
[A23B 11/1375] by pulverisation of the milk, including free falling film -- A23B 11/1375 defines preservation methods for milk or dairy products that rely on pulverising the liquid, typically by atomising it or spreading it as a free-falling film before drying. This subclass is part of the broader category of food preservation that addresses powder-formation techniques for dairy.
[A23B 11/14] by freezing or cooling -- A23B 11/14 relates to methods and apparatus for preserving milk and other dairy products by freezing or cooling. It includes chilling, refrigeration and freezing techniques used to extend shelf life and maintain product quality. The entry falls under food preservation within the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[A23B 11/1403] Freezing in loose unpacked form -- A23B 11/1403 covers methods for preserving milk and other dairy products by freezing them when they are in a loose, unpackaged state, such as bulk or flowing forms. This subclass addresses the equipment, processes and conditions used to achieve rapid solidification without prior packaging.
[A23B 11/145] in packages -- A23B 11/145 relates to the preservation of milk or other dairy products when they are placed in packages, covering packaging materials, sealing techniques and storage conditions that maintain product quality and safety. It falls under the broader categories of human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, and preservation of foods and non-alcoholic beverages.
[A23B 11/1455] Freezing in packages -- A23B 11/1455 covers methods for freezing milk, cheese, yogurt and other dairy products while they remain in their original containers or packaging. It includes techniques that chill the product inside cartons, bottles, tubs or flexible packs, often using blast freezers, plate freezers or cryogenic gases to preserve texture, safety and shelf-life.
[A23B 11/16] by irradiation, e.g. by microwaves -- A23B 11/16 covers preservation of milk or other dairy products using irradiation techniques, notably microwave treatment, to extend shelf life, reduce microbial load, or achieve pasteurisation effects without conventional heat processing. This subclass falls under the broader category of food preservation methods applied to dairy.
[A23B 11/162] by sonic or ultrasonic waves -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus that use sonic or ultrasonic energy to preserve milk and other dairy products, inhibiting microbial growth or enzymatic activity to extend shelf life.
[A23B 11/164] by ultraviolet or infrared radiation -- A23B 11/164 covers methods that preserve milk or other dairy products using ultraviolet or infrared radiation. It falls under the Cooperative Patent Classification hierarchy for human necessities, specifically foods, food preservation, and the preservation of milk and dairy items. The code denotes non-thermal radiation techniques applied to extend shelf life.

[A23B 11/182] Inorganic compounds, e.g. lactoperoxidase - H 2 O 2  systems -- A23B 11/182 covers the use of inorganic compounds, particularly lactoperoxidase combined with hydrogen peroxide, to inhibit microbial growth and extend the shelf-life of milk and other dairy products. This sub-category falls under food preservation methods that employ chemical agents to maintain safety and quality.
