[A23B 4/00] Preservation of specific foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages -- A23B 4/00 covers techniques for preserving particular foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic drinks, including methods such as refrigeration, freezing, dehydration, vacuum packaging, and the use of preservatives tailored to the specific product. It falls within the broader CPC class for food preservation under Human Necessities.
[A23B 40/00] Preservation of flour or dough before baking -- Group A23B40/00 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups A21D6/00 , A21D6/006 and A21D6/008 . Group A23B40/00 is also impacted by reclassification into groups A23B40/10 , A23B40/30 , A23B40/50 and A23B40/60 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[A23B 4/002] Preservation in association with shaping  A23B4/0053 ,  A23B4/01 ,  A23B4/062  and  A23B4/066  take precedence -- The A23B 4/002 entry relates to techniques and devices that preserve foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages while they are being shaped, for example during molding, forming, extrusion or similar processes. It defines the scope for preservation actions that occur concurrently with shaping operations and supersedes the more specific sub-groups A23B 4/0053, 4/01, 4/062 and 4/066.
[A23B 4/005] Preserving by heating -- A23B 4/005 covers methods that preserve foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages by applying heat, including blanching, pasteurisation, sterilisation and other thermal treatments designed to extend shelf-life or maintain quality.
[A23B 4/0053] with gas or liquids, with or without shaping, e.g. in form of powder, granules or flakes -- A23B 4/0053 covers methods and apparatus for preserving foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages by treating them with gases or liquids, optionally shaping the material into forms such as powders, granules or flakes. This subclass falls under the broader category of food preservation within the human necessities sector.
[A23B 4/0056] with packages, or with shaping in the form of blocks or portions -- A23B 4/0056 relates to preserving foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic drinks by using packages or by shaping the product into blocks or portions, enabling extended shelf life and convenient handling. This subclass falls under the broader category of food preservation within the Human Necessities section.
[A23B 4/01] by irradiation or electric treatment -- A23B 4/01 covers methods for preserving foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic drinks using ionising radiation, microwave, radio-frequency, or other electric treatments that inactivate microorganisms or retard spoilage, without adding chemical preservatives. This subclass falls under the broader categories of human necessities, foods, and food preservation.
[A23B 40/10] by cooling -- Group A23B40/10 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups A23B40/00 , A21D6/00 and A21D6/001 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[A23B 4/012] with packages, or with shaping in the form of blocks or portions -- The CPC subclass A23B 4/012 covers preservation techniques for specific foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic drinks in which the product is packaged or shaped into blocks or portioned units, facilitating handling, storage or distribution.
[A23B 4/015] Preserving by irradiation or electric treatment without heating effect -- This subclass covers food preservation methods that use ionising radiation or non-thermal electric treatments, such as gamma rays, electron beams or pulsed electric fields, to inhibit spoilage without applying heat.
[A23B 4/02] Preserving by means of inorganic salts -- A23B 4/02 covers methods for extending the shelf life of foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic drinks by applying inorganic salts such as sodium chloride, potassium nitrate or calcium chloride. The techniques may involve soaking, sprinkling, coating or incorporating the salts to inhibit microbial growth, reduce moisture loss or control pH, thereby preserving the product's quality during storage.
[A23B 4/021] with apparatus adapted for gaseous preserving agents -- A23B 4/021 covers methods and devices for preserving foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages using gaseous preserving agents, such as carbon dioxide or nitrogen, with apparatus specially adapted to apply these gases.
[A23B 4/023] by kitchen salt or mixtures thereof with inorganic or organic compounds -- A23B 4/023 covers methods for preserving foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages by applying kitchen salt or blends of salt with inorganic or organic compounds, such as curing, brining or salt-based pickling. The entry focuses on the chemical action of sodium chloride and its mixtures to inhibit spoilage and extend shelf life.
[A23B 4/0235] with organic compounds or biochemical products -- A23B 4/0235 covers methods and compositions for preserving foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages that employ organic compounds or biochemical products such as antioxidants, enzymes, bacteriocins or other naturally derived additives to extend shelf life and maintain quality.
[A23B 4/027] by inorganic salts other than kitchen salt or mixtures thereof with organic compounds, e.g. biochemical compounds -- A23B 4/027 covers methods for preserving foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages that use inorganic salts other than common kitchen salt, alone or mixed with organic or biochemical compounds. These treatments aim to inhibit microbial growth, control acidity or extend shelf life without relying on traditional sodium chloride.
[A23B 4/03] Drying -- A23B 4/03 concerns drying techniques applied to preserve foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages by reducing their moisture content. It includes methods such as air, heat, freeze or vacuum drying that extend product shelf life.
[A23B 40/30] by heating -- Group A23B40/30 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups A23B40/00 , A21D6/00 and A21D6/003 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[A23B 4/031] Apparatus for drying  A23B4/037  takes precedence -- A23B 4/031 covers devices used to remove moisture from food, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic drinks, such as dryers, dehydrators and related equipment. It falls
[A23B 4/033] with addition of chemicals -- A23B 4/033 relates to preserving particular foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages by adding chemical agents. It covers the use of preservatives, antioxidants, antimicrobials or other additives to extend shelf life and maintain quality.
[A23B 4/037] Freeze-drying -- The CPC subclass A23B 4/037 covers freeze-drying, a preservation technique that removes moisture from foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages by sublimating frozen water under reduced pressure, thereby extending shelf life. It belongs to the hierarchy of food preservation under Human Necessities.
[A23B 4/044] Smoking -- A23B 4/044 covers the use of smoking as a preservation method for foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages, where exposure to smoke from wood, plant material or other substances extends shelf life, imparts flavor and inhibits microbial growth.
[A23B 4/048] with addition of chemicals other than natural smoke -- This CPC subclass covers methods or processes for preserving foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic drinks by adding chemical agents other than natural smoke. It falls under the broader categories of human necessities, foods and preservation of foods. The code is A
[A23B 40/50] by irradiation -- Group A23B40/50 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups A23B40/00 , A21D6/00 and A21D6/005 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[A23B 4/052] Smoke generators -- A23B 4/052 pertains to smoke generators employed in the preservation of foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages, covering devices that produce smoke to inhibit microbial growth, oxidation or spoilage. These systems are used to extend shelf life by exposing products to smoke or smoke-derived compounds.
[A23B 4/0523] Smoke generators using wood-pyrolysis or wood-friction -- A23B 4/0523 covers smoke generators that produce smoke by pyrolyzing wood or by wood friction for the preservation of foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages. The devices may include chambers, burners or mechanisms that create wood-derived smoke for smoking processes.
[A23B 4/0526] Smoke generators or smoking apparatus using liquid smoke in gaseous or liquid form -- The entry A23B 4/0526 covers devices that create smoke or apply liquid smoke, in either gaseous or liquid form, for the preservation, flavoring or visual treatment of foodstuffs and non-alcoholic beverages. It includes smoke generators, smoking chambers and related apparatus that utilize liquid smoke as the source of the smoking medium. These systems are intended to extend shelf life or enhance sensory properties of the product.
[A23B 4/056] Smoking combined with irradiation or electric treatment, e.g. electrostatic smoking -- A23B 4/056 covers methods of smoking food that are combined with irradiation or electric treatments such as electrostatic smoking, used to preserve foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages.
[A23B 4/06] Freezing -- The CPC subclass A23B 4/06 falls under the hierarchy HUMAN NECESSITIES → FOODS OR FOODSTUFFS → PRESERVATION OF FOODS, FOODSTUFFS OR NON-ALCOHOLIC BEVERAGES and deals specifically with freezing as a method for preserving particular foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic drinks.

This classification is part of the broader category dealing with food preservation techniques under human necessities and foodstuffs.
[A23B 4/064] with packages or with shaping in the form of blocks or portions -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus for preserving foods or non-alcoholic beverages when the product is packaged or shaped into blocks or individual portions, such as pre-shaped cheese blocks or packaged meat portions. It belongs to CPC A23B, the group for preservation of foodstuffs.
[A23B 4/066] the materials not being transported through or in the apparatus with or without shaping, e.g. in the form of powder, granules or flakes -- A23B 4/066 relates to the handling of solid preservation agents that remain outside the food-processing equipment, such as powders, granules or flakes, used to keep foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic drinks fresh. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for human necessities, foods, and preservation of foods.
[A23B 4/068] with packages or with shaping in the form of blocks or portions -- A23B 4/068 relates to preserving foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic drinks by incorporating them into packages or by shaping them into blocks or portioned forms, covering techniques that combine preservation with packaging or block-type molding to facilitate storage and handling.
[A23B 4/07] Thawing subsequent to freezing -- A23B 4/07 covers methods, devices and conditions for thawing foods or beverages that have previously been frozen, focusing on controlled defrosting to maintain texture, flavor and safety. It includes techniques for rapid or slow thawing, heating or moisture management, and equipment designed for domestic or industrial use.
[A23B 4/08] with addition of chemicals before or during cooling -- The subclass A23B 4/08 covers preservation techniques in which chemical additives are introduced to foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages before or during the cooling stage. It includes the use of salts, acids, antioxidants or other preservatives applied while the product is being chilled to extend its shelf life.
[A23B 4/09] with direct contact between the food and the chemical, e.g. liquid N -- This subclass deals with preservation techniques in which the food or beverage is placed in direct contact with a chemical agent, such as immersion in liquid nitrogen, to achieve rapid cooling or other protective effects.

[A23B 5/03] Freeze-drying -- A23B 5/03 covers the freeze-drying of eggs and egg products as a method of preserving them. It entails sublimating moisture from frozen egg material under low pressure to produce a stable, lightweight product that retains nutritional and functional properties while extending shelf life.
[A23B 5/035] Spray-drying -- A23B 5/035 covers the use of spray-drying to preserve eggs or egg products, a method that rapidly removes moisture by atomising liquid feed into a hot air stream, producing a stable powder. The technique rapidly freezes the material, extending shelf life while maintaining texture and nutritional quality.

[A23B 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- A23B 99/00 covers any aspect of preserving foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages that does not fit into the specific groups defined elsewhere in the A23B subclass, serving as a catch-all for unassigned preservation techniques, materials or processes.
[A23C] DAIRY PRODUCTS, e.g. MILK, BUTTER OR CHEESE -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preservation of milk or dairy products -> A23B11/00
[A23C 1/00] General dairy technology -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for concentration, evaporation or drying.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Milk preparations; Milk powder or milk powder preparations -> A23C9/00
[A23C 1/01] Drying in thin layers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Drying in thin layers.
[A23C 1/03] on drums or rollers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Drying in thin layers on drums or rollers.
[A23C 1/04] by spraying into a gas stream -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for concentration, evaporation or drying, by spraying into a gas stream (e.g. spray-drying).
[A23C 1/045] by simultaneously or separately spraying of two different liquid products other than water or steam; by separate addition of additives other than milk products or lactose in the spray-dryer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for concentration, evaporation or drying, by spraying into a gas stream (e.g. spray-drying), wherein two different liquid products other than water or steam are sprayed simultaneously or separately, or wherein additives other than milk products or lactose are added separately in the spray-dried.
[A23C 1/05] combined with agglomeration -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for concentration, evaporation or drying, by spraying into a gas stream (e.g. spray-drying), combined with agglomeration (granulation or coating).
[A23C 1/06] Concentration by freezing out the water -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Concentration by freezing out (and separating) the water.
[A23C 1/08] Freeze-drying -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Concentration by freeze-drying.
[A23C 1/10] Foam drying -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Foam drying.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Spray-drying -> A23C1/04 - Freeze-drying -> A23C1/08
[A23C 11/00] Milk substitutes, e.g. coffee whitener compositions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Milk substitute products, e.g. coffee whitener and process for their preparation.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cheese substitutes -> A23C20/00 - Preservation of milk substitutes -> A23B11/20 - Butter substitutes -> A23D
[A23C 11/02] containing at least one non-milk component as source of fats or proteins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Milk substitute products, e.g. coffee whiteners, containing at least one non-milk component as source of fats or proteins, and methods for their preparation.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Use of non-milk fats or proteins for making cheese curd -> A23C19/055 - Use of non-milk fats or proteins as ingredients in whey preparations -> A23C21/04
[A23C 11/04] containing non-milk fats but no non-milk proteins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Milk substitute products, e.g. coffee whiteners, containing non-milk fats but no non-milk proteins, and method for their preparation.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Milk substitutes containing caseinates but no other milk proteins nor milk fats -> A23C11/08 - Milk substitutes containing or not lactose, but no milk components as sources of fats, carbohydrates or proteins -> A23C11/10
[A23C 11/045] obtained by mixing the non-fat components in powdered form with the fats or by dry mixing of the components -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Milk substitute products containing non-milk fats but no non-milk proteins, obtained by mixing the non-fat components in powdered form with the fats, or by dry mixing of the components, and methods for their preparation.
[A23C 11/06] containing non-milk proteins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Milk substitute products containing non-milk proteins, and methods for their preparation.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Milk substitutes containing caseinates but no other milk proteins nor milk fats -> A23C11/08 - Milk substitutes containing or not lactose, but no milk components as sources of fats, carbohydrates or proteins -> A23C11/10
[A23C 11/065] Microbial proteins, inactivated yeast or animal proteins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Milk substitute products containing microbial proteins, inactivated yeast or animal proteins as non-milk proteins, and methods for their preparation.
[A23C 11/08] containing caseinates but no other milk proteins nor milk fats -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Milk substitute products containing caseinates but no other milk proteins nor milk fats, and methods for their preparation.
[A23C 11/10] containing or not lactose but no other milk components as source of fats, carbohydrates or proteins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Milk substitutes containing lactose or not and no other milk components as sources of fats, carbohydrates or proteins, and methods for their preparation.
[A23C 11/103] containing only proteins from pulses, oilseeds or nuts, e.g. nut milk -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Milk substitutes containing lactose or not, but no other milk components as sources of fats, carbohydrates or proteins, which contain proteins only from pulses, oilseeds or nuts, e.g. almond milk, and methods for their preparation.
[A23C 11/106] Addition of, or treatment with, microorganisms  A23C20/025  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Milk substitutes containing lactose or not, but no other milk components as sources of fats, carbohydrates or proteins, which contain proteins only from pulses, oilseeds or nuts, which are treated with microorganisms or to which microorganisms are added, and methods for their preparation.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cheese substitutes containing mainly proteins from pulses or oilseeds -> A23C20/025
[A23C 1/12] Concentration by evaporation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Concentration by evaporation.
[A23C 1/14] combined with other treatment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for concentration, evaporation or drying combined with other treatment.
[A23C 1/16] using additives -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for concentration, evaporation or drying combined with other treatment, wherein additive are used.
[A23C 13/00] Cream -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preservation of cream or cream preparations -> A23B11/30 - Ice-cream -> A23G9/00
[A23C 13/12] Cream preparations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cream preparations (other than ice cream) and methods for their preparation.
[A23C 13/125] in powdered, granulated or solid form -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cream preparations (other than ice cream) in powdered, granulated or solid form, and methods for their preparation.
[A23C 13/14] containing milk products or milk components -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cream preparations (other than ice cream) containing milk products or non-fat milk components, and methods for their preparation.
[A23C 13/16] containing, or treated with, microorganisms, enzymes, or antibiotics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cream preparations (other than ice cream) containing, or treated with, microorganisms, enzymes or antibiotics. Sour cream and methods for the preparation thereof.
[A23C 13/165] Making sour cream by chemical or physical means only -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation of sour cream by chemical or physical means only.
[A23C 15/00] Butter -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preservation of butter or butter preparations -> A23B11/40 - Butter substitutes -> A23D
[A23C 15/02] Making thereof -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for making butter and butter preparations.
[A23C 15/04] from butter oil or anhydrous butter -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for making butter and butter preparations from butter oil or anhydrous butter.
[A23C 15/06] Treating cream prior to phase inversion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing butter and butter preparations, wherein cream (or milk) is treated prior to phase inversion.
[A23C 15/065] Addition of a treatment with microorganisms or enzymes; Addition of cultured milk products -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing butter and butter preparations, wherein, prior to phase inversion, cream (or milk) is treated with microorganisms or enzymes, or wherein, prior to phase inversion, microorganisms, enzymes or cultured milk products are added thereto.
[A23C 15/12] Butter preparations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Butter preparations, i.e. dairy type food products based on butter comprising butter as main ingredient, and method for their manufacture.
[A23C 15/123] Addition of microorganisms or cultured milk products; Addition of enzymes; Addition of starter cultures other than destillates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for making butter preparations (i.e. dairy type food products based on butter comprising butter as main ingredient) wherein microorganisms, cultured milk products, enzymes or starter cultures other than distillates are added, and butter preparations obtained thereby.
[A23C 15/126] Butter containing a minority of vegetable oils; Enrichment of butter with fatty acids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for making butter preparations (i.e. dairy type food products based on butter comprising butter as main ingredient) containing a minority of vegetable oils, and enrichment of butter with fatty acids, as well as butter preparations obtained thereby.
[A23C 15/14] Butter powder -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Butter powder, butter oil (i.e. melted butter, e.g. ghee), anhydrous butter, and methods for the preparation thereof.
[A23C 15/145] Removal of steroids, e.g. cholesterol or free acids; Fractionation of anhydrous milkfat by extraction with solvents other than solvent crystallisation or with supercritical gases or by distillation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing butter powder, butter oil (i.e. melted butter, e.g. ghee) and anhydrous butter, wherein steroids (e.g. cholesterol) or free fatty acids are removed, or wherein anhydrous milkfat is fractionated by extraction with solvents other than solvent crystallisation, with supercritical gases, or by distillation, and butter powder, butter oil (i.e. melted butter, e.g. ghee) and anhydrous butter thus prepared.
[A23C 15/16] Butter having reduced fat content -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing butter having a reduced fat content and butter thus prepared.
[A23C 15/165] prepared by addition of microorganisms; Cultured spreads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Butter having a reduced fat content obtained by addition of microorganisms, including cultured spreads.  **Glossary:** - Cultured spread: This group only relates to butter based cultured spreads.
[A23C 17/00] Buttermilk -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Milk preparations, milk powder or milk powder preparations in which the chemical composition of the milk is modified by non-chemical treatment -> A23C9/14 - Preservation of buttermilk or buttermilk preparations -> A23B11/50
[A23C 17/02] containing, or treated with, microorganisms or enzymes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Buttermilk and buttermilk preparations containing, or treated with, microorganisms or enzymes, and methods for their preparation.
[A23C 19/00] Cheese -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cheese substitutes -> A23C20/00 - Preservation of cheese or cheese preparations -> A23B11/60
[A23C 19/02] Making cheese curd -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd (i.e. coagulated / clotted milk).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Apparatus for making cheese curd -> A01J25/00  **Glossary:** - Curd formation: Curdling, clotting, coagulation of milkCasein precipitation
[A23C 19/024] using continuous procedure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd using a continuous procedure.
[A23C 19/0245] with immobilized enzymes or microorganisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd using a continuous procedure combined with immobilised enzymes or microorganisms.
[A23C 19/028] without substantial whey separation from coagulated milk -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd wherein whey separation is completely avoided; processes wherein whey separation is avoided to a significant extent or where at least less whey is separated than in standard processes
[A23C 19/0285] by dialysis or ultrafiltration -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd without separation of substantial amounts of whey from the coagulated milk, wherein dialysis or ultrafiltration is applied.
[A23C 19/032] characterised by the use of specific microorganisms, or enzymes of microbial origin -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd wherein specific microorganisms, or enzymes of microbial origin are used.
[A23C 19/0321] Propionic acid bacteria -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd wherein propionic acid bacteria / Propionibacteria are used.
[A23C 19/0323] using only lactic acid bacteria, e.g. Pediococcus and Leuconostoc species; Bifidobacteria; Microbial starters in general  using moulds  A23C19/0682 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd wherein only lactic acid bacterial (including Pediococcus, Leuconostoc, and Bifidobacteria) are used.  Use of microbial starters in general for making cheese curd.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Use of moulds for cheesemaking -> A23C19/0682
[A23C 19/0325] using yeasts, alone or in combination with lactic acid bacteria or with fungi, without using other bacteria -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd wherein yeasts are used, either alone or in combination with lactic acid bacteria or with fungi, without using other (i.e. non-lactic acid) bacteria.
[A23C 19/0326] Rennet produced by fermentation, e.g. microbial rennet; Rennet produced by genetic engineering -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd wherein rennet produced by fermentation (e.g. microbial rennet) or by genetic engineering is used.
[A23C 19/0328] Enzymes other than milk clotting enzymes, e.g. lipase, beta-galactosidase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd wherein enzymes other than milk-clotting enzymes are used (e.g. lipase and beta-galactosidase).
[A23C 19/04] characterised by the use of specific enzymes of vegetable or animal origin -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd wherein enzymes of plant, vegetable or animal origin are used.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preparation of cheese curd using microbial enzymes -> A23C19/032
[A23C 19/041] Proteolytic or milk clotting enzymes from plants or vegetables, e.g. papain, ficin, bromelain -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd wherein proteolytic or milk clotting enzymes from plants or vegetables are used (e.g. papaion, ficin, bromelain).
[A23C 19/043] Enzymes other than proteolytic enzymes or milk clotting enzymes, e.g. lipase, lysosyme -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd wherein enzymes of plant/vegetable/animal origin other than proteolytic or milk clotting are used (e.g. lipase, lysozyme).
[A23C 19/045] Coagulation of milk without rennet or rennet substitutes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd by coagulation of milk without rennet or rennet substitutes, which is followed by whey separation.
[A23C 19/0455] Coagulation by direct acidification without fermentation of the milk, e.g. by chemical or physical means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd by coagulation of milk without rennet or rennet substitutes, which is followed by whey separation, characterised in that the milk is directly acidified without fermentation (e.g. by chemical or physical means).
[A23C 19/05] Treating milk before coagulation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd, wherein milk is treated before coagulation. Separation of whey from curd.
[A23C 19/051] Acidifying by combination of acid fermentation and of chemical or physical means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd, wherein before coagulation the milk is acidified by acid fermentation combined with chemical or physical means, e.g. the acidification of the milk before the coagulation of the casein and the formation of the curd is performed by combining lactic acid fermentation and chemical or physical means like hydrochloric acid or electrolysis
[A23C 19/052] Acidifying only by chemical or physical means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd, wherein before coagulation the milk is acidified only by chemical or physical means.
[A23C 19/053] Enrichment of milk with whey, whey components, substances recovered from separated whey, isolated or concentrated proteins from milk -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd, wherein before coagulation of the milk, whey, whey components, substances recovered from separated whey, or isolated or concetrated milk proteins (e.g. WPC, WPI) are added to the milk (thereby "enriching" the milk).
[A23C 19/054] using additives other than acidifying agents, NaCl, CaCl 2 , dairy products, proteins, fats, enzymes or microorganisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd, wherein before coagulation, the milk is treated with additives other than the following: acidifying agents, NaCl, CaCl 2 , dairy product, proteins, fats, enzymes or microorganisms.
[A23C 19/055] Addition of non-milk fats or non-milk proteins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing cheese curd, wherein non-milk fats or proteins are added (including polyol fatty acid polyesters or mineral oils).
[A23C 19/06] Treating cheese curd after whey separation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for treating cheese curd after whey separation (including treatment of cheese curd prepared without whey separation) and products obtained thereby.
[A23C 19/061] Addition of, or treatment with, microorganisms  A23C19/0682  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for treating cheese curd after whey separation (including treatment of cheese curd prepared without whey separation) wherein microorganisms are added to the curd, or used for the treatment thereof, respectively, and products obtained thereby.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Mould-ripened or bacterial surface ripened cheese -> A23C19/0682
[A23C 19/062] using only lactic acid bacteria, e.g. pediococcus, leconostoc or bifidus sp., or propionic acid bacteria; Treatment with non-specified acidifying bacterial cultures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for treating cheese curd after whey separation (including treatment of cheese curd prepared without whey separation) wherein only lactic or propionic acid bacteria (including Pediococcus, Leuconostoc, Bifidobacteria) or non-specified acidifying bacterial cultures are added to the curd, or used for the treatment thereof, respectively, and products obtained thereby.
[A23C 19/063] Addition of, or treatment with, enzymes or cell-free extracts of microorganisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for treating cheese curd after whey separation (including treatment of cheese curd prepared without whey separation) wherein enzymes or cell-free extracts of microorganisms are added to the curd, or used for the treatment thereof, respectively, and products obtained thereby.
[A23C 19/064] Salting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for salting cheese curd after whey separation (including treatment of cheese curd prepared without whey separation) and products obtained thereby.
[A23C 19/068] Particular types of cheese -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Particular types of cheese, such as Cheddar, Mozzarella, cottage cheese and process cheese, as well as aspects of the methods for preparing said particular types of cheese which are associated specifically with the particularity thereof, and not covered elsewhere.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Making cheese curd in general -> A23C19/02
[A23C 19/0682] Mould-ripened or bacterial surface ripened cheeses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mould-ripened or bacterial surface ripened cheese, as well as aspects of the methods for their preparation associated specifically with mould or bacterial surface ripening, and not covered elsewhere.
[A23C 19/0684] Soft uncured Italian cheeses, e.g. Mozarella, Ricotta, Pasta filata cheese; Other similar stretched cheeses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Soft uncured Italian type cheeses, pasta filata cheese (e.g. Mozzarella) and other similar stretched cheeses, as well as aspects of the methods for the preparation thereof associated specifically with the characteristic properties of said cheeses, and not covered elsewhere.
[A23C 19/0686] Cheese from whey, e.g. mysost -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cheese from whey (e.g. Ricotta, Mysost) as well as aspects of the methods for the preparation thereof associated specifically with the characteristic properties of said cheeses, and not covered elsewhere.
[A23C 19/0688] Hard cheese or semi-hard cheese with or without eyes  A23C19/072  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hard or semi-hard cheese other than cheddar, as well as aspects of the methods for the preparation thereof associated specifically with the characteristic properties of said cheeses, and not covered elsewhere.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cheddar type and similar hard cheese without eyes -> A23C19/072
[A23C 19/072] Cheddar type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cheddar type cheese and similar hard cheeses without eyes, as well as aspects of the methods for the preparation thereof associated specifically with the characteristic properties of said cheeses, and not covered elsewhere.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Hard and semi-hard cheese other than cheddar type or similar hard cheeses without eyes -> A23C19/0688
[A23C 19/076] Soft unripened cheese, e.g. cottage or cream cheese -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Soft unripened cheese (e.g. cottage cheese, cream cheese, fresh cheese), as well as aspects of the methods for the preparation thereof associated specifically with the characteristic properties of said cheeses, and not covered elsewhere.
[A23C 19/0765] Addition to the curd of additives other than acidifying agents, dairy products, proteins except gelatine, fats, enzymes, microorganisms, NaCl, CaCl 2  or KCl; Foamed fresh cheese products -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing soft unripened cheese (e.g. cottage cheese, cream cheese) wherein an additive other than acidifying agents, dairy products, proteins - except gelatine -, fats, enzymes, microorganisms, NaCl, CaCl 2 or KCl is used, as well as aspects of the methods for the preparation thereof associated specifically with the characteristic properties of said cheese, and not covered elsewhere. Foamed fresh cheese products (i.e. fresh cheese products containing gas as additive).
[A23C 19/08] Process cheese preparations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Process cheese preparations and methods for their preparation (e.g. melting, emulsifying, sterilising).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cream cheese -> A23C19/076
[A23C 19/081] Surface melting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Surface melting used in the preparation of process cheese preparations.
[A23C 19/082] Adding substances to the curd before or during melting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing process cheese preparations, wherein substances are added to the curd or cheese before or during melting, as well as melting salts.
[A23C 19/084] Treating the curd, or adding substances thereto, after melting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing process cheese preparations, wherein after melting the curd is treated, or substances are added thereto.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Addition of non-milk components to process cheese preparations after melting -> A23C19/093
[A23C 19/086] Cheese powder -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cheese powder and dried cheese preparations.
[A23C 19/09] Other cheese preparations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Other cheese preparations and mixtures of cheese with other foodstuffs, as well as methods for their preparation.
[A23C 19/0904] Liquid cheese products, e.g. beverages, sauces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Liquid cheese products, e.g. cheese sauces and cheese beverages, as well as methods for their preparation.
[A23C 19/0908] Sliced cheese; Multilayered or stuffed cheese; Cheese loaves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sliced, multilayered or stuffed cheese, and cheese loaves, as well as methods for their preparation.
[A23C 19/0912] Fried, baked or roasted cheese products, e.g. cheese cakes; Foamed cheese products, e.g. soufflés; Expanded cheese in solid form -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fried, baked or roasted cheese products (e.g. cheese cakes), foamed cheese products (e.g. soufflés), and expanded cheese in solid form.
[A23C 19/0917] Addition, to cheese or curd, of whey, whey components, substances recovered from separated whey, isolated or concentrated proteins from milk -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Other cheese preparations and mixtures of cheese with other foodstuffs obtained by addition, to cheese or curd, of whey, whey components, substances recovered from separated whey, or isolated or concentrated proteins from milk.
[A23C 19/0921] Addition, to cheese or curd, of minerals, including organic salts thereof, trace elements, amino acids, peptides, protein hydrolysates, nucleic acids, yeast extracts or autolysate, vitamins or derivatives of these compounds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Other cheese preparations and mixtures of cheese with other foodstuffs obtained by addition, to cheese or curd, of minerals (including organic salts thereof), trace elements, amino acids, peptides, protein hydrolysates, nucleic acids, yeast extracts or yeast autolysate, vitmains, or derivatives of these compounds.
[A23C 19/0925] Addition, to cheese or curd, of colours, synthetic flavours or artificial sweeteners, including sugar alcohols -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Other cheese preparations and mixtures of cheese with other foodstuffs obtained by addition, to cheese or curd, of colorants, synthetic flavours, or artificial sweeteners, including sugar alcohols.
[A23C 19/093] Addition of non-milk fats or non-milk proteins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Other cheese preparations and mixtures of cheese with other foodstuffs obtained by addition of non-milk proteins or non-milk fats, including polyol fatty acid polyesters or mineral oils.
[A23C 19/14] Treating cheese after having reached its definite form, e.g. ripening or smoking -- A23C 19/14 covers methods applied to cheese after it has attained its final shape, including processes such as ripening, smoking, coating or ageing that modify texture, flavour or shelf-life. It falls under the dairy products subclass of foodstuffs in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[A23C 19/16] Covering the cheese surface, e.g. with wax coating compositions -- The CPC entry A23C 19/16 concerns methods and compositions for applying a protective layer to cheese, such as wax or other coating materials, to preserve the surface, control moisture loss and guard against microbial or physical contamination. It falls within the dairy-product category of the broader food-stuff classification.
[A23C 19/163] with a non-edible liquid or semi-liquid coating, e.g. wax, polymer dispersions  in combination with an edible coating  A23C19/16 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for treating cheese after reaching its definite form by covering the cheese surface with a non-edible (semi-) liquid coating, e.g. wax or polymer dispersions.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Methods for treating cheese after reaching its definite form by covering the cheese surface with a non-edible (semi-) liquid coating in combination with an edible coating -> A23C19/16

[A23C 20/005] mainly containing proteins from pulses or oilseeds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cheese substitutes containing proteins mainly from pulses or oilseeds and methods for their preparation.

[A23C 20/025] mainly containing proteins from pulses or oilseeds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cheese substitutes containing neither milk components, nor caseinate, nor lactose, as protein, fat or carbohydrate source, and containing proteins mainly from pulses or oilseeds, as well as methods for their preparation.
[A23C 21/00] Whey -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Methods for concentration, evaporation or drying -> A23C1/00 - Processes for preparing milk (powder) preparations wherein the chemical composition of the milk (or whey) is modified by non-chemical treatment (e.g. ultrafiltration, microfiltration) -> A23C9/14 - Preservation of whey or whey preparations -> A23B11/80
[A23C 21/02] containing, or treated with, microorganisms or enzymes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Whey and whey preparations containing or treated with, respectively, microorganisms or enzymes, as well as methods for their preparation.
[A23C 21/023] Lactose hydrolysing enzymes, e.g. lactase, B-galactosidase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Whey and whey preparations containing or treated with, respectively, lactose hydrolysing enzymes (e.g. lactase, beta-glucosidase), as well as methods for their preparation.
[A23C 21/026] containing, or treated only with, lactic acid producing bacteria, bifidobacteria or propionic acid bacteria -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Whey and whey preparations containing or treated only with, respectively, lactic acid producing bacteria, bifidobacteria or propionic acid bacteria, as well as methods for their preparation.
[A23C 21/04] containing non-milk components as source of fats or proteins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Whey and whey preparations containing non-milk components as source of fats or proteins, as well as methods for their preparation.
[A23C 21/06] Mixtures of whey with milk products or milk components -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mixtures of whey with milk products or milk components, as well as methods for their preparation.
[A23C 21/08] containing other organic additives, e.g. vegetable or animal products -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Whey and whey preparations containing other organic additives, e.g. vegetable or animal products, as well as methods for their preparation.
[A23C 21/10] containing inorganic additives -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Whey and whey preparations containing inorganic additives, as well as methods for their preparation.
[A23C 2200/00] Special features -- A23C 2200/00 covers special features applied to dairy products such as milk, butter, or cheese, including additives, processing aids, packaging adaptations, or modifications that enhance appearance, stability, flavor, nutritional value, or shelf-life. It concerns any technical measure that goes beyond the basic composition of the product.
[A23C 2210/00] Physical treatment of dairy products -- A23C 2210/00 covers methods for physically treating dairy products, including operations such as heating, cooling, stirring, homogenising, drying or mechanical separation applied to milk, butter, cheese and related items. The classification is used for patents that focus on the physical processing steps rather than chemical or biological modifications. It falls within the hierarchy of human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, and specifically dairy products.
[A23C 2210/05] Conventional drying followed by fluidised bed drying -- The A23C 2210/05 entry falls under Human Necessities > Foods or Foodstuffs > Dairy Products and covers the physical treatment of dairy items, specifically the combination of conventional drying followed by fluidised-bed drying. This process is used to reduce moisture in milk-derived products such as butter or cheese, improving shelf-life and facilitating further handling or packaging.
[A23C 2210/10] General processes or apparatus not classified in -- A23C 2210/10 covers general processes or apparatus for the physical treatment of dairy products that are not specifically classified elsewhere, such as handling, mixing, or moving milk, butter, cheese or related foods. It applies to equipment and methods used in dairy processing that do not fall under more detailed sub-categories.
[A23C 2210/15] High pressure treatment -- A23C 2210/15 relates to the use of high-pressure processing as a physical treatment for dairy products such as milk, butter or cheese. The method employs elevated pressure to inactivate microorganisms and extend shelf life while maintaining the nutritional and sensory properties of the product. It is classified under physical treatment of dairy goods within the CPC hierarchy for foods and human necessities.
[A23C 2210/20] Treatment using membranes, including sterile filtration -- A23C 2210/20 covers the use of membrane technologies-including ultrafiltration, microfiltration and sterile filtration-to physically treat dairy products such as milk, butter or cheese, enabling separation, concentration or sterilisation while preserving the product's nutritional and functional qualities.
[A23C 2210/202] Treatment of milk with a membrane before or after fermentation of the milk, e.g. UF of diafiltration -- A23C 2210/202 covers physical membrane treatments applied to milk, such as ultrafiltration or diafiltration, performed before or after the milk undergoes fermentation. The classification is used for processes that separate, concentrate, or modify milk components with a membrane to affect the properties of fermented dairy products.
[A23C 2210/204] Use of a membrane during fermentation of milk -- A23C 2210/204 relates to the physical treatment of dairy products, specifically the use of a membrane during the fermentation of milk. It covers processes where a semipermeable barrier is employed to separate, concentrate or modify the milk substrate while lactic or other fermentative cultures develop, improving texture, flavor or shelf-life. This entry falls under the hierarchy Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Dairy Products.
[A23C 2210/206] Membrane filtration of a permeate obtained by ultrafiltration, nanofiltration or microfiltration -- A23C 2210/206 relates to the physical treatment of dairy products by applying membrane filtration-ultrafiltration, nanofiltration or microfiltration-to the permeate already obtained from an initial filtration step. The entry covers methods that further purify, concentrate or modify the liquid stream derived from milk, butter or cheese, enhancing its quality or functionality.
[A23C 2210/208] Removal of bacteria by membrane filtration -- This CPC subclass covers methods for eliminating microbial contamination from milk, butter or cheese using membrane filtration techniques such as microfiltration, ultrafiltration or nanofiltration. The process is applied to dairy streams to reduce bacterial load while preserving product quality.
[A23C 2210/25] Separating and blending -- A23C 2210/25 pertains to physical processes used to separate and blend dairy products such as milk, butter or cheese, employing techniques like centrifugation, filtration, pressing or mixing to achieve a desired composition or texture.
[A23C 2210/252] Separating a milk product in at least two fractions followed by treatment of at least one of the fractions and remixing at least part of the two fractions -- This CPC subclass describes processes in which a milk product-such as milk, butter or cheese-is divided into at least two fractions, one or more of the fractions undergoes a physical, chemical or biological treatment, and thereafter at least part of the treated and untreated fractions are recombined.
[A23C 2210/254] Cream is separated from milk, one of the fractions is treated and at least part of the cream is remixed with the milk -- This CPC subclass covers processes where cream is separated from milk, at least one of the separated fractions is subjected to a physical or chemical treatment, and a portion of the treated cream is then remixed with the milk to produce a modified dairy product.
[A23C 2210/256] Removal or separation of bacteria, or a fraction containing bacteria, by centrifugation -- A23C 2210/256 covers methods that use centrifugation to remove or separate bacteria, or bacterial fractions, from milk, butter, cheese or other dairy products, helping to improve safety and shelf-life. This physical treatment is classified under dairy product processing within the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[A23C 2210/258] Separation or recuperation of fats or lipids from whey or whey products -- This CPC subclass covers methods for separating or recovering fats or lipids from whey and whey products, typically through physical processes such as centrifugation or filtration. It falls under dairy product processing within the broader categories of foods and human necessities.
[A23C 2210/30] Whipping, foaming, frothing or aerating dairy products -- The A23C 2210/30 entry defines processes that physically treat dairy products by introducing air, such as whipping, foaming, frothing or aerating, to create a light, airy texture. It applies to milk-based foods like cream, butter or cheese that are deliberately expanded with gas.
[A23C 2210/40] Microencapsulation -- A23C 2210/40 concerns microencapsulation as a physical treatment
[A23C 2220/00] Biochemical treatment -- A23C 2220/00 refers to biochemical processes applied to dairy products, encompassing the use of enzymes, microorganisms or fermentation to modify, preserve or enhance milk, butter, cheese or related foods. It covers treatments such as culturing, curdling, ripening and enzymatic conversion.
[A23C 2220/10] Enzymatic treatment -- A23C 2220/10 covers the use of enzymes to chemically alter dairy products such as milk, butter or cheese, encompassing processes like curdling, ripening, flavor development or texture modification through enzymatic action. This subclass falls under biochemical treatments applied to dairy foods within the Human Necessities sector.
[A23C 2220/102] Enzymatic treatment in diffusion reactors, e.g. membrane reactors, without immobilisation of the enzyme -- This CPC entry covers the use of free (non-immobilised) enzymes in diffusion-type reactors such as membrane reactors for treating dairy products. It describes continuous enzymatic processes that rely on diffusion across a membrane to modify milk, butter, cheese or related foods, improving efficiency and product quality.
[A23C 2220/104] Enzymatic treatment with immobilised enzymes -- This CPC subclass relates to the biochemical processing of dairy items such as milk, butter or cheese by applying enzymes that are immobilised on a solid support. It covers methods where the immobilised catalyst can be retained, reused or separated from the product, including carriers such as beads, membranes or gels.
[A23C 2220/106] Enzymatic treatment with enzymes in microgranules or soluble matrices -- A23C 2220/106 covers biochemical treatment of dairy products such as milk, butter or cheese using enzymes that are incorporated in microgranules or soluble matrices, enabling controlled enzymatic reactions during processing. This sub-class falls under the broader categories of human necessities, foods, dairy products and biochemical treatment.
[A23C 2220/20] Treatment with microorganisms -- A23C 2220/20 relates to the biochemical treatment of dairy products by applying microorganisms. It covers the use of bacteria, yeasts or molds to modify, preserve or enhance milk, butter, cheese or similar foods through microbial action.
[A23C 2220/202] Genetic engineering of microorganisms used in dairy technology -- A23C 2220/202 covers the use of genetically engineered microorganisms in dairy processing, including bacteria, yeasts or fungi modified to produce enzymes, flavor compounds, or improve texture of milk, butter, cheese and related products. It falls under biochemical treatment of dairy items and addresses biotechnological methods for enhancing dairy technology.
[A23C 2220/204] Use of bacteria which are encapsulated, entrapped or immobilised -- A23C 2220/204 concerns the biochemical treatment of dairy products by employing bacteria that are encapsulated, entrapped or immobilised, enabling controlled fermentation, flavor development, preservation or safety improvements in milk, butter, cheese and related foods.
[A23C 2220/206] Slime forming bacteria -- The entry A23C 2220/206 covers the use of slime-forming bacteria in the biochemical treatment of dairy products such as milk, butter, or cheese. It pertains to processes that employ these microorganisms to modify texture, aid fermentation, or improve stability of dairy foods.
[A23C 2220/208] Fermentation of skim milk or milk and its addition in a small quantity to unfermented skim milk or milk, e.g. cheese milk -- A23C 2220/208 relates to a biochemical treatment where skim milk or milk is fermented and a small amount of the fermented product is added to unfermented milk, a step commonly employed in the preparation of cheese-milk.
[A23C 2230/00] Aspects relating to animal feed or genotype -- A23C 2230/00 covers technologies, methods or compositions that deal with animal feed or the genetic makeup of livestock used for dairy production, including feed additives, breeding strategies and genetic manipulation aimed at improving milk, butter or cheese output. It is situated under the hierarchy Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Dairy Products.
[A23C 2230/05] Milk or milk products from transgenic animals -- A23C 2230/05 covers the production of milk and milk-derived products obtained from transgenic animals, including methods for genetically modifying livestock to express desired dairy traits and the related feeding regimes. It falls under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Dairy Products.
[A23C 2230/10] Animal milk with modified composition due to a specific feed -- A23C 2230/10 in the Cooperative Patent Classification addresses dairy products, specifically animal milk whose composition has been altered through the use of specialized feed. This classification is used for inventions that detail how feeding practices impact milk properties.
[A23C 2230/15] Animal milk with modified composition due to manipulation of the animal, e.g. animal milk comprising antibodies, selection of animals having specific genotypes -- It covers animal milk whose composition is altered by genetic or physiological manipulation of the animal, such as inclusion of antibodies or other bioactive components, and includes selection of animals with particular genotypes to achieve this.
[A23C 2240/00] Use or particular additives or ingredients -- A23C 2240/00 relates to the use of specific additives or ingredients in dairy products such as milk, butter, or cheese. The classification covers methods of incorporating these substances to achieve desired properties, including flavor, texture, preservation, or nutritional enhancement.
[A23C 2240/05] Milk products enriched with milk fat globule membrane -- A23C 2240/05 covers milk-derived foods that have been fortified with milk-fat-globule membrane, an ingredient added to enhance nutritional or functional properties. The entry lies within the hierarchy for dairy products and specifies the use of this particular additive in the composition of milk, butter, cheese or related products.
[A23C 2240/10] Dairy products containing sterols or sterol derivatives -- A23C2240/10 refers to dairy products that have been formulated with sterols or sterol derivatives as additives. It covers milk, butter, cheese and related items where these compounds are incorporated for functional or nutritional purposes.
[A23C 2240/15] Use of plant extracts, including purified and isolated derivatives thereof, as ingredient in dairy products -- A23C 2240/15 covers inventions that incorporate plant extracts, including purified or isolated derivatives, as additives in dairy products such as milk, butter or cheese. It falls under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Dairy Products → Use of particular additives or ingredients, focusing on the functional use of botanical ingredients to modify or enhance dairy items.
[A23C 2240/20] Inert gas treatment, using, e.g. noble gases or CO -- A23C 2240/20 relates to the treatment of dairy products with inert gases, such as noble gases or carbon monoxide, to modify preservation, texture or flavor. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Dairy Products → Use or particular additives or ingredients.

[A23C 2250/05] Emulsifying cheese -- A23C 2250/05 relates to methods, compositions or apparatus for emulsifying cheese, enabling the production of stable cheese emulsions used in processed cheese, spreads and sauces. It falls under dairy product technologies that modify cheese to achieve a uniform, fluid consistency.
[A23C 2250/052] Emulsifying cheese below melting or pasteurisation temperature, e.g. below about 60 degrees -- The CPC entry A23C 2250/052 covers processes that emulsify cheese at temperatures below its melting or pasteurisation point, usually under about 60 °C, to modify texture or stability in dairy production. It falls under the dairy products subclass within foods or foodstuffs of the human necessities group.
[A23C 2250/054] Emulsifying cheese without melting or emulsifying salts, e.g. citrates or (poly-) phosphates or alkali metal (bi-) carbonates or sodium or calcium salts of organic acids -- This CPC subclass A23C2250/054 covers methods of emulsifying cheese without using traditional melting or emulsifying salts such as citrates, phosphates, alkali-metal carbonates or sodium or calcium salts of organic acids. It lies within the dairy products group of the foods and human necessities sections.
[A23C 2250/10] Cheese characterised by a specific form -- A23C 2250/10 relates to cheese that is defined by a particular shape or physical form, such as wheels, blocks, slices or other molded configurations. The code is applied to patents concerning the manufacture, processing or packaging of cheese where that specific form is a defining feature.
[A23C 2250/15] Shredded non-dried cheese -- A23C 2250/15 covers shredded non-dried cheese, including methods for cutting, shaping, and packaging cheese that remains fresh or moist rather than dried. The classification applies to products such as sliced or grated cheese intended for immediate consumption or further culinary use.
[A23C 2250/20] Compacting shredded or ground cheese other than curd or small cheese pieces without melting -- The entry A23C 2250/20 covers methods for compacting shredded or ground cheese-excluding curd or small cheese pieces-into a denser form without applying heat. It addresses non-melting consolidation techniques used to improve handling, storage or packaging of grated dairy products.
[A23C 2250/25] Cheese with fat content lower than 0.5%, including cheese from skim milk, i.e. no addition of fats -- A23C 2250/25 covers cheese that contains less than 0.5 % fat, including varieties made from skim milk and produced without adding any external fats. This subclass falls under dairy products within the human necessities group.
[A23C 2250/30] Blue veined cheeses, e.g. Roquefort, Gorgonzola, Stilton -- A23C 2250/30 designates inventions concerning blue-veined cheeses, such as Roquefort, Gorgonzola and Stilton, including their manufacture, ripening and treatment within the dairy product sector.
[A23C 2250/35] Smoking of cheese or curd -- The A23C 2250/35 code covers methods and apparatus for smoking cheese or curd, including techniques that use smoke to flavor, preserve, or modify the texture of dairy products.
[A23C 2250/40] Breaded cheese products, e.g. coating with a batter mix or other adhesive material followed by breading with crumbs or other particulate coating -- A23C 2250/40 covers cheese products that are coated with a batter or adhesive layer and then covered with crumbs or other particulate material to create a breaded item, typically intended for frying or baking. The classification applies to processed cheese foods where the breading forms an integral part of the product's preparation and presentation.
[A23C 2250/45] Cheese from buttermilk -- A23C 2250/45 deals with cheese produced from buttermilk, covering the preparation methods, formulation and characteristics of such cheese. It is situated within the dairy-products group of the CPC hierarchy, focusing on cheese made using buttermilk as the primary milk source.
[A23C 2260/00] Particular aspects or types of dairy products -- It covers specific varieties or characteristics of dairy products such as milk, butter and cheese, focusing on particular aspects like composition, processing methods or specialized uses.
[A23C 2260/05] Concentrated yoghurt products, e.g. labneh, yoghurt cheese, non-dried non-frozen solid or semi-solid yoghurt products other than spreads -- A23C 2260/05 is a Cooperative Patent Classification for concentrated yoghurt products, covering items such as labneh, yoghurt cheese and other non-dried, non-frozen solid or semi-solid yoghurts that are not marketed as spreads. It falls under the hierarchy Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Dairy Products.
[A23C 2260/10] Spreadable dairy products -- A23C 2260/10 refers to spreadable dairy products, encompassing items such as butter-like or cheese-based spreads that can be applied with a knife. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Dairy Products, covering specific forms and processing of these spreadable items.
[A23C 2260/102] Spreadable fermented dairy product -- A23C 2260/102 relates to spreadable fermented dairy products, including cultured yogurts, kefir and similar items designed to be applied as a spread on bread or other foods. It falls under the hierarchy of human necessities → foods or foodstuffs → dairy products, focusing on particular aspects or types of dairy.
[A23C 2260/104] Water-continuous butterlike spreads -- A23C 2260/104 covers water-continuous butterlike spreads, defined as formulations where water forms a continuous phase within a dairy-based matrix, producing a spreadable product with butter-like sensory properties. The classification includes the composition, processing methods and stabilisation techniques used to achieve the water-continuous structure.
[A23C 2260/15] Frozen dairy products -- A23C 2260/15 pertains to frozen dairy products, including ice cream, frozen yogurt, sorbets and other dairy-based foods that are processed or preserved by freezing for later consumption.
[A23C 2260/152] Frozen fermented milk products, e.g. frozen yoghurt or yoghurt ice cream -- A23C 2260/152 pertains to frozen fermented milk products, such as frozen yoghurt and yoghurt ice cream, and is classified under dairy products within the foods or foodstuffs section of the CPC.
[A23C 2260/154] Frozen non-fermented milk products -- A23C 2260/154 covers frozen, non-fermented milk foods such as ice-cream, frozen desserts, frozen yoghurt and other milk-based products that are solidified without undergoing fermentation. The class is a sub-category of dairy products within the broader foods and foodstuffs section.
[A23C 2260/20] Dry foaming beverage creamer or whitener, e.g. gas injected or containing carbonation or foaming agents, for causing foaming when reconstituted -- This subclass covers dry powdered beverage creamers or whiteners that produce foam upon reconstitution, such as those that have gas injected, contain carbonation, or include foaming agents. The focus is on formulations intended to create a frothy texture in drinks like coffee or tea.
[A23C 2260/25] Dairy compositions providing for tooth protective effects, e.g. anticariogenic effects -- A23C 2260/25 covers dairy-based preparations formulated to protect teeth, such as milk, cheese or butter enriched with anticariogenic agents that help prevent dental caries. The subclass includes compositions that combine dairy nutrients with fluoride, casein phosphopeptides, probiotics or other additives that promote oral health.
[A23C 2270/00] Aspects relating to packaging -- A23C 2270/00 covers inventions relating to the packaging of dairy products such as milk, butter or cheese. It includes the design of containers, packaging materials, sealing methods and dispensing mechanisms intended to protect, preserve and present these food items.
[A23C 2270/05] Gelled or liquid milk product, e.g. yoghurt, cottage cheese or pudding being one of the separate layers of a multilayered soft or liquid food product -- A23C 2270/05 concerns gelled or liquid milk foods such as yoghurt, cottage cheese or pudding when they form a distinct layer within a multilayered soft or liquid food product, focusing on the packaging aspects of these dairy layers.
[A23C 2270/10] Dairy products filled into pressurised containers with dispensing means for atomisation or foaming -- This CPC subclass A23C 2270/10 concerns dairy products such as milk, butter or cheese that are packaged in pressurised containers equipped with dispensing mechanisms that enable atomisation or foaming, covering the specific packaging methods and apparatus used for these foamed or sprayed dairy formulations.
[A23C 2270/15] Separate packaging or storing of foodstuffs or ingredients to be mixed prior to use or serving, e.g. storing into separate compartments of a single container or into separate containers -- A23C 2270/15 covers the separate packaging or storage of foodstuffs or ingredients that are to be mixed immediately before use or serving, such as providing distinct compartments within a single container or using separate containers.
[A23C 23/00] Other dairy products -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Other dairy products (i.e. dairy products not covered by the preceding classes).
[A23C 7/00] Other dairy technology -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dairy technology not covered elsewhere.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preservation of milk or dairy products -> A23B11/00
[A23C 7/02] Chemical cleaning of dairy apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and apparatus adapted for the chemical cleaning of dairy apparatus; use of sterilisation methods therefor.
[A23C 7/04] Removing unwanted substances from milk -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and apparatus adapted for removing unwanted substances other than lactose or milk proteins from milk by methods other than filtration.
[A23C 7/043] using chemicals in liquid or solid state, e.g. flocculating, adsorbing or extracting agents  A23C9/146  and  A23C9/148  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and apparatus adapted for removing unwanted substances other than lactose or milk proteins from milk using chemicals in liquid or solid state, e.g. flocculating, adsorbing or extracting agents.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Use of ion-exchange in the production of milk preparations -> A23C9/146 - Use of molecular sieve or gel filtration in the production of milk preparations -> A23C9/148
[A23C 7/046] by centrifugation without using chemicals, e.g. bactofugation; re-use of bactofugate -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and apparatus adapted for removing unwanted substances other than lactose or milk proteins from milk without using chemicals, e.g. bactofucation; re-use of bactofugate.

Milk preparations, milk powder and milk powder preparations per se.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Mixtures of whey with milk products or milk components -> A23C21/06 - Preservation of milk or milk preparations -> A23B11/10
[A23C 9/005] Condensed milk; Sugared condensed milk  A23C1/06  and  A23C1/12  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Condensed milk and sugared condensed milk as well as methods and apparatus adapted for their preparation.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Concentration by freezing out water -> A23C1/06 - Concentration by evaporation -> A23C1/12

[A23C 9/1213] Oxidation or reduction enzymes, e.g. peroxidase, catalase, dehydrogenase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fermented milk preparations and treatment using enzymes, wherein an oxidation and/or reduction enzyme (oxidoreductase, e.g. peroxidase, catalase, dehydrogenase) is added and/or used for the treatment.
[A23C 9/1216] Other enzymes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fermented milk preparations and treatment using enzymes, wherein other enzymes than oxidoreductases , e.g. lipid acyltransferase, mutarotase are added and/or used for the treatment.

Continuous bulk fermentation, heating or cooling, i.e. continuous inlet and outlet flow of yoghurt.

[A23C 9/148] by molecular sieve or gel filtration -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing milk preparations, milk powder and milk powder preparations by modifying the chemical composition of the milk (or milk derived material) using molecular sieve or gel filtration.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Ion exchange chromatographic treatment -> A23C9/1465

[A23C 9/1544] Non-acidified gels, e.g. custards, creams, desserts, puddings, shakes or foams, containing eggs or thickening or gelling agents other than sugar; Milk products containing natural or microbial polysaccharides, e.g. cellulose or cellulose derivatives; Milk products containing nutrient fibres -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-acidified gels, e.g. custards, creams, desserts, dairy puddings, shakes or foams, containing eggs or thickening or gelling agents other than sugar, and methods for their preparation. [A23D] EDIBLE OILS OR FATS, e.g. MARGARINES, SHORTENINGS OR COOKING OILS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preservation of edible oils or fats -> A23B20/00 - Producing, refining or preserving fats or fatty substances -> C11B - Fatty acids obtained from fats, oils or waxes -> C11C  **Glossary:** - oil: substance that is in a viscous liquid state ("oily") at ambient temperatures or slightly warmer, and is both hydrophobic (immiscible with water) and lipophilic (miscible with other oils). This general definition includes compound classes with otherwise unrelated chemical structures, properties and uses, including vegetable oils, petrochemical oils and volatile essential oils. Oil is a nonpolar substance. The term "oil" is used for any substance that does not mix with water and has a greasy feel, such as petroleum (or crude oil) and heating oil, regardless of its chemical structure. - edible oil: edible or cooking oil that is a fat of plant, animal or microbial origin, is liquid at room temperature and is suitable for food use. Some of the many different kinds of edible vegetable oils include: olive oil, palm oil, soybean oil, canola oil, pumpkin seed oil, corn oil, sunflower oil, safflower oil, peanut oil, grape seed oil, sesame oil, argan oil and rice bran oil. Many other kinds of vegetable oils are also used for cooking. The generic expression "vegetable oil" when used to characterise an oil-based cooking product, refers to a blend of a variety of oils often based on palm, corn, soybean or sunflower oils. Fish oil is an example of an edible oil of animal origin. Microbial oils are also included in this category. - fat: unctuous substance of little consistency that melts easily, known in chemistry as body fat and present in various parts of the human or animal body. Fats consist of a wide group of compounds that are generally soluble in organic solvents and largely insoluble in water. Chemically, fats are generally triesters of glycerol and fatty acids. Fats may be either solid or liquid at normal room temperature, depending on their structure and composition. Although the words "oils", "fats" and "lipids" are all used to refer to fats, "oils" is usually used to refer to fats that are liquids at normal room temperature, while "fats" is usually used to refer to lipids that are solids at normal room temperature. The term "lipids" is used to refer to both liquid and solid fats, along with other related substances. Fats form a category of lipids that are distinguished from other lipids by their chemical structure and physical properties. This category of molecules is important for many forms of life, serving both structural and metabolic functions. They are an important part of the diet of most heterotrophs (including humans). Fats or lipids are broken down in the body by enzymes called lipases produced in the pancreas. - shortening: fat that is used in food preparation, especially baked goods, and is called shortening because it promotes a "short" or crumbly texture (as in shortbread). The term "shortening" can be used more broadly to apply to any fat that is used for baking and that is solid at room temperature, such as lard, but as used in recipes it refers to a hydrogenated vegetable oil that is solid at room temperature. Shortening generally has a higher smoke point than butter and margarine, and it may have 100% fat content, compared to about 80% for butter and margarine.
[A23D 7/00] Edible oil or fat compositions containing an aqueous phase, e.g. margarines -- Group A23D7/00 is impacted by reclassification into groups A23B20/00 and A23B20/10 . Groups A23D7/00 , A23B20/00 and A23B20/10 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
