[A23D 7/0056] Spread compositions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Spreads characterised by other components (e.g. proteins, starches, etc.)
[A23D 7/01] Other fatty acid esters, e.g. phosphatides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-specified use characterized by non-trigyleride ester ingredients including mono- and diglycerides (e.g. lecithins, glycerides but not triglycerides.)
[A23D 7/011] Compositions other than spreads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-spreads characterised by fatty acid esters (e.g. lecithins, glycerides but not triglycerides.)
[A23D 7/013] Spread compositions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Spreads characterised by fatty acid esters (e.g. lecithins, glycerides but not triglycerides.)
[A23D 7/015] Reducing calorie content -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compositions characterized by lowering fat etc. such as halvarines
[A23D 7/02] characterised by the production or working-up -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compositions characterised by general production methods imparting properties to final product
[A23D 7/04] Working-up -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Working up procedures specified
[A23D 7/05] characterised by essential cooling -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compositions charaterised by use of cooling procedures (e.g. votators, crystallisers)
[A23D 9/00] Other edible oils or fats, e.g. shortenings or cooking oils -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Oils or fats suitable for food use (including genetically modified) and characterised by their trigylceride composition.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preservation of other edible oils -> A23B20/30
[A23D 9/007] characterised by ingredients other than fatty acid triglycerides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  OIls or fats suitable for food use (incl. genetically modified) characterized by non-ester, non-triglyceride ingredients
[A23D 9/013] Other fatty acid esters, e.g. phosphatides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  OIls or fats suitable for food use (incl. genetically modified) characterized by the non-triglyceride ester ingredients
[A23D 9/02] characterised by the production or working-up -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compositions characterised by general production methodswhich lead to an oil/fat with a particular composition/form , e.g. olive oils with high antioxidants levels obtained from a second pressing or cold pressing of oils in general.
[A23D 9/04] Working-up -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compositions characterised by specific production methods which lead to an oil/fat with a particular composition/form, e.g. olive oily with high antioxidant level obtained from a second pressing using extrusion or cold pressing in an extruder.
[A23D 9/05] Forming free-flowing pieces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  characterized by process which forms free flowing pieces including by coating, chilling etc.
[A23F] COFFEE -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Synthetic spices, flavouring agents or condiments, e.g. synthetic tea flavours -> A23L27/20 - Synthetic coffee flavours -> A23L27/28 - Machines for cleaning, blanching, drying or roasting fruits or vegetables, e.g. coffee -> A23N12/00 - Coffee mills -> A47J42/00  **Glossary:** - alkaloid: stimulant xanthine drugs caffeine, theobromine and theophylline contained in coffee beans and/or tea leaves - coffee: stimulant product or beverage made from roasted seeds, commonly called coffee beans, which come from a plant of the genus Coffea, e.g. Coffea arabica or Coffea canephora - coffee substitutes: coffee substitutes (solid or liquid) are non-coffee products that normally do not contain caffeine and are used to substitute for coffee while preserving its taste. Grain coffee and other substitutes can be made by roasting or decocting various organic substances. Common ingredients include acorn, barley and malt, beech nut, chicory root, corn, fig or rye. - decaffeination: process of removing the alkaloid caffeine from coffee beans or tea leaves - herbal tea, tisane, ptisan: any Camellia sinensis-free product or beverage prepared by hot water infusion of plant material, usually leaves, but also fruits, flowers and possibly even bark or other parts. Herbal teas belong to the group of tea substitutes. - tea: stimulant product or beverage made from the leaves of the traditional tea plant (Camellia sinensis) - tea substitutes: tea substitutes (solid or liquid) are non-tea products, i.e. are not derived from the traditional tea plant (Camellia sinensis), and include mate (Ilex paraguariensis), lemon balm (Melissa officinalis) or rose hips (Rosa canina); cf. "herbal tea"
[A23F 2200/00] Special features -- A23F 2200/00 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers special features related to coffee, including additives, processing methods, and packaging that enhance or modify the product's characteristics beyond basic preparation. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and United States Patent and Trademark Office for detailed categorization of inventions.
[A23F 3/00] Tea -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preservation of tea, tea substitutes or herbal tea -> A23B85/00  **Glossary:** - tea: Camellia sinensis - tea substitutes: tea substitutes (solid or liquid) are non-tea products, i.e. are not derived from the traditional tea plant (Camellia sinensis), and include mate (Ilex paraguariensis), lemon balm (Melissa officinalis) or rose hips (Rosa canina); cf. "herbal tea"
[A23F 3/06] Treating tea before extraction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Tea harvesting methods, e.g. for producing morning tea; Withering or wilting; Bruising; Deactivation of enzymes in leaves (fixation or kill-green); Sweltering or yellowing; Drying; Aging or curing  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Tea extract preparations -> A23F3/16 - Reducing or removing alkaloid content -> A23F3/36
[A23F 3/08] Oxidation -- A23F 3/08 concerns methods and apparatus for oxidising tea, i.e., the controlled enzymatic browning of tea leaves that develops their colour, flavour and aroma. It covers processes such as withering, bruising, rolling and fermentation that promote oxidation, as well as conditions and treatments used to achieve the desired degree of oxidation.
[A23F 3/10] Fermentation with addition of microorganisms or enzymes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Post-fermentation of dried, rolled leaves (Puer tea).
[A23F 3/12] Rolling or shredding tea leaves -- A23F 3/12 covers processes that mechanically treat tea leaves by rolling or shredding to develop flavor and prepare them for drying or packaging. The subclass includes equipment and methods that break down leaf structure without crushing, facilitating the quality of the final infusion.
[A23F 3/14] Tea preparations, e.g. using additives -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Leaf tea compositions per se, which may contain non-flavouring additives.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Tea compositions characterised by flavouring additives -> A23F3/40
[A23F 3/16] Tea extraction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Extraction of non-water soluble tea constituents, using e.g. solvent.
[A23F 3/163] Liquid or semi-liquid tea extract preparations, e.g. gels or liquid extracts in solid capsules -- **Glossary:** - tea juice: juice obtained by pressing fresh or partly processed tea leaves
[A23F 3/166] Addition of, or treatment with, enzymes or microorganisms -- A23F 3/166 covers processes that add or treat coffee or tea with enzymes or microorganisms, such as fermentation, enzymatic hydrolysis, or microbial inoculation, to modify flavor, aroma, nutritional properties, or shelf-life. It falls within the hierarchy of human necessities, foods, coffee and tea.
[A23F 3/18] Extraction of water soluble tea constituents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Brewing, steeping, infusing or extracting leaf tea material with hot or cold water or with a polar solvent.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Addition of, or treatment with, enzymes or microorganisms -> A23F3/166 - Isolation of tea flavour or tea oil -> A23F3/42
[A23F 3/20] Removing unwanted substances -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Removing unwanted substances other than caffeine from tea extracts or infusions.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Addition of, or treatment with, enzymes or microorganisms -> A23F3/166 - Reducing or removing alkaloid content -> A23F3/38
[A23F 3/205] Using flocculating or adsorbing agents -- The A23F 3/205 subclass concerns processes for treating coffee or tea with flocculating or adsorbing agents to remove suspended solids, improve clarity, or modify flavor. It includes the selection, dosage and application of such agents during brewing, extraction or post-processing steps. This falls under the broader categories of human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, coffee and tea.
[A23F 3/22] Drying or concentrating tea extract -- A23F 3/22 relates to methods for drying or concentrating tea extract, such as spray-drying, freeze-drying, evaporation or other techniques that produce tea powders, concentrates or extracts for use in beverages and food products. It falls under the hierarchy human necessities → foods or foodstuffs → coffee → tea.

[A23F 3/24] by freezing out the water -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Freeze concentration.  **Glossary:** - freeze concentration: tea extract is cooled down to form ice crystals, which are then separated
[A23F 3/26] by lyophilisation -- A23F 3/26 is a CPC subclass under HUMAN NECESSITIES → FOODS OR FOODSTUFFS → COFFEE that concerns coffee that has been processed by lyophilisation, i.e., freeze-drying to remove moisture while preserving flavor and aroma. This method is used to produce instant or shelf-stable coffee products.
[A23F 3/28] by spraying into a gas stream -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Spray drying.
[A23F 3/30] Further treatment of dried tea extract -- The A23F 3/30 subclass covers post-drying processes for tea extract, including purification, fractionation, blending and conversion into powders, concentrates or other functional forms intended for use in beverages, foods or nutraceutical products.
[A23F 3/32] Agglomerating, flaking or tabletting -- A23F 3/32 covers processes for converting coffee or tea particles into agglomerated forms such as flakes, granules or compressed tablets. The classification includes methods of mixing, binding, compressing or otherwise shaping the material to improve handling, storage or brewing performance. It is part of the CPC hierarchy under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Coffee → Tea.
[A23F 3/34] Tea substitutes, e.g. matè -- A23F 3/34 covers inventions related to non-tea beverages used as tea alternatives, such as matè and other herbal or plant-based infusions that mimic tea's preparation or consumption characteristics. It includes processes, compositions, and apparatuses for producing and serving these substitutes.
[A23F 3/36] Reducing or removing alkaloid content -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Decaffeinated tea.
[A23F 3/363] by addition of alkaloid neutralising or complexing agents  A23F3/166  takes precedence -- A23F 3/363 covers tea preparations in which alkaloid-neutralising or complexing agents are added, typically to reduce caffeine or other alkaloid effects. This subclass refines the broader category A23F 3/166, which includes general processes for treating tea, by specifying the use of such agents.
[A23F 3/366] by extraction of the leaves with selective solvents -- A23F 3/366 covers methods of obtaining tea extracts by treating the harvested leaves with solvents that selectively dissolve desired components, such as caffeine, polyphenols or flavors, while leaving undesired material behind. The classification is used for processes that isolate these constituents for food, beverage or pharmaceutical applications.
[A23F 3/38] Reducing or removing alkaloid content from tea extract -- A23F 3/38 covers processes for reducing or eliminating alkaloids from tea extracts, typically to improve flavor or reduce bitterness. The methods may involve chemical, physical, or enzymatic treatments applied during extraction or post-extraction stages.
[A23F 3/385] using flocculating, precipitating, adsorbing or complex-forming agents, or ion-exchangers -- A23F 3/385 covers processes for treating coffee or tea with flocculating, precipitating, adsorbing or complex-forming agents, including ion-exchangers, to separate or remove undesired components, clarify the brew, or recover valuable constituents. This subclass is part of the broader CPC group for foodstuffs, specifically coffee and tea.
[A23F 3/40] Tea flavour -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Synthetic tea flavours -> A23L27/20
[A23F 3/405] Flavouring with flavours other than natural tea flavour or tea oil -- This subclass covers processes or compositions that flavor tea with additives that are not natural tea flavour or tea oil, such as synthetic or other natural flavourings. It includes methods for imparting taste to tea leaves, tea bags or infusions using these alternative flavours.
[A23F 3/42] Isolation of tea flavour or tea oil -- A23F 3/42 concerns methods for extracting the characteristic flavour or essential oil from tea leaves, including solvent extraction, steam distillation, super-critical carbon-dioxide extraction and related techniques, aimed at recovering volatile compounds for use in foods, beverages, cosmetics or fragrances.
[A23F 3/423] by solvent extraction; Tea flavour from tea oil -- **Glossary:** - stripping: removing volatile constituents from tea leaves by passing a hot gas (e.g. steam) through the leaves
[A23F 3/426] by distillation, e.g. stripping leaves; Recovering volatile gases  flavour from tea oil  A23F3/423 -- A23F 3/426 pertains to methods that recover volatile flavor gases from tea or coffee leaves by distillation, such as stripping leaves to extract tea-oil aromas. This subclass details processes for capturing and concentrating the fragrant compounds released during the distillation of tea-derived oils.
[A23F 5/00] Coffee -- Groups A23F5/00 - A23F5/505 are impacted by reclassification into groups A23B90/00 and A23B90/10 . All groups listed should be considered in order to perform a complete search.

[A23F 5/043] in the presence of inert particles -- A23F 5/043 covers methods for preparing or treating coffee when inert particles are present, such as the addition, removal or handling of non-reactive solids during brewing, extraction, or filtration processes. This includes techniques that manage inert material to affect flavor, clarity, or consistency of the final coffee product.
[A23F 5/046] with agitation or transportation of the beans by gases; Fluidised-bed roasting or fluidised-bed cooling after roasting  A23F5/043  takes precedence -- A23F 5/046 relates to coffee processing methods that use gases to agitate or transport the beans, specifically fluidised-bed roasting or fluidised-bed cooling after roasting, where the bean mass is moved or mixed by a gas-flow system.
[A23F 5/06] of roasting extracted coffee -- The CPC subclass A23F 5/06 covers processes and equipment for roasting coffee that has already been extracted, i.e., heating the brewed or liquid coffee rather than green beans, within the broader categories of human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, and coffee.
[A23F 5/08] Methods of grinding coffee -- A23F 5/08 covers the various techniques and equipment used to grind coffee beans, describing mechanical, electrical or combined processes that achieve the desired particle size for brewing and extraction. It includes methods for controlling grind consistency, speed, temperature and the handling of grounds during and after grinding.
[A23F 5/10] Treating roasted coffee -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for treating roasted coffee.  All roasted coffee compositions, except flavoured roasted coffee composition. Those roasted coffee compositions may be characterised by e.g. physical properties such as particle size distribution, density or moisture content.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Removing unwanted substances -> A23F5/16 - Reducing or removing alkaloid contents or caffeine -> A23F5/20 - Coffee extraction, making instant coffee -> A23F5/24
[A23F 5/105] Treating in vacuum or with inert or noble gases; Storing in gaseous atmosphere; Packaging -- A23F 5/105 covers processes for preserving coffee by removing air or replacing it with inert or noble gases, as well as methods of storing coffee in a controlled gaseous atmosphere and the associated packaging techniques that maintain freshness and prevent oxidation.
[A23F 5/12] Agglomerating, flaking or tabletting -- The entry covers processes that convert coffee beans or grounds into agglomerated forms such as flakes, granules or tablets to improve handling, brewing or packaging. It includes methods like pressing, binding and drying that produce compact coffee products.
[A23F 5/125] Tablets or other similar solid forms -- A23F 5/125 covers solid coffee products shaped as tablets or comparable forms, such as instant-coffee tablets, coffee sachets that dissolve, and pressed coffee blocks. [A23F 5/145] Coating whole beans with a layer -- This CPC subclass covers methods for applying a coating to whole coffee beans, including protective, flavoring or functional layers applied before roasting or packaging. [A23F 5/16] Removing unwanted substances -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Reducing or removing alkaloid contents or caffeine -> A23F5/20

[A23F 5/166] by extraction of the beans, ground or not, with selective solvents other than water or aqueous bean extracts, including supercritical gases -- A23F 5/166 covers coffee extraction methods that use selective solvents other than water, applied to whole or ground beans. The process isolates desired constituents-such as flavor compounds or caffeine-by employing organic solvents or supercritical gases like carbon dioxide, rather than traditional aqueous extracts.
[A23F 5/18] from coffee extract -- A23F 5/18 pertains to the production of coffee from coffee extracts, encompassing methods and processes for creating coffee products that utilize concentrated or diluted coffee solutions.

It covers the application of these substances during extraction, filtration or post-treatment steps to improve coffee quality.
[A23F 5/226] by extraction with selective solvents -- The CPC subclass A23F 5/226 covers coffee that is obtained through extraction using selective solvents, such as super-critical carbon dioxide or other liquids that preferentially dissolve desired coffee constituents while leaving undesired components behind. [A23F 5/26] Extraction of water soluble constituents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Brewing, steeping and infusion methods for producing a coffee beverage from roasted coffee beans.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Addition of, or treatment with, enzymes or microorganisms -> A23F5/246

It specifies the flow of the extraction liquid through the stationary solid matrix to obtain the brew.
[A23F 5/265] the solid substances being transported through the apparatus during the extraction cycle -- A23F 5/265 relates to the solid materials that are conveyed through a coffee-making apparatus during the extraction cycle, such as coffee grounds or filter residues that move within the device as water passes through them. It addresses the handling, transport and disposition of those solids inside the extraction system.
[A23F 5/267] using additives, specific extraction media or specific coffee blends -- A23F 5/267 covers processes for preparing coffee that involve the use of additives, special extraction media, or defined coffee blends, focusing on formulations that modify flavor, aroma, or functional properties through added substances or tailored brewing liquids. It includes techniques that combine different coffee types or incorporate auxiliary ingredients to achieve specific product characteristics.
[A23F 5/28] Drying or concentrating coffee extract -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus for removing moisture from coffee extract or increasing its solid content, such as spray-drying, freeze-drying, evaporation and other concentration techniques that produce powders, granules or concentrates for food and beverage use.
[A23F 5/285] by evaporation, e.g. drying in thin layers or foam drying  A23F5/32 ,  A23F5/34  take precedence -- A23F 5/285 covers methods of drying coffee by evaporation, including techniques that dry the beans in thin layers or use foam drying. It is a sub-category of A23F 5/32 and A23F 5/34, which address general evaporative drying processes for food products.

[A23F 5/34] by spraying into a gas stream -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Spray drying.

[A23F 5/385] Tablets or other similar solid forms -- A23F 5/385 relates to solid coffee products such as tablets, sticks or other compressed forms that dissolve or release coffee when water is added. The subclass covers their preparation, composition and consumer use as ready-to-brew coffee items.
[A23F 5/40] using organic additives, e.g. milk, sugar -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Instant coffee mixtures containing organic additives such as milk powder, whitener, creamer, sugar, sweetener and foaming agents, including instant cappuccino products.

[A23F 5/42] using inorganic additives -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Instant coffee mixtures, including instant cappuccino products, containing inorganic additives, e.g. foaming agents.
[A23F 5/44] Coffee substitutes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Coffee substitutes, typically based on roasted chicory, roasted cereals or guarana, for preparing a beverage by adding hot water for brewing or infusion; any beverage product explicitly described as a coffee substitute or analogue.
[A23F 5/46] Coffee flavour -- A23F 5/46 covers coffee flavour, encompassing the creation, modification and use of flavouring substances that impart coffee taste to foods, beverages and related products, including natural extracts, synthetic analogues and processing methods designed to develop or enhance coffee aroma and taste.
[A23F 5/465] Flavouring with flavours other than natural coffee flavour or coffee oil -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Flavouring with artificial or natural substances that do not have a coffee flavour or aroma or that cannot be obtained naturally from coffee.
[A23F 5/48] Isolation of coffee flavour or coffee oil -- The A23F 5/48 entry covers processes that separate coffee's aromatic constituents or oil from beans or extracts, including solvent, supercritical CO₂ and steam-distillation techniques. It is used to obtain flavour-rich fractions for food, beverage or fragrance applications.

[A23G] COCOA -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[A23G 1/00] Cocoa -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preservation of cocoa or cocoa products -> A23B80/00
[A23G 1/0003] Processes of manufacture not relating to composition or compounding ingredients -- A23G 1/0003 covers manufacturing steps for cocoa products that do not involve combining or formulating ingredients, such as roasting, grinding, pressing, or refining cocoa beans. These processes affect the physical or chemical state of the cocoa material itself rather than creating a mixture with other substances. The classification is used for patents describing purely processing operations within the cocoa sub-section of food stuffs.
[A23G 1/0006] Processes specially adapted for manufacture or treatment of cocoa or cocoa products  A23G1/002  -  A23G1/005  take precedence -- The subclass A23G 1/0006 covers manufacturing and treatment processes that are specifically designed for cocoa or cocoa-derived products, including steps such as roasting, grinding, refining, liquor production and other operations that alter raw cocoa beans into usable forms. It groups the specialized techniques used to transform cocoa into chocolate, cocoa butter, cocoa powder and related ingredients.
[A23G 1/0009] Manufacture or treatment of liquids, creams, pastes, granules, shreds or powders -- A23G 1/0009 covers the manufacture or treatment of cocoa-derived liquids, creams, pastes, granules, shreds or powders, encompassing processes such as mixing, refining, conching, drying, granulation and powdering.
[A23G 1/0013] Weighing or portioning processes -- A23G 1/0013 covers methods and equipment for weighing or portioning cocoa, including measuring the mass of cocoa beans, nibs, liquor or powder and dividing it into defined amounts for further processing. The classification addresses automated or manual techniques that ensure consistent portion sizes in chocolate-making and related food-industry operations.
[A23G 1/0016] Transformation of liquids, pastes, creams, lumps, powders, granules or shreds into powders, granules or shreds; Manufacture or treatment of powders -- A23G 1/0016 covers processes for converting liquids, pastes, creams, lumps, powders, granules or shreds into powders, granules or shreds, and includes the manufacture or treatment of such powders, particularly in the context of cocoa products. This subclass falls under the cocoa category of foodstuffs within the human necessities section of the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[A23G 1/002] Processes for preparing or treating cocoa beans or nibs -- The A23G 1/002 entry covers industrial processes used to prepare or treat cocoa beans and nibs, including steps such as fermenting, drying, roasting, cleaning and alkalisation that modify flavor, texture or chemical properties before further use in chocolate production.
[A23G 1/0023] Cocoa butter extraction by pressing -- A23G 1/0023 covers the process of extracting cocoa butter from cocoa beans by mechanical pressing. It is classified under the cocoa subgroup of foodstuffs in the human necessities section. The method involves applying pressure to cocoa mass to separate the fat component.
[A23G 1/0026] Mixing; Roller milling for preparing chocolate -- A23G 1/0026 covers the mixing and roller-milling operations used to turn cocoa mass into chocolate, including refining, conching, and shaping steps that develop texture and flavor. It falls under the cocoa group of food items in the Human Necessities section.
[A23G 1/003] with introduction or production of gas, or under vacuum; Whipping; Manufacture of cellular mass -- A23G 1/003 covers methods for preparing cocoa products that involve introducing gas, applying vacuum, whipping, or creating a cellular structure. Techniques include
[A23G 1/0033] Chocolate refining, i.e. roll or mill refining -- A23G 1/0033 covers the refining stage of chocolate production in which cocoa mass is passed through rolls or a mill to reduce particle size and achieve a smooth texture. This process follows earlier steps such as grinding and is essential for creating high-quality chocolate products.
[A23G 1/0036] Conching -- A23G 1/0036 relates to the conching process used in chocolate production, where melted cocoa mass is mixed, heated and aerated to develop flavor and smooth texture. The step reduces particle size and volatile acids, improving the final product's mouthfeel and aroma.
[A23G 1/004] Longitudinal conching, i.e. backward and forward motion -- A23G 1/004 covers a method of conching cocoa in which the chocolate mass is moved back and forth along its length, using longitudinal motion to improve texture and flavor development. The technique alternates forward and reverse movement of the conching elements to promote uniform mixing and refine particle size.
[A23G 1/0043] Circular conching, i.e. circular motion -- A23G 1/0043 covers a cocoa processing step in which the conching operation is performed with a continuous circular motion of the material, rather than a reciprocating or oscillating movement. The circular conching method aims to improve mixing and aeration, resulting in smoother texture and enhanced flavor development in chocolate.
[A23G 1/0046] Processes for conditioning chocolate masses for moulding -- This subclass covers methods for treating chocolate mass prior to moulding, such as tempering, shearing, cooling or other operations that adjust viscosity, crystal form and texture to obtain a uniform, stable material suitable for being poured into molds and delivering consistent product quality.
[A23G 1/005] Moulding, shaping, cutting or dispensing chocolate  A23G1/0076  -  A23G1/0096  take precedence -- A23G 1/005 covers processes and devices for moulding, shaping, cutting or dispensing chocolate, including techniques that form chocolate into desired shapes or portions. It encompasses general methods for handling molten or solid chocolate before it sets, while more detailed sub-groups such as A23G 1/0076-1/0096 provide specific refinements.
[A23G 1/0053] Processes of shaping not covered elsewhere -- A23G 1/0053 pertains to processes for shaping cocoa products that are not addressed in other specific categories within the Cooperative Patent Classification system, focusing on unique or specialized methods.
[A23G 1/0056] Processes in which the material is shaped at least partially by a die; Extrusion of cross-sections or plates, optionally with associated cutting -- The A23G 1/0056 entry covers manufacturing processes for cocoa where the material is at least partially formed by a die, such as extruding it into sheets or specific cross-sections and optionally cutting the extruded product, methods commonly applied in chocolate and confectionery production.
[A23G 1/0059] Cutting or modelling of a section of plate; Embossing; Punching -- A23G 1/0059 covers methods for shaping a solid cocoa plate, such as cutting a portion, embossing decorative patterns, or punching holes, used primarily in chocolate manufacturing to produce tablets, bars or ornamental pieces.
[A23G 1/0063] Processes in which the material is shaped at least partially in a mould, in the hollows of a surface, a drum or an endless band, or by drop-by-drop casting or dispensing of the material on a surface, e.g. injection moulding or transfer moulding -- This subclass covers methods of shaping cocoa or cocoa-based material by forming it at least partly in a mould, in the hollows of a surface, on a drum or endless band, or by drop-by-drop casting onto a surface, including processes such as injection moulding and transfer moulding.
[A23G 1/0066] Processes for laying down material in moulds or drop-by-drop on a surface, optionally with associated heating, cooling, portioning, cutting cast-tail or anti-drip processes -- A23G 1/0066 covers methods for forming cocoa or chocolate products by depositing material into molds or applying it drop-by-drop onto a surface, optionally combined with heating, cooling, portioning, cutting, cast-tail or anti-drip steps to shape and finish the confectionery. This subclass falls under the broader categories of human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, and specifically cocoa.
[A23G 1/0069] Compression moulding of paste, optionally in the form of a ball, a rope or other preforms, powders or granules -- A23G 1/0069 covers the process of compression moulding cocoa paste into shapes such as balls, ropes, other preforms, or converting it into powders and granules. The method applies pressure to form the desired product without heating above the melting point, facilitating handling and further processing of cocoa preparations.
[A23G 1/0073] Moulding or shaping of cellular or expanded articles -- A23G 1/0073 covers methods for moulding or shaping cellular or expanded cocoa articles, such as aerated chocolate or cocoa-based foams, by forming porous structures in the material. It pertains to the cocoa subgroup within foods, addressing techniques that give expanded cocoa products their shape and texture.
[A23G 1/0076] Processes for moulding hollow products, open shells or other articles having cavities, e.g. open cavities -- A23G 1/0076 pertains to manufacturing methods that shape cocoa-based items into hollow or cavity-containing forms such as open shells, cups or other articles with internal voids, using moulding or similar forming processes. This subclass falls under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Cocoa.
[A23G 1/0079] Chocolate moulds processing  A23G1/0076  takes precedence -- A23G 1/0079 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to the processing of chocolate using molds, with a note that A23G1/0076 takes precedence. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with cocoa and its products under human necessities and foods or foodstuffs.
[A23G 1/0083] characterised by the material of the moulds -- A23G 1/0083 covers cocoa-related processes in which the moulds used are defined by their material composition, such as metal, plastic, silicone or ceramic. It classifies inventions that specify or rely on particular mould materials to affect the shape, release, or surface quality of cocoa products like tablets or bars.
[A23G 1/0086] Moulding in plastic or elastomeric material, or plastic or elastomer coated material -- A23G 1/0086 covers methods and devices for shaping cocoa or cocoa-based foods by moulding them in plastic or elastomeric materials, or by using moulds or surfaces coated with such polymers, focusing on the design of the moulds and the application of the coating to form the final product.
[A23G 1/0089] Tapping or jolting tables treatment -- A23G 1/0089 covers the treatment of cocoa beans by tapping or jolting on tables, a mechanical process used to loosen husks,
[A23G 1/0093] Moulds conveying, e.g. associated manipulations -- The CPC subclass A23G 1/0093 covers devices and operations used to move or transport moulds during cocoa processing, including any handling or manipulation steps that convey the moulds from one stage to another. It pertains to equipment that assists in the conveyance of moulded cocoa products within the manufacturing line.
[A23G 1/0096] Removing chocolate from a mould  discharging baked goods from tins  A21B3/18 -- A23G 1/0096 pertains to the process of removing chocolate from molds and discharging baked goods from tins, aligning with A21B3/18. This classification covers techniques and apparatus for these specific food processing tasks.
[A23G 1/02] Preliminary treatment, e.g. fermentation of cocoa -- This subclass covers the initial processing steps applied to cocoa beans, especially fermentation and other preparatory treatments that develop flavor before further refining. It falls under the cocoa sector of foodstuff classifications.
[A23G 1/04] Apparatus specially adapted for manufacture or treatment of cocoa or cocoa products -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Machines for roasting cocoa -> A23N12/00
[A23G 1/042] Manufacture or treatment of liquids, creams, pastes, granules, shreds or powders -- A23G 1/042 is a CPC subclass under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Cocoa that covers the manufacture or treatment of cocoa-based liquids, creams, pastes, granules, shreds or powdered products, detailing processes used to transform raw cocoa into these various forms.
[A23G 1/045] Weighing or portioning apparatus -- A23G 1/045 covers devices used to weigh or portion cocoa, including scales, dispensers and batch-size regulators employed in chocolate manufacturing or confectionery production. The classification applies to equipment that measures exact quantities of cocoa solids or powder and divides them into predefined portions for processing, mixing or packaging.
[A23G 1/047] Transformation of liquids, pastes, creams, lumps, powders, granules or shreds into powders, granules or shreds; Manufacture or treatment of powders -- A23G 1/047 covers processes for converting liquids, pastes, creams, lumps, powders, granules or shreds into powders, granules or shreds, focusing on the manufacture or treatment of powders in cocoa-related food production.
[A23G 1/06] Apparatus for preparing or treating cocoa beans or nibs -- A23G 1/06 covers machines and equipment designed to process cocoa beans or nibs, including roasting, cracking, winnowing, grinding or other treatments that prepare the raw material for chocolate production. The entry is situated in the hierarchy under human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, cocoa.
[A23G 1/08] Cocoa butter presses -- A23G 1/08 refers to apparatus and processes for extracting cocoa butter, specifically presses that separate the fat from cocoa liquor or cake. It includes mechanical designs that apply pressure to cacao material to obtain the butter for further use.
[A23G 1/10] Mixing apparatus -- A23G 1/10 covers mixing apparatus used in
[A23G 1/105] with introduction or production of gas, or under vacuum; Whipping; Manufacture of cellular mass -- A23G 1/105 relates to cocoa processing methods that involve introducing or generating gas, operating under vacuum, or whipping the material, resulting in a cellular or aerated mass such as foamed chocolate or mousse. This subclass specifies techniques for creating lightweight, porous cocoa products through gas incorporation or vacuum treatment.
[A23G 1/12] Chocolate-refining mills, i.e. roll refiners -- A23G 1/12 covers machines designed for refining chocolate, specifically roll-type refiners that grind cocoa mass into a smooth, uniform product. These devices are used in chocolate production to reduce particle size and improve texture, and fall under the CPC hierarchy for human necessities, foods, and cocoa.
[A23G 1/125] Conches -- A23G 1/125 pertains to conches, the machines and processes that refine chocolate after cocoa liquor is formed. Conching agitates and heats the mass to develop a smooth texture, reduce particle size and eliminate unwanted volatile acids, thereby enhancing flavor. The subclass covers equipment, methods and improvements specifically related to this cocoa-processing step.
[A23G 1/14] Longitudinal conches -- A23G 1/14 relates to longitudinal conches, a chocolate-making operation in which the cocoa mass is passed continuously through a long, narrow channel to reduce particle size, improve smoothness and develop flavor, differing from batch-type conching.
[A23G 1/16] Circular conches -- A23G 1/16 covers circular conches, a type of cylindrical, rotating vessel used in chocolate manufacture to refine the texture and flavor of cocoa mass by prolonged mixing and heating. The equipment continuously circulates the chocolate paste, promoting particle size reduction and volatile compound release, and is classified under cocoa processing within the Human Necessities section.
[A23G 1/18] Apparatus for conditioning chocolate masses for moulding -- This subclass covers machines that treat and temper chocolate mixtures before they are poured into molds, ensuring proper viscosity, crystallisation, and temperature uniformity for consistent molding. It includes continuous or batch conditioners, tempering tables, and related equipment.
[A23G 1/20] Apparatus for moulding, cutting or dispensing chocolate -- This CPC subclass A23G 1/20 covers machines that shape, cut or dispense chocolate, such as moulding equipment, slicers and dispensers used in confectionery production. It falls under the hierarchy human necessities / foods or foodstuffs / cocoa.
[A23G 1/201] Apparatus not covered by groups  A23G1/21  -  A23G1/28 -- A23G 1/201 covers devices used in processing or handling cocoa that are not specifically listed in groups A23G1/21 through A23G1/28, such as auxiliary conveyors, storage bins or cleaning equipment. These apparatuses support the production of cocoa products but do not directly perform grinding, roasting or pressing functions.
[A23G 1/202] Apparatus in which the material is shaped at least partially by a die; Extrusion of cross-sections or plates, optionally with an associated cutting device -- The subclass describes machines that shape material by forcing it through a die to produce continuous cross-sectional shapes or flat plates, and may incorporate a cutting device that trims the extruded product. It covers equipment where the material is at least partially shaped by a die during extrusion.
[A23G 1/203] Devices for cutting or modelling of a section or plate; Embossing; Punching, e.g. stamping-tool -- A23G 1/203, within the CPC hierarchy under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Cocoa, designates devices used to cut, shape, emboss or punch sections or plates of cocoa material, such as stamping tools employed in cocoa processing and confectionery manufacturing.
[A23G 1/205] Apparatus in which the material is shaped at least partially in a mould, in the hollows of a surface, a drum or an endless band, or by drop-by-drop casting or dispensing of the material on a surface, e.g. injection moulding or transfer moulding -- A23G 1/205 covers equipment that shapes cocoa or chocolate material by forming it at least partially in a mould, the hollows of a surface, a rotating drum, an endless band, or by drop-by-drop casting or dispensing onto a surface. Typical processes include injection moulding, transfer moulding and similar techniques used to produce molded confectionery items.
[A23G 1/206] Apparatus for laying down material in moulds or drop-by-drop on a surface, optionally with associated heating, cooling, portioning, cutting cast-tail or anti-drip device -- A23G 1/206 covers equipment that deposits cocoa or related food material into moulds or onto a surface in a controlled, drop-by-drop manner. The devices may include features for heating or cooling the material, portioning it, cutting excess cast-tail, or preventing drips during the laying-down process.
[A23G 1/207] Compression moulding of paste, optionally in the form of a ball, a rope or other preforms, powders or granules -- A23G 1/207 covers processes for compression-moulding cocoa paste, including shaping it into balls, ropes, other preforms or converting it into powders or granules. The subclass falls under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Cocoa within the Cooperative Patent Classification used by the EPO and USPTO.
[A23G 1/208] Moulding or shaping of cellular or expanded articles -- A23G 1/208 covers methods for moulding or shaping porous or expanded cocoa-based items, such as aerated chocolate, cocoa granules or other cellular confectionery products. It is part of the CPC hierarchy under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Cocoa and addresses the production of cellular articles from cocoa.
[A23G 1/21] Apparatus for moulding hollow products, open shells or other articles having cavities, e.g. open cavities -- A23G 1/21 covers machines and tools that form hollow or cavity-shaped cocoa products, such as open-ended shells, confections or other items with interior voids. The apparatus typically includes moulds, carriers and feeding mechanisms that shape melted chocolate or cocoa mass into open-cavity forms for further processing or filling. It is used in the manufacture of hollow chocolate pieces, decorative shells and similar cocoa-based food articles.
[A23G 1/22] Chocolate moulds -- A23G 1/22 relates to moulds used for shaping chocolate, covering the forms, cavities and associated equipment that give chocolate its final shape and texture before cooling and setting. This subclass falls under the cocoa category of food products, addressing tools that are essential in confectionery manufacturing.
[A23G 1/223] Mould materials -- A23G 1/223 covers the materials employed to make moulds for shaping cocoa-based products, including chocolate, cocoa butter and related confections. It encompasses compositions, coatings or inserts used to form reusable or disposable mould cavities that determine the final geometry and surface finish of molded cocoa items.
[A23G 1/226] Moulds of plastic or elastomeric material, or plastic or elastomer coated moulds -- A23G 1/226 covers moulds used in cocoa processing that are made from plastic or elastomeric materials, or moulds of other substances that have a plastic or elastomer coating. The classification applies to any such mould shape or form intended for forming chocolate, cocoa powders, confections or related cocoa products.
[A23G 1/24] Tapping or jolting tables -- A23G 1/24 covers equipment and methods for tapping or jolting tables used in cocoa processing, where kernels are separated from husks by vibrating or shaking the surface. This technique assists in removing debris and improving the efficiency of cocoa bean cleaning before further grinding or roasting.
[A23G 1/26] Conveying devices for chocolate moulds -- A23G 1/26 relates to equipment that transports chocolate moulds during production, including conveyors, rollers, and automated handling systems that move moulds between processing steps such as tempering, filling, cooling and demoulding. This subclass is used for patents describing mechanisms that convey chocolate moulds within cocoa-based food manufacturing lines.
[A23G 1/28] Apparatus for removing chocolate from the moulds -- A23G 1/28 covers devices designed to extract solid chocolate from moulds during cocoa product manufacturing. It falls under the Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Cocoa hierarchy, focusing on equipment that releases molded chocolate without damage.
[A23G 1/30] Cocoa products, e.g. chocolate -- A23G 1/30 covers inventions relating to processed cocoa items such as chocolate, cocoa butter, cocoa powder and other cocoa-derived foods. The subclass includes methods and apparatus for manufacturing, shaping, tempering and packaging these cocoa products. It falls under the broader categories of foods, foodstuffs and cocoa within the CPC system.

The subclass is applied when the patent claim focuses on the formulation of the cocoa mixture rather than on a distinct processing step.

This subgroup captures the range of formulations where the level of these components is a defining technical feature.
[A23G 2220/00] Products with special structure -- A23G 2220/00 covers cocoa-based food items that possess a distinct physical form or architecture, such as filled, layered, aerated, or otherwise specially structured chocolate products. These items are made by incorporating gas or creating a cellular matrix to achieve a light texture.
[A23G 2220/10] with a supported structure -- A23G 2220/10 covers cocoa-based food products that are formed on or attached to a supporting structure, such as chocolate or cocoa desserts molded on a substrate, coating a layer, or incorporating a reinforcement. This subclass falls under the hierarchy of foods, cocoa, and products with special structure.
[A23G 2220/12] being an inedible support, e.g. a stick -- A23G 2220/12 refers to the use of inedible supports, such as sticks, for cocoa-based items. [A23G 3/0036] Mould conveying, e.g. with the associated manipulation -- A23G 3/0036 relates to mould-conveying systems used in the manufacture of cocoa sweetmeats. It covers devices and methods for moving moulds that contain chocolate or cocoa confectionery, including conveyors, transfer mechanisms and associated handling equipment that transport or manipulate the moulds during production.
[A23G 3/0038] Removing articles from the mould; associated manipulation -- A23G 3/0038 covers the step of taking finished chocolate or other cocoa sweetmeat pieces out of their moulds and any related handling operations. It includes techniques, tools or processes used to release, flip, or transfer the molded articles without damage, often as part of continuous or batch production lines. This falls within the CPC class for cocoa sweetmeats under foods or foodstuffs.

[A23G 3/0042] Moulding or shaping of cellular or expanded articles -- A23G 3/0042 relates to processes and equipment for moulding or shaping cellular or expanded food products, such as aerated chocolate or confectionery, where a foam-like mass is formed and given a definite shape by a mould. It falls under the cocoa and sweetmeats subclass of the human necessities hierarchy.
[A23G 3/0044] Sugar-cookers processing -- A23G 3/0044 covers apparatus and methods for processing sugar in the manufacture of confectionery, particularly cocoa-based sweetmeats. It includes sugar-boiling, caramelisation, crystallisation and temperature-control techniques used in candy and chocolate production.
[A23G 3/0046] Batch-rolling, rope-forming, or sizing -- A23G 3/0046 covers processes for shaping cocoa-based confectionery, specifically the batch-rolling of sweetmeats, forming them into rope-like strands, or applying a sizing coating. The classification is used for inventions that treat these manufacturing steps for cocoa sweets.
[A23G 3/0048] Candy batch cooling, cooling tables processing -- A23G 3/0048 covers techniques and equipment for cooling batches of candy, particularly on cooling tables, during the manufacture of sweetmeats derived from cocoa. It includes methods for controlling temperature and solidifying confectionery products after molding or shaping.
[A23G 3/0051] Candy-pulling processes; processes for making cotton candy or candy floss -- A23G 3/0051 covers candy-pulling methods and the production of cotton candy or candy floss, detailing processes for creating these sweetmeats within the cocoa food category of human necessities.
[A23G 3/0053] Processes for moulding candy in the plastic state -- A23G 3/0053 covers the manufacturing steps used to shape candy while it is still soft and pliable, including extrusion, pressing or injection techniques that form the confection into desired designs before it hardens. It relates to sweetmeats made from cocoa-based or other sugary mixtures.
[A23G 3/0055] with introduction of sticks -- A23G 3/0055 covers cocoa-based sweetmeats that are made with a stick introduced into the product, such as chocolate-coated or candy sticks used for handling, shaping or serving the confection. It includes varieties where the stick forms part of the edible item or is embedded in the cocoa product.
[A23G 3/0057] Fondant beating or creaming -- The CPC subclass A23G 3/0057 relates to methods and equipment for beating or creaming fondant, a sugar-based paste used in confectionery. It covers actions that incorporate air, improve texture, and prepare the fondant for molding or coating, particularly in cocoa-derived sweetmeats.
[A23G 3/0059] Casting fondant in bulk -- A23G 3/0059 relates to the production of fondant by casting it in bulk, describing processes and equipment for pouring liquid sugar-based mixtures into molds or trays where they set into a smooth, pliable confection. The classification covers large-scale manufacturing techniques used for sweetmeats and other cocoa-derived products.

[A23G 3/0087] the solid particles being in a fluidised bed or whirling bed, e.g. convexed fluidised bed -- This subclass pertains to cocoa confectionery processes in which the solid cocoa particles are treated within a fluidised or whirling (convexed) bed during manufacture, using a moving gas stream to keep the particles suspended for improved mixing, coating, or granulation.
[A23G 3/0089] Coating with atomised liquid, droplet bed, liquid spray -- A23G 3/0089 covers methods for applying a coating to cocoa-based sweetmeats by atomising a liquid and creating a droplet bed or spray onto the product, enabling thin, uniform layers such as glazes, enrobements or flavorings.

[A23G 3/0093] Coating by dipping in a liquid, at the surface of which another liquid or powder may be floating -- A23G 3/0093 covers a method for coating cocoa sweetmeats by immersing them in a liquid bath that contains a second liquid or powder suspended on the surface, allowing the coating material to be transferred to the product during the dip. This technique is used in the manufacture of confectionery.

This subclass sits within HUMAN NECESSITIES → FOODS OR FOODSTUFFS → COCOA → Sweetmeats.
[A23G 3/52] Aerated, foamed, cellular or porous products -- A23G 3/52 relates to cocoa sweetmeats that are aerated, foamed, cellular or porous, such as aerated chocolate, mousse-type confections and other lightweight, porous cocoa-based treats produced by incorporating gases or foaming agents into the product.
[A23G 3/54] Composite products, e.g. layered, coated, filled -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[A23G 3/545] hollow products, e.g. with inedible or edible filling, fixed or movable within the cavity -- The CPC subclass A23G 3/545 covers hollow confectionery products, particularly cocoa-based items that contain a filling within a cavity. The filling may be edible or inedible and can be either fixed in place or movable, such as removable or flowing interiors.
[A23G 3/56] Products with edible or inedible supports, e.g. lollipops -- A23G 3/56 relates to confectionery items that are attached to a solid support, which may be edible or inedible. Typical examples include lollipops, candy on a stick and similar products where the support forms an integral part of the sweetmeat.
[A23G 3/563] products with an inedible support, e.g. a stick -- A23G 3/563 covers sweetmeats that are mounted on a non-edible holder, such as a wooden, plastic or metal stick, enabling the product to be handled or consumed without direct contact with the edible portion. Examples include lollipops, chocolate-coated sticks and similar confectionery items.
[A23G 3/566] products with an edible support, e.g. a cornet -- A23G 3/566 covers cocoa-based sweetmeats that are built on an edible support such as a wafer, pastry shell or cornet, where the holder is intended to be eaten together with the confection. It includes products like chocolate-filled cones or ice-cream cornets and the manufacturing steps for attaching the edible base to the cocoa filling.
[A23G 4/00] Chewing gum -- A23G 4/00 classifies inventions related to chewing gum, covering its composition, flavouring, sweetening agents, texture modifiers, and methods for manufacturing, packaging, and delivering the gum. It includes formulations that provide chewability, stability, and sensory properties, as well as novel gum bases and functional additives.
[A23G 4/02] Apparatus specially adapted for manufacture or treatment of chewing gum -- **Definition:** This place covers:  apparatus or method of manufacture
[A23G 4/025] for coating or surface-finishing -- A23G 4/025 pertains to methods and materials used for coating or surface-finishing of chewing gum, enhancing its appearance or protecting it within the broader category of cocoa-based foodstuffs.
[A23G 4/04] for moulding or shaping -- A23G 4/04 pertains to the process of moulding or shaping chewing gum, a specific subcategory under the broader classification of cocoa products within the human necessities domain. This code is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office.

The classification captures innovations where the gum's geometry or texture is the key inventive feature.

[A23G 9/00] Frozen sweets, e.g. ice confectionery, ice-cream -- A23G 9/00 refers to the subclass of frozen desserts within the CPC, covering products such as ice cream and other ice confectionery, including cocoa-flavoured varieties. It is situated under the broader categories of human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, and cocoa.
[A23G 9/04] Production of frozen sweets, e.g. ice-cream -- A23G 9/04 relates to the manufacturing of frozen sweets such as ice-cream or other ice confectionery. The classification falls under the cocoa subgroup of foods and describes processes for preparing these frozen desserts, including formulation, mixing, freezing and any associated steps needed to produce the final product.
[A23G 9/045] of slush-ice, e.g. semi-frozen beverage -- A23G 9/045 relates to the manufacture or composition of slush-ice and similar semi-frozen beverages, classified under cocoa-based foodstuffs within the broader category of human necessities.

It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Cocoa → Frozen sweets.
[A23G 9/086] using a rotatable container containing the cooling medium -- A23G 9/086 covers methods for producing frozen cocoa-based desserts such as ice-cream that use a rotating container filled with a cooling medium to freeze the product. The rotation improves heat transfer and yields a uniform texture while the cooling medium directly contacts the container.
[A23G 9/10] using containers which are rotated or otherwise moved in a cooling medium -- A23G 9/10 concerns the manufacture of frozen sweets such as ice-cream in which the product is placed in containers that are rotated or otherwise moved while immersed in a cooling medium, aiding rapid freezing and uniform texture. This falls under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Cocoa → Frozen Sweets.
[A23G 9/103] the container rotating about its own axis -- A23G 9/103 relates to frozen confectionery containers that rotate about their own axis, such as tubs or molds that spin to mix, aerate or shape ice-cream or other frozen sweets during preparation or serving. This feature aids uniform texture and presentation of the product.
[A23G 9/106] provided with agitating means -- A23G 9/106 covers frozen confectionery such as ice-cream that incorporates an agitating device, for instance a mixer or propeller, used during production, shaping or serving to create a smooth texture and uniform consistency.
[A23G 9/12] using means for stirring the contents in a non-moving container -- A23G 9/12 relates to methods of stirring the contents of frozen sweets while the container remains stationary, such as using built-in agitators or external devices during ice-cream or sorbet production. The classification covers inventions that improve texture and limit ice-crystal formation by continuous agitation in non-moving molds or tubs.
[A23G 9/14] Continuous production -- A23G 9/14 covers continuous processes for making frozen sweets such as ice-cream, ice pops and other ice confectionery. It includes equipment and steps that allow the product to be produced, pasteurised, frozen and shaped in an uninterrupted flow, rather than in batch operations. This subclass falls under the cocoa section of foods, reflecting its relevance to cocoa-flavoured frozen desserts.
[A23G 9/16] the products being within a cooled chamber, e.g. drum -- A23G 9/16 addresses frozen confectionery, such as ice-cream, that remains inside a cooled chamber during production, for example a drum or similar vessel, covering the handling, shaping and solidifying of the product within that refrigerated environment.
[A23G 9/163] with intermittent operation -- A23G 9/163 pertains to cocoa-based frozen desserts, such as ice-cream or other ice confectionery, that are produced using a process with intermittent operation. The subclass covers methods or apparatus where the freezing cycle is periodically stopped and restarted, for example to control crystal growth, improve texture, or incorporate additives during the freezing sequence. It falls under the hierarchy Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Cocoa → Frozen sweets.
[A23G 9/166] Feeding arrangements -- A23G 9/166 covers the feeding arrangements for frozen cocoa-based desserts such as ice-cream and other ice confectionery. It includes devices, packaging, portioning systems and methods for serving, dispensing, storing or handling these products to facilitate consumption.
[A23G 9/18] the products being on the outer wall of a cooled body, e.g. drum or endless band -- A23G 9/18 covers frozen desserts such as ice-cream or ice confectionery that are formed on the outer surface of a cooled drum, endless belt or similar refrigerated body, where the product solidifies while in contact with that outer wall. This sub-class falls under Human Necessities > Foods or Foodstuffs > Cocoa > Frozen sweets.
[A23G 9/20] the products being mixed with gas, e.g. soft-ice -- A23G 9/20 covers frozen sweet products such as ice-cream and other ice confectioneries in which a gas-typically air or nitrogen-is incorporated during preparation to create a soft-ice texture. The classification applies to methods and compositions where the gas is mixed with the product to achieve a light, aerated structure.
