[A23G 9/506] products with an edible support, e.g. a cornet -- A23G 9/506 concerns frozen dessert products that are built on an edible holder, such as a wafer, pastry, chocolate or other edible shell, which serves as a support for the ice-cream or similar frozen confection. Typical examples include ice-cream cones, cups formed from edible material, and similar items where the container itself is intended to be eaten together with the frozen sweet.
[A23G 9/52] Liquid products -- **Definition:** This place covers:  premix for ice cream, intermediate product for ice cream or frozen dessert preparation  **Glossary:** - ice cream: any edible frozen or congealed semi-liquid or pasty substance, e.g. slush ice - slush ice: semi-frozen beverage, granité
[A23J] PROTEIN COMPOSITIONS FOR FOODSTUFFS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Obtaining of protein compositions for foodstuffs, e.g. from meat, fish or sea animals, blood, eggs, hair, cereals, leguminous or other vegetable seeds, waste water, yeasts or milk.  Bulk opening of eggs and separation of yolks from whites.  Working-up of proteins for foodstuffs, e.g. by texturizing using freezing, extrusion, expansion, coagulation from or in a bath; by hydrolysis using chemical agents or enzymes.  Phosphatide compositions for foodstuffs, e. g. lecithin.
[A23J 1/00] Obtaining protein compositions for foodstuffs -- The CPC subclass A23J 1/00 covers processes for preparing protein-based food ingredients, including extraction, precipitation, enzymatic hydrolysis, fermentation, and purification of animal, plant or microbial proteins intended for use in foods such as meat analogues, dairy replacements and nutritional supplements. It addresses the technical steps required to obtain functional protein compositions suitable for incorporation into food products.
[A23J 1/001] from waste materials, e.g. kitchen waste -- A23J 1/001 covers processes for producing protein-rich food ingredients using waste streams such as kitchen leftovers. The classification falls under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Protein Compositions for Foodstuffs and focuses on converting discarded material into usable protein formulations.
[A23J 1/002] from animal waste materials  A23J1/10  takes precedence -- A23J 1/002 covers methods of obtaining protein compositions for foodstuffs that are derived from animal waste materials, such as slaughterhouse off-cuts, blood, or manure, and processing them into usable food ingredients. This sub-class falls under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Protein Compositions for Foodstuffs, and is superseded by the broader A23J 1/10 category.
[A23J 1/003] from animal excrements, e.g. poultry manure -- This CPC subclass A23J 1/003 covers methods for obtaining protein compositions for foodstuffs from animal excrements such as poultry manure, placing it under protein compositions for food within the foods or foodstuffs group of human necessities.
[A23J 1/004] from waste products of dairy plant  whey  A23J1/20 -- A23J 1/004 covers methods for obtaining protein ingredients for food products from waste streams of dairy plants, such as whey. It is classified under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Protein Compositions for Foodstuffs.
[A23J 1/005] from vegetable waste materials -- A23J 1/005 falls under the CPC group for protein compositions for foodstuffs and covers methods of obtaining protein isolates or concentrates from vegetable waste materials. The entry addresses how agricultural residues such as peel, stalks or pomace can be processed to recover usable protein for nutritional or functional applications in foods.
[A23J 1/006] from vegetable materials  A23J1/005 ,  A23J1/12  and  A23J1/14  take precedence -- The CPC subclass A23J 1/006 deals with processes for obtaining protein compositions for foodstuffs from vegetable materials. It resides in the hierarchy Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Protein Compositions for Foodstuffs, and the more specific groups A23J 1/005, A23J 1/12 and A23J 1/14 take precedence over it.
[A23J 1/007] from leafy vegetables, e.g. alfalfa, clover, grass -- A23J 1/007 covers methods for extracting or producing protein-rich compositions from leafy plants such as alfalfa, clover or grass for use as food ingredients. It belongs to the hierarchy Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Protein Compositions for Foodstuffs.
[A23J 1/008] from microorganisms  A23J1/18  takes precedence -- A23J 1/008 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to obtaining protein compositions for foodstuffs specifically from microorganisms, with a note that A23J1/18 takes precedence. This classification is part of the broader category of Foods or Foodstuffs under Human Necessities.
[A23J 1/009] from unicellular algae  seaweed  A23J1/006 -- A23J 1/009 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers the process of obtaining protein compositions for foodstuffs specifically from unicellular algae and seaweed, a subset of broader protein sources. This classification is used by the European Patent Office (EPO) and United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) to organize patent applications related to food technology.
[A23J 1/02] from meat -- A23J 1/02 covers processes for extracting or isolating protein-rich fractions from animal meat for use as protein compositions in food products, focusing on methods that recover functional proteins while removing undesired components. It falls under protein compositions for foodstuffs within the broader categories of foods or foodstuffs and human necessities.
[A23J 1/04] from fish or other sea animals -- A23J 1/04 covers processes for producing protein-rich food ingredients that are obtained from fish or other marine animals, including extraction, purification and concentration methods used to create protein compositions for use in food products.
[A23J 1/06] from blood -- A23J 1/06 covers the production of protein-rich food ingredients derived from animal blood, such as plasma, serum, hemoglobin or dried blood meals, for incorporation into foods. These materials are processed to improve functional and nutritional properties for human consumption.
[A23J 1/08] from eggs -- A23J 1/08 covers methods of obtaining protein compositions for foodstuffs using egg material. It includes extraction, separation, purification or fractionation of egg white, yolk or whole-egg proteins intended for incorporation into processed foods or nutritional products.
[A23J 1/09] separating yolks from whites -- A23J 1/09 covers techniques for separating egg yolk from egg white, including mechanical, centrifugal or membrane methods, to obtain distinct protein fractions for food applications. This subclass falls under protein compositions for foodstuffs, focusing on the preparation of protein isolates from eggs.
[A23J 1/10] from hair, feathers, horn, skins, leather, bones, or the like -- A23J 1/10 covers methods for obtaining protein substances from animal by-products such as hair, feathers, horn, skins, leather, bones or similar materials, for use in food-grade protein compositions. This subclass falls under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Protein Compositions for Foodstuffs.
[A23J 1/12] from cereals, wheat, bran, or molasses -- A23J 1/12 covers processes for obtaining protein-rich fractions from cereal-derived materials such as wheat, bran or molasses, intended for use as protein ingredients in food products. It falls under protein compositions for foodstuffs within the broader food necessities category.
[A23J 1/125] by treatment involving enzymes or microorganisms  enzymatic hydrolysis of proteins  A23J3/34 -- A23J 1/125 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to obtaining protein compositions for foodstuffs through processes that involve enzymes or microorganisms, specifically focusing on enzymatic hydrolysis of proteins as detailed in A23J3/34.
[A23J 1/14] from leguminous or other vegetable seeds -- A23J 1/14 relates to processes for extracting or preparing protein compositions from leguminous or other vegetable seeds for use in food products, falling under the broader categories of human necessities, foods, and protein compositions for foodstuffs.
[A23J 1/142] by extracting with organic solvents -- A23J 1/142 covers the process of obtaining protein compositions for foodstuffs by extracting the proteins with organic solvents. This subclass falls under protein compositions for foods, detailing solvent-based recovery methods used to isolate functional proteins from raw material.
[A23J 1/144] Desolventization -- A23J 1/144 covers the desolventization step in producing protein preparations for food, describing processes that remove residual solvents from protein isolates or concentrates after extraction. It focuses on techniques that ensure the final protein composition is safe and suitable for incorporation into foodstuffs.
[A23J 1/146] by using wave energy or electric current -- A23J 1/146 relates to processes for producing protein ingredients for food products that employ wave energy or an electric current. It falls within the hierarchy of human necessities → foods or foodstuffs → protein compositions for foodstuffs, and specifies the use of these physical energy sources to extract or modify proteins for culinary use.
[A23J 1/148] by treatment involving enzymes or microorganisms  enzymatic hydrolysis of proteins  A23J3/34 -- A23J 1/148 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to obtaining protein compositions for foodstuffs through processes that involve enzymes or microorganisms, specifically focusing on enzymatic hydrolysis of proteins as detailed in A23J3/34.
[A23J 1/16] from waste water of starch-manufacturing plant or like wastes -- A23J 1/16 relates to processes for obtaining protein compositions for foodstuffs by extracting them from wastewater generated by starch-manufacturing plants or comparable waste streams, falling under the hierarchy of human necessities → foods or foodstuffs → protein compositions for foodstuffs.
[A23J 1/18] from yeasts -- A23J 1/18 covers methods for obtaining protein compositions from yeast, including fermentation, cell disruption and purification steps, to produce yeast-derived protein extracts suitable for incorporation into foodstuffs. It falls under protein compositions for foodstuffs within the broader categories of foods or foodstuffs and human necessities.
[A23J 1/20] from milk, e.g. casein -- A23J 1/20 covers processes for obtaining protein compositions from milk, primarily casein, for use in foodstuffs. It falls under the hierarchy Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Protein Compositions for Foodstuffs, focusing on the recovery and preparation of milk-derived proteins for culinary or nutritional applications.
[A23J 1/202] Casein or caseinates -- A23J 1/202 relates to casein and its salts, called caseinates, when employed as protein ingredients in food products. The subclass covers processes for producing these protein compositions for incorporation into dairy and other foodstuffs.
[A23J 1/205] from whey, e.g. lactalbumine -- A23J 1/205 relates to methods for obtaining protein ingredients for food applications from whey, specifically including the recovery of lactalbumin. It falls under the protein compositions for foodstuffs subclass within the broader category of foods or foodstuffs in the CPC system.
[A23J 1/207] Co-precipitates of casein and lactalbumine -- The CPC subclass A23J 1/207 covers co-precipitates of casein and lactalbumin, which are protein mixtures obtained by simultaneous precipitation for use in food products. These compositions are intended to improve the nutritional or functional properties of foods.
[A23J 1/22] Drying casein -- A23J 1/22 covers the drying of casein as a step in preparing protein compositions for foodstuffs. The classification pertains to methods that remove moisture from casein to produce a stable, dry protein ingredient, facilitating its use in various food applications.
[A23J 3/00] Working-up of proteins for foodstuffs -- A23J 3/00 covers the working-up of protein substances intended for food use, including isolation, purification, concentration, enzymatic or chemical modification, drying and other preparatory steps that render proteins suitable as edible ingredients or nutritional additives.
[A23J 3/04] Animal proteins -- A23J 3/04 covers processes and methods for isolating, purifying, modifying or otherwise treating animal-derived proteins intended for use as food ingredients. It includes steps such as extraction, precipitation
[A23J 3/06] Gelatine -- A23J 3/06 relates to the processing of proteins for food applications, specifically the extraction, purification and drying steps used to produce gelatin from animal collagen as a gelling agent in foods.
[A23J 3/08] Dairy proteins -- The A23J 3/08 entry covers the preparation and treatment of dairy proteins for use in food products, including extraction, purification, concentration and modification of milk-derived proteins such as casein and whey. It addresses methods that convert raw dairy protein into functional ingredients for processed foods.
[A23J 3/10] Casein -- This subclass deals with the processing and purification of casein, the main protein found in milk, for incorporation into food products. It includes methods such as extraction, precipitation, acidification and separation steps used to obtain casein suitable for use in foods.
[A23J 3/12] from blood -- A23J 3/12 covers methods of processing blood-derived proteins for use as food ingredients, such as extracting, purifying, concentrating, or drying serum, plasma or hemoglobin to create protein preparations suitable for incorporation into foodstuffs. This subclass is part of the broader CPC group dealing with working-up of proteins for food applications.
[A23J 3/14] Vegetable proteins -- A23J 3/14 relates to protein compositions derived from plant sources that are used in food products. It includes the extraction, purification and modification of vegetable proteins to enhance functional properties such as solubility, emulsification or texture in foodstuffs.
[A23J 3/16] from soybean -- A23J 3/16 covers processes for working-up protein preparations intended for food use, specifically those derived from soybeans. It includes extraction, purification and modification steps that render soybean protein suitable for incorporation into various food products.
[A23J 3/18] from wheat -- This subclass covers methods of working up proteins for foodstuffs where the protein source is wheat, including extraction, purification and modification steps used to produce wheat-derived protein ingredients for food applications.
[A23J 3/20] Proteins from microorganisms or unicellular algae -- A23J 3/20 covers the preparation of protein ingredients derived from microorganisms or unicellular algae for use in food products. It includes processes that isolate, purify or modify such proteins to obtain suitable food-grade material.
[A23J 3/22] by texturising -- A23J 3/22 covers processes that improve or modify the texture
[A23J 3/222] Texturising casein -- The A23J 3/222 subclass covers processes that modify casein to give it a desired texture for use in food products. It includes techniques such as heating, acid or enzymatic treatment, and mechanical actions that alter the protein's structure to improve mouthfeel, firmness, or elasticity in items like cheese, desserts, and protein-enriched foods.
[A23J 3/225] Texturised simulated foods with high protein content  synthetic caviar  see   A23L17/35 -- A23J 3/225 covers the preparation of texturised protein-rich simulated foods, specifically products such as synthetic caviar that mimic the shape and mouthfeel of real seafood. It falls under the hierarchy of human necessities → foods or foodstuffs → protein compositions for foodstuffs → working-up of proteins for foodstuffs.
[A23J 3/227] Meat-like textured foods  meat extenders  A23L13/00 -- A23J 3/227 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers the creation of meat-like textured foods and meat extenders, focusing on the working-up of proteins to achieve specific textures and properties suitable for foodstuffs. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with protein compositions for foods.
[A23J 3/24] using freezing -- A23J 3/24 relates to the processing of protein-based foodstuffs by means of freezing, covering methods that employ low temperatures to preserve, texture-modify, or otherwise treat protein preparations during the working-up stage. This includes freezing steps integrated into the production of meat analogues, dairy proteins, and other protein foods to improve stability or functionality.
[A23J 3/245] Texturising casein using freezing -- The entry covers a method for texturising casein, a milk protein, by subjecting it to freezing to modify its physical structure for use in food products. It falls under the CPC subgroup for working-up of proteins in foodstuffs.
[A23J 3/26] using extrusion or expansion -- A23J 3/26 relates to processes that treat protein ingredients for food by extrusion or expansion, altering texture, solubility or functional properties to produce items such as texturized protein, snack foods or aerated products. This subclass falls under working-up of proteins for foodstuffs within the protein compositions for foodstuffs group.
[A23J 3/265] Texturising casein using extrusion or expansion -- A23J 3/265 pertains to the process of texturizing casein, a protein found in milk, through methods such as extrusion or expansion, enhancing its physical properties for use in food products.
[A23J 3/28] using coagulation from or in a bath, e.g. spun fibres -- A23J 3/28 covers processes that coagulate proteins within a liquid bath, such as forming spun-fiber structures, as part of preparing protein ingredients for food. The subclass addresses using bath-based coagulation to shape or recover protein materials during food-stuff processing.
[A23J 3/285] Texturising casein using coagulation from or in a bath -- A23J 3/285 covers processes that create a specific texture in casein by inducing coagulation while the protein remains in a bath or is removed into a bath for coagulation. The method is used to modify the physical properties of casein for food applications such as cheeses, gels, or protein-enriched products. It falls under protein-composition preparation within foodstuffs.
[A23J 3/30] by hydrolysis -- This subclass covers processes that treat protein preparations intended for food by applying hydrolysis, typically using enzymes or acids to break down proteins into smaller peptides or amino acids, improving functional properties such as solubility, digestibility, or flavour. It is indexed under CPC A23J 3/30 within the hierarchy human necessities → foods or foodstuffs → protein compositions for foodstuffs → working-up of proteins for foodstuffs.
[A23J 3/32] using chemical agents -- A23J 3/32 concerns the use of chemical agents in the working-up of protein compositions intended for food applications, covering processes such as precipitation, modification, stabilization or extraction of proteins during food manufacturing.
[A23J 3/325] of casein -- A23J 3/325 pertains to the processing of casein specifically for use in food products, falling under the broader category of working-up proteins for foodstuffs. This classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office.
[A23J 3/34] using enzymes -- A23J 3/34 covers the use of enzymes in the processing or treatment steps that prepare protein ingredients for food applications, such as hydrolysis, coagulation or modification to improve functionality.
[A23J 3/341] of animal proteins -- A23J 3/341 pertains to the processing of animal proteins specifically for use in foodstuffs, focusing on methods that enhance their functionality or nutritional value. This classification covers techniques such as denaturation, hydrolysis, and other modifications aimed at improving texture, solubility, or digestibility of animal-derived protein sources.
[A23J 3/342] of collagen; of gelatin -- A23J 3/342 concerns the preparation of collagen or gelatin from protein sources for use in food products. It falls under protein compositions for foodstuffs and covers specific working-up steps such as extraction, purification or modification of these proteins.
[A23J 3/343] of dairy proteins -- The class A23J 3/343 relates to methods for processing dairy proteins as part of food preparation. It includes the extraction, purification, modification and other working-up steps that convert milk-derived proteins into forms suitable for incorporation into protein-based foodstuffs.
[A23J 3/344] of casein -- A23J 3/344 pertains to the processing of casein, a protein found in milk, for use in food products. This classification covers methods and techniques specifically aimed at preparing casein for consumption.
[A23J 3/345] of blood proteins -- A23J 3/345 pertains to the processing of blood proteins specifically for use in foodstuffs, focusing on methods that enhance their functionality or safety as ingredients. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with protein compositions and their preparation for human consumption.
[A23J 3/346] of vegetable proteins -- This subclass covers methods for processing vegetable-derived proteins to make them suitable for use in food products, including extraction, purification, modification, and formulation steps. It falls under the broader category of protein compositions for foodstuffs in the CPC.
[A23J 3/347] of proteins from microorganisms or unicellular algae -- This CPC subclass covers methods for obtaining protein isolates from microorganisms or unicellular algae for use in food products. It includes extraction, purification and drying steps for processing microbial or algal proteins, and falls under A23J 3/347 within the working-up of proteins for foodstuffs.
[A23J 3/348] of proteins obtained from waste materials  A23J3/341 ,  A23J3/346  take precedence -- A23J 3/348 deals with the processing of proteins that are sourced from waste materials for use in food products. It falls under the working-up of proteins for foodstuffs within the broader categories of human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, and protein compositions for foodstuffs, and it is subordinate to the more specific A23J 3/341 and A23J 3/346 entries.
[A23J 7/00] Phosphatide compositions for foodstuffs, e.g. lecithin -- A23J 7/00 relates to phosphatide compositions, chiefly lecithin, employed as food additives to enhance emulsification, texture, stability and nutritional value of foodstuffs, including methods of preparing and applying these lipid-based ingredients.
[A23K] FODDER -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeding-stuffs that are specially adapted, processed or formulated for non-human animals.  Processes that are specially adapted for production of such animal feeding-stuffs.  Processes that are specially adapted for preservation of materials (e.g. grass or roots) in order to produce such animal feeding-stuffs.
[A23K 10/00] Animal feeding-stuffs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feedstuffs and processes for production in general.
[A23K 10/10] obtained by microbiological or biochemical processes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-specified microbiological processes for fodder or non-specified addition of microbes in general.
[A23K 10/12] by fermentation of natural products, e.g. of vegetable material, animal waste material or biomass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Use of microorganisms to produce fodder ingredients or fodders (biomass) and the ingredients of fodders per se.
[A23K 10/14] Pretreatment of feeding-stuffs with enzymes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Treating fodder by addition of enzymes (in general and specific) for particular effects.
[A23K 10/16] Addition of microorganisms or extracts thereof, e.g. single-cell proteins, to feeding-stuff compositions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods of adding microbes such as probiotics to fodders and the fodders per se.
[A23K 10/18] of live microorganisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods of adding live microbes such as probiotics to fodders and the fodders per se.
[A23K 10/20] from material of animal origin -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of fodder from animal or waste material in general, and fodders containing same as a significant ingredient.
[A23K 10/22] from fish -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of fodder from fish (including shellfish, crustaceans), and fodders containing same as a significant ingredient.
[A23K 10/24] from blood -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of fodder from blood or blood products and fodder containing these as a significant ingredient.
[A23K 10/26] from waste material, e.g. feathers, bones or skin -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of fodder from animal (including fish, etc.) waste material, and fodders containing same as a significant ingredient.
[A23K 10/28] from waste dairy products -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of fodder from dairy wastes and fodder containing these as a significant ingredient.
[A23K 10/30] from material of plant origin, e.g. roots, seeds or hay -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of fodder from vegetable material in general (including plant extracts) and fodders containing same as a significant ingredient.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Animal feeding-stuffs obtained by microbiological or biochemical processes -> A23K10/10
[A23K 10/32] from hydrolysates of wood or straw -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of fodder from chemical or enzymatic hydrolysates of wood or straw (including lignaceous or cellulosic) material, and fodders containing same as a significant ingredient.
[A23K 10/33] from molasses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of fodder from molasses and fodder containing molasses as a significant ingredient.
[A23K 10/35] from potatoes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of fodder from potatoes (including by-products), and fodders containing same as a significant ingredient.
[A23K 10/37] from waste material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of fodder from vegetable waste material, and fodders containing same as a significant ingredient.
[A23K 10/38] from distillers' or brewers' waste -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of fodder from waste products of brewing and distilling (e.g. solubles) and fodder containing same as a significant ingredient.
[A23K 10/40] Mineral licks, e.g. salt blocks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Block of salt and other minerals for livestock to lick.
[A23K 20/00] Accessory food factors for animal feeding-stuffs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Animal feed or fodder supplemented with additives which may, for example, enhance the nutritional value, digestibility or appeal of the feed, may effect weight-gain of the animal, or the appearance of the flesh or meat of the animal, etc.  Specific additives being classified in the subgroups, e.g. vitamins as fodder additives are classified in A23K20/174 , sugars as fodder additives are classified in A23K20/163 .
[A23K 20/10] Organic substances -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of organic substances of undefined composition, including mixtures thereof.
[A23K 20/105] Aliphatic or alicyclic compounds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of non-aromatic carbon compounds (not including fatty, amino, or heterocyclic, etc. compounds covered by other subclasses).
[A23K 20/111] Aromatic compounds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of non-heterocyclic compounds containing at least one benzene ring.
[A23K 20/116] Heterocyclic compounds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of heterocyclic compounds in general.
[A23K 20/121] containing oxygen or sulfur as hetero atom -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of saturated or unsaturated oxo- or sulfo- heterocycles (e.g. oxirane, oxetane, etc., and thiirane, thietane, etc., compounds).
[A23K 20/126] Lactones -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of lactone type cyclic esters.
[A23K 20/132] containing only one nitrogen as hetero atom -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of saturated or unsaturated single nitrogen containing heterocycles (e.g. aziridine, pyridine, etc.).
[A23K 20/137] containing two hetero atoms, of which at least one is nitrogen -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of saturated or unsaturated heterocycles, with more than one hetero atom and containing at least one nitrogen atom (e.g. oxazepines, thiazepines, pyridazine).
[A23K 20/142] Amino acids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of amino acids (including esters, salts, etc.).
[A23K 20/147] Polymeric derivatives, e.g. peptides or proteins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of peptide and worked up protein material, including proteolyzates.
[A23K 20/153] Nucleic acids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of nucleic acids and fragments (DNA, RNA, etc.).
[A23K 20/158] Fatty acids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of oils, fats, fatty acids, fatty materials including waxes.
[A23K 20/163] Sugars -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of saccharides including simple sugars and starches as well as cellulosic materials.
[A23K 20/168] Steroids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of steroids (e.g. cortisone, prednisolone, etc.) and derivatives.
[A23K 20/174] Vitamins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Animal feed or fodder supplemented with additives which may, for example, enhance the nutritional value , digestibility or appeal of the feed, may effect weight-gain of the animal, or the appearance of the flesh or meat of the animal, etc.
[A23K 20/179] Colouring agents, e.g. pigmenting or dyeing agents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of colouring agent to the fodder resulting in, for example, brightly coloured food, e.g. to attract birds, or resulting in enhanced colour of the flesh or meat, e.g. pink colouring to salmon flesh.
[A23K 20/184] Hormones -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of hormones (e.g., growth hormone, oxtocin, etc.) and derivatives.
[A23K 20/189] Enzymes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of enzymes (in general and specific).
[A23K 20/195] Antibiotics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of antibiotics per se (not including probiotics - see A23K10/16 ).
[A23K 20/20] Inorganic substances, e.g. oligoelements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of inorganic materials, e.g. metals, salts, gases, etc.
[A23K 20/22] Compounds of alkali metals -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of group 1 elements (Li, Na, K, etc.).
[A23K 20/24] Compounds of alkaline earth metals, e.g. magnesium -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of group 2 elements (Be, Mg, Ca, etc.).
[A23K 20/26] Compounds containing phosphorus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of organic and inorganic P compounds (e.g. pyrophosphates, etc.).
[A23K 20/28] Silicates, e.g. perlites, zeolites or bentonites -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of Si compounds (including earths, clays, etc.).
[A23K 20/30] Oligoelements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Addition of trace elements (e.g. Fe, Cu, Mn, Zn, Se, I., etc.).
[A23K 30/00] Processes specially adapted for preservation of materials in order to produce animal feeding-stuffs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feedstuffs characterized by preservation method and the feedstuffs per se.
[A23K 30/10] of green fodder -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feedstuffs characterized by preservation of green material (grasses, lucerne, etc.) and the feedstuffs per se.
[A23K 30/12] Dehydration -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feedstuffs characterized by dehydration preservation of green material (grasses, lucerne, etc.) and the feedstuffs per se.
[A23K 30/15] using chemicals or microorganisms for ensilaging -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feedstuffs characterized by addition of chemicals (or biochemicals) to produce silage and the silage per se.
[A23K 30/18] using microorganisms or enzymes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feedstuffs characterized by addition of enzymes and/or microbes to produce silage and the silage per se.
[A23K 30/20] Dehydration -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feedstuffs characterized by dehydration preservation and the feedstuffs per se.
[A23K 40/00] Shaping or working-up of animal feeding-stuffs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for producing fodders not covered in subgroups, as well as fodders resulting from processes.
[A23K 40/10] by agglomeration -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for producing fodders by agglomeration, granulation, powdering, as well as granular, powdered fodders resulting from processes.
[A23K 40/20] by moulding, e.g. making cakes or briquettes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for producing fodders by molding, pressing; as molded, compressed or pressed fodders resulting from such processes.
[A23K 40/25] by extrusion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for producing fodders by extrusions as well as extruded fodders resulting from such processes.
[A23K 40/30] by encapsulating -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[A23K 40/35] Making capsules specially adapted for ruminants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for making coatings designed to bypass the rumen and products per se.
[A23K 50/00] Feeding-stuffs specially adapted for particular animals -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeds adapted for specific animals in general.
[A23K 50/10] for ruminants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeds for ruminant animals (e.g. cows, goats, sheep, etc.).
[A23K 50/15] containing substances which are metabolically converted to proteins, e.g. ammonium salts or urea -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeds for ruminants which contain nitrogenous protein precursors.
[A23K 50/20] for horses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeds for equine animals (e.g. horses, donkeys, zebra).
[A23K 50/30] for swines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeds for suidae (e.g. pig, hog, boar, etc.).
[A23K 50/40] for carnivorous animals, e.g. cats or dogs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeds for carnivorous animals in general (canines, felines, mink, etc.).
[A23K 50/42] Dry feed -- **Definition:** This place covers:  0-15% H 2 O.
[A23K 50/45] Semi-moist feed -- **Definition:** This place covers:  15-50% H 2 O.
[A23K 50/48] Moist feed -- **Definition:** This place covers:  50-100% H 2 O.
[A23K 50/50] for rodents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeds for rodents (e.g. mice, rats, hamsters, etc.).
[A23K 50/60] for weanlings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeds for young animals in general.
[A23K 50/70] for birds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeds for avian animals in general (e.g. pigeons, wild birds, etc.).
[A23K 50/75] for poultry -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeds for domesticated farmed avians (e.g. chicken, turkey, geese, duck, etc.).
[A23K 50/80] for aquatic animals, e.g. fish, crustaceans or molluscs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeds used in aquaculture in general (including shellfish and marine mammals).
[A23K 50/90] for insects, e.g. bees or silkworms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeds for insects (e.g. honeybees, flies, etc.).
[A23L] FOODS, FOODSTUFFS OR NON-ALCOHOLIC BEVERAGES, NOT OTHERWISE PROVIDED FOR -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This subclass is residual to food, foodstuff and non-alcoholic beverages covered by subclasses A21D or A23B - A23J .  Examples of foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages covered by this subclass:  Preparation or treatment of the above foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages provided for in this subclass, including:  Modification of such foods or beverages to change their nutritional content for dietary reasons and these modified foods or beverages per se.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preservation of foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages -> A23B  **Glossary:** - food or foodstuffs: in addition to the commonly accepted meaning of food or foodstuffs, the term in this subclass also extends to cover less traditional items such as thickening agents (e.g. starch), flavouring agents, artificial sweeteners or food colouring agents
[A23L 11/00] Pulses, i.e. fruits of leguminous plants, for production of food -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preservation of pulses; Preservation of products from legumes -> A23B75/00  **Glossary:** - legume: plant in the family Fabaceae or Leguminosae - soy bean or soya bean: the bean of Glycine max
[A23L 11/01] Pulses or legumes in the form of whole pieces or fragments thereof, without mashing or comminuting  A23L11/10  –  A23L11/70  take precedence -- A23L 11/01 pertains to pulses or legumes that are kept as whole pieces or fragments, without being mashed or finely ground, under the Cooperative Patent Classification by EPO/USPTO. This entry is part of a broader category dealing with foods and foodstuffs not otherwise specified, focusing specifically on fruits from leguminous plants used for food production.
[A23L 11/03] Soya beans, e.g. full-fat soya bean flakes or grits -- A23L 11/03 covers soybeans used as food, including full-fat soybean flakes or grits, and relates to the processing or application of these pulses in edible products. It falls under the hierarchy HUMAN NECESSITIES → FOODS OR FOODSTUFFS → FOODS, FOODSTUFFS OR NON-ALCOHOLIC BEVERAGES, NOT OTHERWISE PROVIDED FOR → Pulses, i.e. fruits of leguminous plants, for production of food.
[A23L 11/05] Mashed or comminuted pulses or legumes; Products made therefrom  A23L11/30  takes precedence; tofu or soya cheese  A23L11/45 ; soy drinks  A23L11/65 -- A23L 11/05 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers mashed or comminuted pulses and legumes, as well as products made from them, excluding tofu, soya cheese, and soy drinks which are classified under different codes.
[A23L 11/07] Soya beans, e.g. oil-extracted soya bean flakes  A23L11/30  takes precedence -- This subclass covers soybeans intended for food, specifically oil-extracted soy bean flakes used as a processed pulse. It is placed under A23L 11/07 within the broader group for pulses and is superseded by the more specific A23L 11/30 entry.
[A23L 11/10] Rapid cooking pulses -- A23L 11/10 covers inventions that enable rapid cooking of pulses, such as beans, lentils or peas, by using specialized methods, equipment or pre-treatment processes that shorten the time needed to make them edible. It includes technologies for heating, pressure, microwave or enzymatic treatments designed specifically for leguminous food products.
[A23L 11/30] Removing undesirable substances, e.g. bitter substances -- A23L 11/30 covers methods for eliminating unwanted components, such as bitter taste compounds, from pulses that are used as food. The subclass relates to food processing techniques aimed at improving the sensory quality of leguminous fruits before consumption.
[A23L 11/31] by heating without chemical treatment, e.g. steam treatment, cooking -- This subclass A23L 11/31 covers methods for preparing pulses-legume fruits such as beans, lentils or peas-by applying heat without any chemical treatment, for example steam treatment or cooking, to render them suitable for food use. It falls under the hierarchy of human necessities, foods, and specifically pulses for food production.
[A23L 11/32] by extraction with solvents -- A23L 11/32 covers processes for obtaining food from pulses-legume fruits such as beans, peas or lentils-by extracting their constituents with solvents. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, specifically the subclass dealing with non-alcoholic food products derived from pulses.
[A23L 11/33] using enzymes; Enzymatic transformation of pulses or legumes -- The A23L 11/33 classification covers food-processing methods that employ enzymes to modify pulses or other leguminous plant fruits, such as beans, peas, lentils, or chickpeas. It includes enzymatic treatments for improving texture, flavor, nutritional value, or functional properties of these plant foods.
[A23L 11/34] using chemical treatment, adsorption or absorption -- A23L 11/34 covers methods for treating pulses-fruits of leguminous plants used as food-through chemical means, including adsorption or absorption techniques, to modify their properties or improve safety and quality during food production.
[A23L 11/35] combined with heat treatment -- A23L 11/35 covers methods of treating pulses-legume fruits used as food-by applying heat. This includes cooking, blanching, sterilising, roasting or other thermal processes that prepare the pulses for consumption or further processing. The subclass groups technologies that combine heat treatment with other steps such as mixing, shaping or drying.
[A23L 11/36] using irradiation, e.g. with wave energy; using electrical means or magnetic fields -- A23L 11/36 relates to processes applied to pulses-legume fruits used as food-where irradiation (including wave energy) or electrical and magnetic fields are employed to treat, preserve, sterilise or modify the material. This subclass groups methods that use such energy forms to affect the physical or biochemical properties of the pulses.
[A23L 11/37] using microorganisms -- A23L 11/37 covers the use of microorganisms in the processing or improvement of pulses-fruits of leguminous plants used as food. It includes microbial fermentation, enzymatic treatment, or inoculation to enhance nutritional value, texture, flavor, or preservation of beans, peas, lentils and similar crops.
[A23L 11/40] Pulse curds -- A23L 11/40 pertains to curdled food products made from pulses, the edible seeds of leguminous plants such as beans, peas, lentils or soy, where the pulse protein is coagulated to form a cheese-like curd.
[A23L 11/45] Soy bean curds, e.g. tofu -- A23L 11/45 covers soy bean curds such as tofu, including their composition, processing methods and treatment steps used to produce these pulse-based foods. It is a narrow subclass within the pulse food preparations hierarchy of the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[A23L 11/50] Fermented pulses or legumes -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Removing undesirable substances -> A23L11/30 - Soy sauce -> A23L27/50
[A23L 11/60] Drinks from legumes, e.g. lupine drinks -- A23L 11/60 relates to beverages produced from leguminous plants, such as lupine drinks, covering the preparation, formulation and processing of legume-based drinks within the food and non-alcoholic beverage category.
[A23L 11/65] Soy drinks -- A23L 11/65 refers to soy-based beverages, covering the preparation, composition and processing of soy drinks as a non-alcoholic foodstuff derived from leguminous pulses.
[A23L 11/70] Germinated pulse products, e.g. from soy bean sprouts -- A23L 11/70 covers food products derived from germinated pulses, including items such as soybean sprouts and other sprouted legumes ready for consumption. It
[A23L 13/00] Meat products -- A23L 13/00 designates the class of meat products, covering the preparation, processing and preservation of meat and meat-based foods.
[A23L 13/03] Coating with a layer; Stuffing, laminating, binding, or compressing of original meat pieces -- A23L 13/03 covers techniques for applying a layer to meat pieces or for stuffing, laminating, binding, or compressing original meat pieces to form composite products such as sausages, terrines, and restructured meats, including processes that create a coating, fill cavities, or fuse meat layers without adding non-meat fillers.
[A23L 13/06] with gravy or sauce -- A23L 13/06 covers meat products that are prepared, presented or served with a gravy or sauce, including cooked cuts, processed slices or composite meats accompanied by liquid or semi-liquid flavorings. The subclass groups dishes where the sauce is an integral part of the product, such as roasts, stews or pre-packaged meat items that include gravy in the packaging.
[A23L 13/10] Meat meal or powder -- This subclass covers meat meal or powder, meaning finely ground or pulverized meat products prepared as dry foods or feed, and includes the processes for producing those dry forms.
[A23L 13/20] from offal, e.g. rinds, skins, marrow, tripes, feet, ears or snouts -- A23L 13/20 classifies methods and apparatus for handling off-cuts of animal carcasses-such as rinds, skins, marrow, tripe, feet, ears or snouts-when producing meat products. It covers the preparation, processing, and utilization of these offal parts within the broader category of foods and foodstuffs.
[A23L 13/30] Meat extracts -- A23L 13/30 covers the preparation of meat extracts and related products, such as concentrated meat juices, broths, stocks and dried or powdered forms used as flavorings or nutritional supplements. It includes the processes for extracting, concentrating, preserving and formulating these meat-derived substances.
[A23L 13/40] containing additives -- A23L 13/40 pertains to meat products that incorporate additives such as preservatives, flavorings, curing agents or other substances used to alter taste, texture, shelf-life or nutritional properties, and it is situated within the CPC hierarchy for foods, specifically under meat products not otherwise provided for.
[A23L 13/42] Additives other than enzymes or microorganisms in meat products or meat meals -- A23L 13/42 deals with the incorporation of non-enzyme, non-microbial additives into processed meat products or meat meals, covering substances such as salts, phosphates, antioxidants, flavorings, binders and other chemical agents that affect preservation, texture, taste or nutritional qualities.
[A23L 13/422] Addition of natural plant hydrocolloids, e.g. gums of cellulose derivatives or of microbial fermentation gums -- This CPC subclass concerns the incorporation of natural plant hydrocolloids, including cellulose-derived gums and microbial fermentation gums, into meat products to modify texture, stability or shelf life. It falls under the meat-products group within the foods section of Human Necessities.
[A23L 13/424] Addition of non-meat animal protein material, e.g. blood, egg, dairy products, fish; Proteins from microorganisms, yeasts or fungi -- A23L 13/424 covers meat products in which non-meat animal proteins-such as blood, egg, dairy, or fish extracts-or proteins obtained from microorganisms, yeasts or fungi are incorporated to modify composition, flavor, texture or nutritional value. This subclass is part of the CPC hierarchy for foods and foodstuffs, specifically meat items enriched with additional protein sources.
[A23L 13/426] Addition of proteins, carbohydrates or fibrous material from vegetable origin other than sugars or sugar alcohols  A23L13/422  takes precedence -- A23L 13/426 covers meat product formulations in which vegetable-derived proteins, carbohydrates or fibrous ingredients-excluding sugars and sugar alcohols-are incorporated to improve texture, flavor or nutritional value. It is a sub-class of A23L 13/422, which takes precedence in the hierarchy.
[A23L 13/428] Addition of flavours, spices, colours, amino acids or their salts, peptides, vitamins, yeast extract or autolysate, nucleic acid or derivatives, organic acidifying agents or their salts or acidogens, sweeteners, e.g. sugars or sugar alcohols; Addition of alcohol-containing products -- A23L 13/428 pertains to methods for adding flavorings, spices, colors, amino acids or their salts, peptides, vitamins, yeast extracts or autolysates, nucleic acids or derivatives, organic acidifying agents or their salts, sweeteners such as sugars or sugar alcohols, and alcohol-containing products to meat products.
[A23L 13/43] Addition of vegetable fats or oils; Addition of non-meat animal fats or oils; Addition of fatty acids -- A23L 13/43 covers methods for incorporating vegetable or non-meat animal fats, oils, and fatty acids into meat products. It includes techniques such as mixing, emulsifying or otherwise treating meat to enhance texture, flavor, or stability by adding these lipid sources.
[A23L 13/432] Addition of inorganic compounds, e.g. minerals; oligo-elements -- A23L 13/432 covers processes for adding inorganic compounds, such as minerals and trace elements, to meat products. It relates to improving composition, preservation or nutritional value by incorporating these additives during meat processing.
[A23L 13/45] Addition of, or treatment with, microorganisms  A23L13/74  takes precedence -- A23L 13/45 concerns methods of adding or treating meat products with microorganisms, such as inoculation, fermentation, ripening or bioprotective applications to alter flavor, safety or shelf life. It is situated in the CPC hierarchy under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Meat Products and is subordinate to the broader subgroup A23L 13/74, which takes precedence.
[A23L 13/46] Addition of, or fermentation with fungi, e.g. yeasts; Enrichment with dried biomass other than starter cultures -- A23L 13/46 covers methods for treating meat products by adding or fermenting them with fungi such as yeasts, and for enriching the products with dried fungal biomass that is not used as a starter culture.
[A23L 13/48] Addition of, or treatment with, enzymes  A23L13/74  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass covers methods for adding or treating meat products with enzymes, such as tenderising, flavour development or preservation. It falls under the broader categories of human necessities, foods and meat products. The more specific subclass A23L13/74 supersedes it.
[A23L 13/50] Poultry products, e.g. poultry sausages -- The code A23L 13/50 covers inventions related to processed poultry items such as chicken or turkey sausages, pâtés, and similar meat preparations. It includes methods of manufacturing, seasoning, forming, and packaging these products, as well as the resulting poultry-based food compositions. This sub-class falls under the broader categories of human necessities, foods, and specifically meat products.
[A23L 13/52] Comminuted, emulsified or processed products; Pastes; Reformed or compressed products from poultry meat -- A23L 13/52 covers methods and products that involve comminuting, emulsifying, or otherwise processing poultry meat into pastes, reformed forms, or compressed shapes, such as chicken pâtés, meat spreads, and formed loaf-type items. The classification addresses the preparation techniques, additives, and texture-modifying steps used to create these processed poultry foods.
[A23L 13/55] Treatment of original pieces or parts  binding meat pieces in a compact form  A23L13/52 -- A23L 13/55 relates to processes that treat original meat pieces or parts by binding them into a compact form, such as formed, restructured or compacted meat products. It falls under the human necessities hierarchy, specifically foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages, and more narrowly meat products, covering methods of shaping or consolidating raw meat material.
[A23L 13/57] Coating with a layer or stuffing -- A23L 13/57 covers methods for preparing meat products by applying a coating layer or by stuffing the meat, including breading, battering, marinating, or inserting a filler material. The classification pertains to processes that add an outer layer or interior filling to meat to modify texture, flavor, or preservation.
[A23L 13/60] Comminuted or emulsified meat products, e.g. sausages -- A23L 13/60 classifies inventions relating to comminuted or emulsified meat products such as sausages, where meat is finely divided, mixed with other ingredients and formed into a cohesive mass. The code falls under the CPC hierarchy for human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, and specifically meat products.
[A23L 13/62] Coating with a layer, stuffing or laminating -- A23L 13/62 covers processes for applying a coating, adding a layer, stuffing or laminating meat products, such as sausages or cured meats, to improve texture, flavor, preservation or appearance. It falls within the CPC hierarchy under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Meat products.
[A23L 13/65] Sausages -- The CPC subclass A23L 13/65 pertains to sausages, covering the preparation, composition, and processing of meat products formed into a cohesive encased product, including cooking, drying, smoking, and ingredient variations.
[A23L 13/67] Reformed meat products other than sausages -- A23L 13/67, within the CPC hierarchy under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Meat products, pertains to reformed meat products that are not sausages, such as ham, pâté, meat spreads, and other mechanically or chemically treated meat preparations.
[A23L 13/70] Tenderised or flavoured meat pieces -- A23L 13/70 covers meat pieces that have been mechanically tenderised or have flavorings added, including marinated, seasoned, brined, or otherwise treated cuts such as steaks, fillets or minced portions. The classification applies to processes, additives, and products where the meat's texture or taste is modified before cooking or consumption. It falls under the broader categories of foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages, specifically meat products.
[A23L 13/72] using additives, e.g. by injection of solutions -- A23L 13/72 covers methods for treating meat products by adding substances, such as injecting solutions containing additives, to improve flavor, texture, preservation, or nutritional value. The classification pertains to the processing stage where the additives are introduced directly into the meat.
[A23L 13/74] using microorganisms or enzymes -- A23L 13/74 covers the use of microorganisms or enzymes in meat products, such as starter cultures, ripening agents, or enzyme treatments that modify flavor, texture, preservation, or nutritional properties. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, and non-alcoholic food and beverage categories dealing specifically with meat.
[A23L 13/75] using macerating or marinating solutions, e.g. marinades containing spices, acids, condiments or flavouring agents -- A23L 13/75 covers methods for treating meat with macerating or marinating solutions, including the use of spices, acids, condiments or flavoring agents to enhance flavor, tenderness or preservation. It falls under the food classification for meat products.
[A23L 13/76] by treatment in a gaseous atmosphere, e.g. ageing or ripening -- A23L 13/76 pertains to the processing of meat products through treatment in a gaseous atmosphere, such as for aging or ripening, enhancing flavor and texture through controlled environmental conditions.
[A23L 13/77] by mechanical treatment, e.g. kneading, rubbing or tumbling -- A23L 13/77 covers meat products that are processed by mechanical means such as kneading, rubbing or tumbling. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Foods, Foodstuffs or Non-Alcoholic Beverages, not otherwise provided for → Meat products, specifying the mechanical treatment steps applied to the meat.
[A23L 15/00] Egg products -- Group A23L15/00 is impacted by reclassification into group A23L15/10 . Groups A23L15/00 and A23L15/10 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[A23L 15/10] Egg products with specific shapes or structures, e.g. rolled -- Group A23L15/10 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group A23L15/00 . Groups A23L15/00 and A23L15/10 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[A23L 15/20] Addition of proteins, e.g. hydrolysates, fats, carbohydrates, natural plant hydrocolloids; Addition of animal or vegetable substances containing proteins, fats, or carbohydrates -- A23L 15/20 covers processes for enriching egg products with added proteins such as hydrolysates, as well as fats, carbohydrates, natural plant hydrocolloids and other animal or vegetable substances that contain these nutrients, improving texture, nutritional value or functional properties.
[A23L 15/25] Addition or treatment with microorganisms or enzymes -- A23L 15/25 covers processes that add or treat egg products with microorganisms or enzymes, including fermentation, enzymatic conversion, or probiotic incorporation to modify flavor, texture, nutritional value or shelf-life. It falls under the CPC group for egg-based foods.
[A23L 15/30] Addition of substances other than those covered by  A23L15/20  –  A23L15/25 -- A23L 15/30 covers methods for incorporating additives into egg products that are not addressed in the preceding sub-groups A23L 15/20 to A23L 15/25, such as flavorings, stabilisers, preservatives or nutritional enhancers, applied during processing or formulation of the egg-based food.
[A23L 15/35] Egg substitutes -- A23L 15/35 covers egg substitutes, products that mimic or replace eggs in food preparations, including liquid, powdered or solid formulations derived from plant or other non-egg sources. The code is part of the CPC hierarchy for human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, and specifically egg products.
[A23L 17/00] Food-from-the-sea products -- A23L 17/00 relates to food-from-the-sea products, encompassing the preparation, preservation, treatment and packaging of fish, crustaceans, mollusks, seaweed and other marine organisms intended for human consumption. It falls under the CPC section for human necessities, specifically foods and non-alcoholic beverages.
[A23L 17/10] Fish meal or powder -- A23L 17/10 pertains to fish meal or powder, which are dried, ground or otherwise processed fish products used chiefly as protein-rich ingredients in animal feed, pet food, or as a nutritional additive. The classification covers their composition, preparation methods, and related handling techniques.
[A23L 17/20] Fish extracts -- A23L 17/20 covers fish extracts, including broths, stocks, soups and other liquid preparations obtained from fish or fish parts for use as food or ingredients. It encompasses the processing steps, compositional variants and uses of these sea-derived extracts within the broader category of foods and non-alcoholic beverages.
[A23L 17/30] Fish eggs, e.g. caviar -- A23L 17/30 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers fish eggs, including caviar, detailing their processing and preservation methods to ensure quality and safety for consumption.
[A23L 17/35] Fish-egg substitutes -- A23L 17/35 relates to food-from-the-sea products that serve as substitutes for fish eggs, covering the composition, preparation and processing of such alternatives. It falls within the CPC hierarchy under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Foods, Foodstuffs or Non-Alcoholic Beverages, Not Otherwise Provided For → Food-from-the-Sea Products.
[A23L 17/40] Shell-fish -- A23L 17/40 covers inventions concerning shell-fish, including methods for catching, handling, preserving, processing, cooking or otherwise preparing them for consumption, as well as apparatus, containers, packaging and additives specifically used with shell-fish.
[A23L 17/50] Molluscs -- A23L 17/50 covers the processing, preparation and preservation of edible molluscs such as clams, oysters, mussels and scallops. It includes methods for cleaning, cooking, canning, freezing or otherwise treating these marine foods for consumer use. This code falls under the broader group for food-from-the-sea products.
[A23L 17/60] Edible seaweed -- A23L 17/60 is a CPC entry covering edible seaweed, classified under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Foods, Foodstuffs or Non-Alcoholic Beverages, Not Otherwise Provided for → Food-from-the-Sea products. It includes the harvesting, processing and use of marine algae as food items.
[A23L 17/65] Addition of, or treatment with, microorganisms or enzymes -- This CPC subclass deals with the addition of, or treatment with, microorganisms or enzymes to seafood products, covering processes such as fermentation, enzymatic tenderising, ripening and other biotechnological modifications of fish, crustaceans, molluscs and other marine foodstuffs.
[A23L 17/70] Comminuted, e.g. emulsified, fish products; Processed products therefrom such as pastes, reformed or compressed products  A23L17/65 ,  A23L17/10 ,  A23L17/20 ,  A23L17/40 ,  A23L17/50  take precedence -- A23L 17/70 relates to comminuted fish products, such as emulsified pastes and reformed or compressed foods derived from fish, and to the processes for preparing these items; it builds on the preceding subclasses A23L17/65, A23L17/10, A23L17/20, A23L17/40 and A23L17/50.
[A23L 17/75] Coating with a layer, stuffing, laminating, binding or compressing of original fish pieces -- A23L 17/75 covers processes that apply a coating, stuffing, laminating, binding or compression to whole fish pieces, such as fillets or steaks, to form ready-to-eat or value-added seafood products. The classification includes methods that create a protective layer, embed additional ingredients, or reshape fish meat without altering its basic structure.
[A23L 19/00] Products from fruits or vegetables -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Marmalades, jams, jellies or the like -> A23L21/10 - Treating harvested fruit or vegetables in bulk -> A23N
[A23L 19/01] Instant products; Powders; Flakes; Granules  A23L19/10  –  A23L19/12  take precedence -- A23L 19/01 covers instant food products derived from fruits or vegetables, specifically powders, flakes and granules that are prepared for quick reconstitution. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Foods, Foodstuffs or Non-Alcoholic Beverages, Not Otherwise Provided For → Products from fruits or vegetables.
[A23L 19/03] consisting of whole pieces or fragments without mashing the original pieces -- A23L 19/03 covers food products derived from fruits or vegetables that are presented as whole pieces or fragments, without being mashed or pureed. The entry applies to items such as sliced, diced, or chopped fruit and vegetable preparations that retain the original shape.
[A23L 19/05] Stuffed or cored products; Multilayered or coated products; Binding or compressing of original pieces -- A23L 19/05 relates to food products derived from fruits or vegetables that are stuffed, cored, multilayered, coated or bound together, including processes that compress original pieces into composite forms. It covers techniques for filling, layering, coating and binding whole or cut fruit or vegetable pieces to create combined or enhanced food items.
[A23L 19/07] Fruit waste products, e.g. from citrus peel or seeds -- A23L 19/07 covers waste materials obtained from fruit processing, such as citrus peels, seeds, pulp and other by-products, and their use in further applications like extraction of oils, flavors, nutraceuticals, animal feed or compost. It falls under HUMAN NECESSITIES > FOODS OR FOODSTUFFS > FOODS, FOODSTUFFS OR NON-ALCOHOLIC BEVERAGES, NOT OTHERWISE PROVIDED FOR > Products from fruits or vegetables.
[A23L 19/09] Mashed or comminuted products, e.g. pulp, purée, sauce, or products made therefrom, e.g. snacks  A23L19/01 ,  A23L19/10 ,  A23L27/63  and  A23L21/00  take precedence -- A23L 19/09 relates to mashed or comminuted food products derived from fruits or vegetables, such as pulp, purées, sauces and snack items made from them. The classification is subordinated to the more specific sub-groups A23L 19/01, A23L 19/10, A23L 27/63 and A23L 21/00, which take precedence.
[A23L 19/10] of tuberous or like starch containing root crops -- A23L 19/10 covers food products derived from tuberous or similar starch-rich root crops such as potatoes, sweet potatoes, yams and other underground storage organs. It includes processed or prepared items made primarily from these starch-containing vegetables, fitting within the broader class for foods and foodstuffs made from fruits or vegetables.
[A23L 19/105] Sweet potatoes -- A23L 19/105 covers sweet potatoes, the edible tubers of Ipomoea batatas used as a vegetable food product. The code is placed in the CPC hierarchy under human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, and specifically products from fruits or vegetables.
[A23L 19/11] Cassava, manioc, tapioca, or fermented products thereof, e.g. gari -- A23L 19/11 designates food products made from the tuber cassava (manioc), including raw and processed forms such as tapioca and fermented derivatives like gari. The classification covers the preparation, treatment and utilisation of these cassava-based items in food manufacturing and related applications.
[A23L 19/115] Konjak; Konntaku -- A23L 19/115 covers food products made from the konjac (Amorphophallus konjac) plant, including the tuber, flour, jelly and other edible preparations derived from this vegetable. The code is applied to items such as konnyaku or konjac-based foods used as low-calorie gelling agents or meat substitutes.
[A23L 19/12] of potatoes -- A23L 19/12 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers products made from potatoes, detailing specific processing methods and formulations for potato-based foods. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office to organize patent applications related to food technology.
[A23L 19/13] Mashed potato products -- A23L 19/13 relates to mashed potato products, including prepared or instant mashed potatoes, dried, frozen or otherwise processed forms that consist mainly of potatoes reduced to a mash for ready-to-eat or reconstituted consumption. It covers the composition, preparation methods and packaged forms of such potato-based mash foods.
[A23L 19/135] Preformed edible shells filled with mashed potatoes; Stuffed mashed potato products -- A23L 19/135 covers pre-formed edible shells that are filled with mashed potatoes, including any ready-to-cook or ready-to-eat stuffed mashed-potato products. It classifies food items where a potato-based filling is enclosed in a shell, typically for convenient preparation or consumption.
[A23L 19/14] Original non-roasted or non-fried potato pieces -- A23L 19/14 covers original potato pieces that have not been roasted or fried, such as raw sliced or diced potatoes intended for further processing or cooking. It falls under the CPC group for products from fruits or vegetables within the foods and foodstuffs subclass.
[A23L 19/15] Unshaped dry products, e.g. powders, flakes, granules or agglomerates -- A23L 19/15 covers unshaped dry food products derived from fruits or vegetables - such as powders, flakes, granules or agglomerates - that are not formed into a defined shape.
[A23L 19/18] Roasted or fried products, e.g. snacks or chips -- A23L 19/18 pertains to roasted or fried edible products such as snack foods and chips made from fruits or vegetables. The subclass covers the preparation, shaping, seasoning and preservation of these items, including methods for achieving crispness and flavor in the final product.
[A23L 19/19] from powdered or mashed potato products  A23L19/135  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass A23L 19/19 covers food preparations made from powdered or mashed potato
[A23L 19/20] by pickling, e.g. sauerkraut or pickles -- The A23L 19/20 subclass covers processes and products obtained by pickling fruits or vegetables, including the manufacture of sauerkraut, pickles and similar fermented foods. It is positioned within the CPC group for foods derived from fruits or vegetables under the human necessities section.
[A23L 2/00] Non-alcoholic beverages -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-alcoholic beverages:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Soup concentrates -> A23L23/10 - Preparation of non-alcoholic beverages by removal of alcohol -> C12H3/00
[A23L 2/02] containing fruit or vegetable juices -- This subclass covers non-alcoholic drinks that contain fruit or vegetable juices, including their composition, preparation and packaging. It is part of the CPC group A23L for non-alcoholic beverages.
[A23L 2/04] Extraction of juices -- A23L 2/04 covers methods for extracting juice from fruit, vegetables, or other edible raw materials, including mechanical, enzymatic, or solvent processes, and the related equipment used in those operations.
[A23L 2/06] from citrus fruits -- A23L 2/06 classifies non-alcoholic beverages that are prepared from citrus fruits, such as juices, soft drinks and flavored waters. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Foods, Foodstuffs or Non-Alcoholic Beverages, Not Otherwise Provided for → Non-Alcoholic Beverages.
[A23L 2/08] Concentrating or drying of juices -- A23L 2/08 covers methods and apparatuses for concentrating or drying fruit and vegetable juices, including evaporation, freeze-drying, spray-drying and other techniques that remove water to produce a more stable, transportable product. It addresses both laboratory-scale and industrial processes aimed at preserving flavor, nutrients and shelf life while reducing volume. This subclass falls under non-alcoholic beverages within the broader foods and foodstuffs category.
[A23L 2/082] by membrane processes -- A23L 2/082 covers non-alcoholic beverage production or treatment steps that employ membrane technologies such as filtration, dialysis, ultrafiltration, nanofiltration or reverse osmosis. It includes using membranes to separate, concentrate, purify or modify liquids like water, juice, milk or soft drinks, typically to remove solids, microorganisms or unwanted solutes.
[A23L 2/085] by osmosis, reverse osmosis, electrodialysis -- A23L 2/085 covers non-alcoholic beverages that are treated by membrane separation methods such as osmosis, reverse osmosis or electrodialysis, focusing on processes used to concentrate, purify or modify the drink's composition. This subclass falls under foods and foodstuffs, specifically non-alcoholic beverages, in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[A23L 2/087] by ultrafiltration, microfiltration -- The CPC subclass A23L 2/087 covers non-alcoholic beverages that are processed by ultrafiltration or microfiltration, such as juices, milk-based drinks or other water-based refreshments. It falls under the hierarchy of Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Foods, Foodstuffs or Non-Alcoholic Beverages, Not Otherwise Provided For, specifying these membrane-filtration techniques.
[A23L 2/10] by heating or contact with dry gases -- A23L 2/10 covers processes for making non-alcoholic beverages in which the drink or its components are heated or exposed to dry gases such as air, nitrogen or carbon dioxide. The treatment may be used to dry, sterilise, roast or otherwise modify the product before or during final preparation.
[A23L 21/00] Marmalades, jams, jellies or the like -- This subclass covers the preparation, composition and preservation of fruit-based spreads such as marmalades, jams and jellies, including related formulations and processing methods. It falls under the foodstuffs group A23L dealing with foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages not otherwise provided for.
[A23L 2/102] Spray-drying -- A23L 2/102 relates to spray-drying techniques used to convert liquid non-alcoholic beverages into powdered form. The subclass addresses the equipment, parameters and methods for atomising the liquid and drying the droplets, enabling stable, transportable beverage powders.
[A23L 2/105] Foam-drying -- Foam-drying is a process for removing water from non-alcoholic beverages by turning the liquid into a stable foam and then drying the foam, typically with hot air or vacuum, to produce a porous powder that reconstitutes quickly. It is classified under CPC A23L 2/105 within the non-alcoholic beverage subgroup of foods and foodstuffs.
[A23L 2/107] Electric or wave heating -- A23L 2/107 covers heating techniques for non-alcoholic beverages that rely on electric or electromagnetic wave energy, such as microwave, induction or resistive heating. It applies to equipment and processes used to prepare, keep warm, or serve drinks without using conventional fire-based heating.
[A23L 21/10] Marmalades -- A23L 21/10 designates the preparation and composition of marmalades, jams, jellies and similar fruit spreads. It belongs to the CPC group for foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages, specifically the sub-category covering marmalades, jams, jellies or the like.
[A23L 21/11] obtained by enzymatic digestion of fruit or vegetable compositions -- A23L 21/11 covers processes that produce marmalades, jams, jellies or similar spreads by enzymatically digesting fruit or vegetable mixtures, using enzymes to break down pectins and other polysaccharides to achieve the desired texture and sweetness. This subclass falls under the broader categories of human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, and non-alcoholic beverages not otherwise provided for.

[A23L 27/11] obtained by solvent extraction -- A23L 27/11 covers spices that are produced through solvent-extraction processes, where organic or inorganic solvents are used to dissolve and isolate flavor compounds from plant material. It applies to the preparation of spice extracts, powders or concentrates obtained by this method, distinguishing them from spices obtained by mechanical, fermentation or other extraction techniques.

[A23L 27/201] Compounds of unspecified constitution characterised by the chemical reaction for their preparation  A23L27/215  takes precedence -- A23L 27/201 covers spices that contain compounds of unspecified structure, defined primarily by the chemical reaction used to prepare them, rather than by a known molecular formula. This subclass sits under foods and foodstuffs, specifically spices, and is overridden by the more specific A23L 27/215.
[A23L 27/202] Aliphatic compounds -- A23L 27/202 pertains to aliphatic compounds used in spices, such as fatty acids, aldehydes, ketones and related derivatives that provide flavor or aroma. [A23L 27/2022] containing sulfur -- A23L 27/2022 relates to spices that include sulfur-containing compounds, encompassing the preparation, processing or use of such spices in food products. [A23L 27/2024] having oxygen as the only hetero atom -- A23L 27/2024 defines a subgroup of spice compounds whose molecular structure contains only oxygen as the hetero atom, excluding nitrogen, sulfur or other hetero elements. It covers flavoring substances such as certain essential-oil constituents that meet this restriction.
[A23L 27/2026] Hydroxy compounds -- A23L 27/2026 covers hydroxy compounds used in spices, such as phenolic acids, alcohols and related derivatives, for purposes including flavor enhancement, preservation or color modification. [A23L 27/205] Heterocyclic compounds -- A23L 27/205 covers heterocyclic chemical compounds that are employed as flavoring agents or additives in spices and related foodstuffs. The subclass is part of the CPC hierarchy under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Spices, and it includes any heterocyclic substances used to impart taste, aroma, or preservative effects in non-alcoholic food products.
[A23L 27/2052] having oxygen or sulfur as the only hetero atoms -- A23L 27/2052 classifies spices whose chemical composition includes only oxygen or sulfur as hetero atoms, excluding compounds that contain nitrogen, halogens or other hetero elements. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, specifically the spices subgroup. This entry is used to identify and catalogue such oxygen- or sulfur-based spice substances in patent documents.

[A23L 27/31] containing amino acids, nucleotides, peptides or derivatives -- A23L 27/31 covers spices, seasonings or other food additives whose composition includes amino acids, nucleotides, peptides or their derivatives.
