[A23L 27/33] containing sugars or derivatives -- A23L 27/33 covers spice mixtures or preparations that incorporate sugars or sugar derivatives, such as sucrose, glucose, or related compounds, used as carriers, sweeteners, or functional additives to modify taste, stability or handling of the spice product. This subclass falls under the broader categories of foodstuffs and spices within the CPC system.
[A23L 27/34] Sugar alcohols -- Sugar alcohols are polyol compounds derived from sugars that provide sweet taste with reduced caloric value, commonly used as low-calorie sweeteners and bulking agents in food products. They are chemically similar to sugars but differ in that the aldehyde or ketone group is reduced to a hydroxyl, giving them a sugar-like structure while being only partially metabolized. Typical examples include sorbitol, mannitol, xylitol, and erythritol, which serve as sugar substitutes in confectionery, chewing gum, and diet foods.
[A23L 27/35] Starch hydrolysates -- A23L 27/35 refers to starch hydrolysates, which are products derived from the hydrolysis of starch into sugars and related oligomers. These substances are used as sweeteners, food additives, animal feed components, or industrial intermediates and fall under the CPC hierarchy of Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Foods, Foodstuffs or Non-Alcoholic Beverages, Not Otherwise Provided for → Spices.
[A23L 27/36] Terpene glycosides -- A23L 27/36 covers terpene glycosides, which are glycosylated terpene compounds used in food and spice applications as flavor enhancers, scent modifiers or functional ingredients. The classification includes processes for extracting, synthesizing, or formulating these natural or modified molecules in edible products.
[A23L 27/37] Halogenated sugars -- A23L 27/37 relates to halogenated sugars employed as spice ingredients. It covers sugar molecules in which one or more hydrogen atoms are replaced by a halogen (such as chlorine, bromine or fluorine), used to modify flavor, aroma or preservative properties in food preparations.
[A23L 27/38] L-sugars -- A23L 27/38 relates to the use of L-sugars as spice ingredients, covering their preparation, formulation and application to enhance flavor, preserve, or modify the properties of foodstuffs. It includes natural or synthetic optically active monosaccharides and their derivatives employed in seasoning blends.
[A23L 27/39] Addition of sweetness inhibitors -- A23L 27/39 covers the use of agents that suppress or mask sweetness in spice preparations, allowing manufacturers to adjust flavor balance without adding sugar. It includes formulations and processes for incorporating sweetness inhibitors into seasonings, blends or other food products.
[A23L 2/74] using membranes, e.g. osmosis, ultrafiltration -- A23L 2/74 relates to the preparation or treatment of non-alcoholic beverages by means of membrane processes such as osmosis, ultrafiltration, nanofiltration or reverse-osmosis. It covers methods that separate, concentrate, filter or modify liquids using selective permeable barriers.
[A23L 27/40] Table salts -- A23L 27/40 covers table salts, the edible sodium chloride used as a seasoning or preservative in food. It includes processes for producing, refining, granulating, fortifying or packaging salt for culinary use.
[A23L 27/45] Salt substitutes completely devoid of sodium chloride -- The A23L 27/45 subclass covers salt substitutes that contain no sodium chloride, focusing on alternative seasoning compounds or mixtures used in place of common table salt. It includes formulations, preparation methods and uses of these completely non-NaCl salt replacements.
[A23L 27/50] Soya sauce -- The A23L 27/50 entry covers soy sauce, a fermented condiment produced from soybeans, wheat, salt and water and used to season foods. It pertains to the preparation, composition and processing of this sauce, which falls under the spices subgroup of foodstuffs in the CPC hierarchy.
[A23L 2/76] by removal of gases -- A23L 2/76 covers methods and equipment for producing non-alcoholic drinks in which gases are removed, such as degassing or de-carbonation processes that eliminate dissolved carbon dioxide or other gases from the beverage. It includes techniques for extracting, venting or otherwise reducing gas content to achieve the desired product characteristics.
[A23L 27/60] Salad dressings -- The A23L 27/60 class covers inventions related to salad dressings, including their composition, preparation methods and stabilisation of oil-water emulsions. It includes formulations such as vinaigrettes, creamy dressings and flavored sauces, as well as processes for mixing, preserving and packaging these food products.

[A23L 27/66] Use of milk products or milk derivatives in the preparation of dressings -- A23L 27/66 concerns the incorporation of milk, cream, cheese or other dairy derivatives into dressings and similar food preparations. [A23L 27/70] Fixation, conservation, or encapsulation of flavouring agents -- A23L 27/70 covers methods for fixing, preserving or encapsulating flavouring agents in spices, including techniques that protect volatile or sensitive compounds during processing, storage or cooking. The subclass addresses both natural and added flavours, aiming to retain aroma and taste in spice products.
[A23L 27/72] Encapsulation -- A23L 27/72 concerns the encapsulation of spices, covering processes and compositions that surround spice particles with a protective coating to preserve flavor, mask odor, improve stability or control release during food preparation and storage. The classification applies to any technique that encloses spices in a carrier material for use in foods or non-alcoholic beverages.
[A23L 27/74] with a synthetic polymer matrix or excipient, e.g. vinylic, acrylic polymers -- A23L 27/74 relates to spices that are incorporated into or coated with a synthetic polymer matrix or excipient, such as vinylic or acrylic polymers, to improve handling, stability or controlled release in food applications.
[A23L 27/75] the flavouring agents being bound to a host by chemical, electrical or like forces, e.g. use of precursors -- This subclass relates to methods and compositions in which flavoring agents are attached to a host or carrier by chemical bonds, electrostatic forces or similar interactions, for example through the use of precursor compounds that generate the flavor in situ.
[A23L 27/77] Use of inorganic solid carriers, e.g. silica -- A23L 27/77 relates to the use of inorganic solid carriers, such as silica, as carriers or encapsulating agents for spices. It addresses methods of applying these carriers to improve the handling, stability, dispersibility or controlled release of spice ingredients in food products.
[A23L 27/79] in the form of films -- A23L 27/79 relates to spices that are prepared as thin edible films, such as flavoring sheets or coating layers that can be applied to foods. It covers the methods for forming, drying and using these film forms to deliver spice aroma or taste.
[A23L 2/78] by ion-exchange -- A23L 2/78 covers the preparation of non-alcoholic beverages that use ion-exchange processes, such as removing or adding ions to adjust mineral content, flavor, or stability of the drink. The subclass includes methods employing resin or membrane ion-exchangers to treat water or beverage bases before or during production.

[A23L 27/82] Acid flavourants -- A23L 27/82 covers acid flavourants used in spices, referring to substances that impart sour or acidic taste characteristics to seasonings. The class includes both natural and synthetic acids applied to spice blends or individual spice products to modify flavor profile, and it encompasses methods of incorporation and typical concentration ranges.
[A23L 27/84] Flavour masking or reducing agents -- A23L 27/84 covers substances or compositions that are added to spices or other food items to mask or reduce undesirable flavours such as bitterness, off-tastes or pungency. It includes natural or synthetic flavour-masking agents, encapsulated forms and the methods of applying them to food products.
[A23L 27/86] Addition of bitterness inhibitors -- A23L 27/86 covers the use of bitterness-inhibiting agents in food products, particularly spices, to mask or reduce unpleasant bitter flavours. It includes methods of adding such inhibitors during processing or formulation, and the specific compounds employed for this purpose. This subclass falls under the broader categories of foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages.
[A23L 27/88] Taste or flavour enhancing agents -- A23L 27/88 covers substances added to spices to modify or intensify their sensory properties, including natural extracts, synthetic compounds and mixtures that enhance taste or aroma. The subclass includes flavor potentiators, taste modifiers, and aroma boosters used in food preparation, processing or seasoning to improve the perceived quality of the final product.
[A23L 2/80] by adsorption -- A23L 2/80 pertains to the preparation or treatment of non-alcoholic beverages in which an adsorbent is employed to remove, recover or modify components such as flavours, aromas, pigments or impurities. The subclass covers adsorption-based processes for clarifying, de-colorising, deodorising or enriching drinks.
[A23L 2/82] by flocculation -- A23L 2/82 covers the clarification of non-alcoholic drinks by flocculation, where added agents cause suspended particles to aggregate into larger flocs that settle or can be removed. The methods apply to juices, soft drinks and other water-based beverages to improve clarity and stability.
[A23L 2/84] using microorganisms or biological material, e.g. enzymes -- A23L 2/84 pertains to the preparation of non-alcoholic beverages by employing microorganisms or biological material such as enzymes, covering processes that use these biological agents to modify or produce the drink.

[A23L 5/00] Preparation or treatment of foods or foodstuffs, in general -- **Glossary:** - food or foodstuffs: in addition to the commonly accepted meaning of foods or foodstuffs, the term in this subclass also extends to cover less traditional items such as thickening agents (e.g. starch), flavouring agents, artificial sweeteners or food colouring agents - functional food/dietetic food/nutraceutical: food composition, formulation or supplement having an alleged effect on health or prevention of diseases - essential oil: concentrated, hydrophobic liquid containing volatile aromatic compounds from plants - nutritive value: the nutritive value represents the energetic or calorific content of nutrition
[A23L 5/10] General methods of cooking foods, e.g. by roasting or frying -- A23L 5/10 covers general cooking techniques applied to foods, such as roasting, frying, grilling and other heat-based methods used to prepare a wide range of edible products. [A23L 5/17] in a gaseous atmosphere with forced air or gas circulation, in vacuum or under pressure -- A23L 5/17 pertains to the preparation or treatment of foods or foodstuffs by processes involving a specific atmospheric condition, such as using forced air or gas circulation, or treating under vacuum or pressure. [A23L 5/20] Removal of unwanted matter, e.g. deodorisation or detoxification -- A23L 5/20 concerns techniques and equipment for eliminating unwanted substances from foods or non-alcoholic drinks, including deodorisation, detoxification and other purification steps performed during preparation or treatment.

It falls under the CPC subgroup for general food preparation techniques.
[A23L 5/23] by extraction with solvents -- A23L 5/23 covers food or foodstuff preparation steps that involve extracting components using solvents, such as removing flavors, colors, fats, or bioactive substances from raw or processed material. It falls under the broader category of general food preparation and treatment methods, focusing specifically on solvent-based extraction techniques.
[A23L 5/25] using enzymes -- A23L 5/25 concerns the use of enzymes in the preparation or treatment of foods and foodstuffs. It covers enzymatic processes such as hydrolysis, fermentation, ripening, tenderising, flavor development or nutrient enhancement applied to raw or processed products. This entry falls under the hierarchy of Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → General preparation or treatment of foods.
[A23L 5/27] by chemical treatment, by adsorption or by absorption -- A23L 5/27 pertains to the chemical treatment of foods or foodstuffs by adsorption or absorption, including the use of catalysts, sorbents, binders or other agents to modify, preserve, or enhance the product during preparation or processing. It falls within the broader category of general food preparation and treatment methods.
[A23L 5/273] using adsorption or absorption agents, resins, synthetic polymers, or ion exchangers -- A23L 5/273 covers food and beverage preparation methods that employ adsorption or absorption agents such as resins, synthetic polymers, or ion-exchange materials to modify, purify, or stabilize products. The classification includes processes that use these agents to remove contaminants, adjust composition, or enhance shelf-life.
[A23L 5/276] Treatment with inorganic compounds  A23L5/273  takes precedence -- This subclass covers methods of treating foods or foodstuffs using inorganic compounds such as acids, bases, salts or
[A23L 5/28] using microorganisms -- A23L 5/28 covers the use of microorganisms in the preparation or processing of foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages. It includes methods where bacteria, yeasts, molds or other microbes are employed to ferment, preserve, flavor, enhance nutritional value or otherwise treat food products.
[A23L 5/30] Physical treatment, e.g. electrical or magnetic means, wave energy or irradiation -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cooking foods, e.g. by roasting or frying -> A23L5/10
[A23L 5/32] using phonon wave energy, e.g. sound or ultrasonic waves -- A23L 5/32 pertains to the preparation or treatment of foods or foodstuffs using phonon wave energy, such as sound or ultrasonic waves, for processes like mixing, emulsifying, or enhancing food properties.
[A23L 5/34] using microwaves -- A23L 5/34 concerns food-preparation or treatment methods that use microwave energy, including heating, cooking, drying or sterilising of foodstuffs. It is a sub-group of A23L, which covers general processes for preparing or treating foods and non-alcoholic beverages.
[A23L 5/36] using irradiation with frequencies of more than 10 MHz -- A23L 5/36 relates to the application of high-frequency irradiation, specifically electromagnetic waves above 10 MHz, in the processing or treatment of foodstuffs. This includes using radio-frequency or microwave energy to achieve preservation, microbial reduction, texture modification, or other improvements during food preparation.
[A23L 5/40] Colouring or decolouring of foods -- A23L 5/40 covers the methods and substances used to add or remove color in foods, including the application of natural or synthetic dyes, bleaching agents, and other coloring or de-coloring treatments, as part of general food preparation or processing.
[A23L 5/41] Retaining or modifying natural colour by use of additives, e.g. optical brighteners -- A23L 5/41 pertains to processes for preserving or altering the natural colour of foods and foodstuffs by adding colour-affecting substances such as optical brighteners. It covers the use of additives that maintain or modify visual appearance during preparation, storage or treatment.
[A23L 5/42] Addition of dyes or pigments, e.g. in combination with optical brighteners -- This CPC subclass covers processes for incorporating colourants such as dyes, pigments or optical brighteners into foods or foodstuffs during preparation or treatment, including methods of mixing, applying or fixing these additives to achieve desired visual properties in the final product.
[A23L 5/43] using naturally occurring organic dyes or pigments, their artificial duplicates or their derivatives -- A23L 5/43 relates to the use of naturally occurring organic dyes or pigments, their artificial duplicates or derivatives, in the preparation or treatment of foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages. This subclass falls under the broader categories of human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, and general food preparation methods.
[A23L 5/44] using carotenoids or xanthophylls -- A23L 5/44 covers the use of carotenoids or xanthophylls as natural colourants, antioxidants, or nutritional enhancers in the preparation or treatment of foods and foodstuffs. It includes methods for incorporating these pigments into products such as sauces, dairy, bakery items, and beverages to improve appearance, stability, or health value.
[A23L 5/46] using dyes or pigments of microbial or algal origin -- A23L 5/46 covers the use of dyes or pigments derived from microorganisms or algae in the preparation or treatment of foods and foodstuffs, including their incorporation as colourants or functional additives.
[A23L 5/47] using synthetic organic dyes or pigments not covered by groups -- A23L 5/47 concerns the use of synthetic organic dyes or pigments that are not specified in any other subgroup during the preparation or treatment of foods and foodstuffs. It covers applications of such colourants in processes aimed at modifying appearance, without addressing nutritional or preservative functions.
[A23L 5/48] Compounds of unspecified constitution characterised by the chemical process for their preparation -- A23L 5/48 falls within the CPC class for food preparation and treatment and covers food-related compounds whose identity is defined by the chemical process used to make them rather than by a specific molecular structure. It includes substances of unspecified constitution that are characterized solely by their method of synthesis, typically used as additives, preservatives or functional ingredients in foods and non-alcoholic beverages.
[A23L 5/49] Removing colour by chemical reaction, e.g. bleaching -- A23L 5/49 relates to processes that eliminate colour from foods or foodstuffs by chemical reaction, such as bleaching. It falls within the CPC group for preparation or treatment of foods, under the broader categories of human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, and non-alcoholic beverages.
[A23L 5/51] Concentration -- A23L 5/51 covers concentration processes used in the preparation or treatment of foods and foodstuffs, such as evaporation, ultrafiltration, or other techniques that increase the solid or soluble component content of a product.
[A23L 5/55] Rehydration or dissolving of foodstuffs -- A23L 5/55 covers methods and apparatus for rehydrating or dissolving foodstuffs, including the addition of water or other liquids to dry or concentrated products to restore their original state. It falls within the broader class for preparation or treatment of foods, specifically addressing the step of converting dehydrated or powdered foods back into consumable forms.
[A23L 5/57] Chemical peeling or cleaning of harvested fruits, vegetables or other foodstuffs  mechanical aspect  A23N ,  A23P -- A23L 5/57 covers the mechanical aspects of chemically peeling or cleaning harvested fruits, vegetables and other foodstuffs, detailing equipment and processes that remove skins, residues or contaminants after harvest. It falls within the broader A23L class for food preparation and is related to the A23N and A23P subclasses dealing with post-harvest treatment.
[A23L 7/00] Cereal-derived products -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preparation of malt for brewing -> C12C  **Glossary:** - Meal: The edible whole or coarsely ground grains of a cereal grass - Farinaceous: Made from, rich in, or consisting of starch, having a mealy or powdery texture - Snack: Food products with low water content and a long shelf life - Semi-finished or partly finished products: Food products that need an extra step, e.g. frying in oil, before being ready-to-eat
[A23L 7/10] Cereal-derived products -- A23L 7/10 covers food products obtained from cereals such as wheat, rice, maize, oats and their derivatives, including flours, breads, breakfast cereals, pasta and snack items. The code is used for inventions concerning processing, shaping, cooking, preserving or treating these cereal-derived foods.
[A23L 7/101] Addition of antibiotics, vitamins, amino-acids, or minerals -- A23L 7/101 relates to cereal-derived food products in which antibiotics, vitamins, amino-acids, or minerals are added as additives, covering the formulation and processing steps that incorporate these nutritional or preservative substances into the final product.
[A23L 7/104] Fermentation of farinaceous cereal or cereal material -- A23L 7/104 covers the fermentation of farinaceous cereals or cereal-derived materials, describing processes that convert wheat, rice, corn, rye, oats, millet and similar grains into fermented foods such as breads, sourdoughs, soy-free yogurt, tempeh, alcoholic drinks or other fermented products. It relates to food-processing techniques within the broader class of cereal-derived foods.
[A23L 7/107] Addition or treatment with enzymes not combined with fermentation with microorganisms -- A23L 7/107 pertains to cereal-derived food products that are improved or processed by adding enzymes without any simultaneous microbial fermentation. It covers enzyme-treated doughs, breads, pasta, breakfast cereals and similar items where the enzymatic action is used alone to modify texture, flavor, nutritional value or shelf life.
[A23L 7/109] Types of pasta, e.g. macaroni or noodles -- A23L 7/109 pertains to cereal-derived food products and specifically covers various types of pasta, including macaroni, noodles and similar shapes, whether dried or fresh. It falls under the hierarchy of human necessities, foods or foodstuffs, and more precisely foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages not otherwise provided for.
[A23L 7/11] Filled, stuffed or multilayered pasta -- A23L 7/11 concerns pasta that is filled, stuffed or formed in multiple layers, such as ravioli, tortellini, cannelloni and layered lasagna sheets. The subclass includes the cereal-based dough, the filling materials and the processes for making these multi-component pasta items, and is used for patents on formulations, manufacturing methods and applications of
[A23L 7/111] Semi-moist pasta, i.e. containing about 20% of moist; Moist packaged or frozen pasta; Pasta fried or pre-fried in a non-aqueous frying medium, e.g. oil; Packaged pasta to be cooked directly in the package -- A23L 7/111 relates to semi-moist pasta products containing about 20 % moisture, encompassing moist packaged or frozen pasta, pasta that has been fried or pre-fried in a non-aqueous medium such as oil, and pasta designed to be cooked directly in its package.
[A23L 7/113] Parboiled or instant pasta -- A23L 7/113 covers cereal-derived products specifically relating to parboiled or instant pasta, including the preparation processes and the resulting ready-to-cook or quick-cooking pasta items. It falls within the foodstuffs category of the Cooperative Patent Classification, under human necessities and non-alcoholic foods.

[A23L 7/117] Flakes or other shapes of ready-to-eat type -- A23L 7/117 refers to ready-to-eat cereal products that are formed into flakes or other shaped pieces, such as puffed, extruded or formed grains, intended for immediate consumption without further cooking. It covers the manufacturing methods that give these cereals their characteristic shape and texture.
[A23L 7/122] Coated, filled, multilayered or hollow ready-to-eat cereals -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ready-to-eat cereals that are coated, filled, multilayered or hollow, e.g. a cheese snack comprising a cereal-based core and a cheese coating.
[A23L 7/126] Snacks or the like obtained by binding, shaping or compacting together cereal grains or cereal pieces, e.g. cereal bars -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cereals grains or pieces bonded, shaped or compacted to form snacks, e.g. cereal bars with or without fruits, baked snack comprising fibrous cereals, apple.
[A23L 7/13] Snacks or the like obtained by oil frying of a formed cereal dough -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Formed cereal dough fried oil to make snacks, e.g. corn-based snack made by frying a corkscrew-shaped food piece, crisp-fried tortilla chips.
[A23L 7/135] Individual or non-extruded flakes, granules or shapes having similar size, e.g. breakfast cereals -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Flakes, granules in individual form, loose blends of individual pieces not agglomerated, e.g. cereal-based flakes easily dispersed in a cold liquid.
[A23L 7/139] made from wholegrain or grain pieces without preparation of meal or dough -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Individual flakes made from wholegrain or grains pieces, e.g. cereal grains.
[A23L 7/143] Cereal granules or flakes to be cooked and eaten hot, e.g. oatmeal -- A23L 7/143 covers cereal granules or flakes that are designed to be cooked and eaten hot, such as oatmeal. It belongs to the CPC hierarchy for human necessities, specifically foods and foodstuffs derived from cereals.

[A23L 7/152] Cereal germ products -- A23L 7/152 covers food products made from the germ of cereal grains, such as wheat, rye, barley, oats or rice. These include wheat germ flour, toasted germ, germ oil and other preparations that use the nutrient-rich germ component of cereals for nutritional or functional purposes.

[A23L 7/161] Puffed cereals, e.g. popcorn or puffed rice -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Puffed cereals, e.g. "natural" release of pressure from the cereals under heat.
[A23L 7/165] Preparation of puffed cereals involving preparation of meal or dough as an intermediate step -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Puffed cereals preparation comprising a step comprising meal or dough making before the final step for obtaining the aerated cereals.
[A23L 7/17] by extrusion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Puffed cereals prepared with a step involving meal or dough making followed by a step of extrusion.
[A23L 7/174] Preparation of puffed cereals from wholegrain or grain pieces without preparation of meal or dough -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Puffed cereals prepared from whole grain or grain pieces with no step of meal or dough making.
[A23L 7/178] by pressure release with or without heating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Puffed cereals prepared from whole grain or grain pieces by pressure release, i.e. external pressure, e.g. mechanical, being applied and released, e.g. puffed-food starch material snack prepared from starch material grain such as corn and expansion of the food starch in a puffing chamber.
[A23L 7/183] by heating without using a pressure release device -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Puffed cereals prepared from whole grain or grain pieces by heating without the use of external pressurizing device, e.g. process for the preparation of expanded millets comprising the step of treating dried polished grains at high temperature in order to obtain the puffed millets.
[A23L 7/187] Discontinuously-working apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Puffed cereals prepared from whole grain or grain pieces by heating without pressure release in an apparatus working intermittently, e.g. domestic apparatus for making pop-corn.  Industrial processes, i.e. continuous processes for making popcorn are not classified in this group.
[A23L 7/191] After-treatment of puffed cereals, e.g. coating or salting -- A23L 7/191 relates to post-processing treatments applied to puffed cereals, including the application of edible coatings, flavoring agents, or salt. The provision of such treatments aims to enhance the product's taste, texture, appearance, or shelf-life.
[A23L 7/196] Products in which the original granular shape is maintained, e.g. parboiled rice -- A23L 7/196 covers cereal-derived foods that retain their original granular shape after processing, such as parboiled rice and similar grains that are treated without losing their form.
[A23L 7/1963] coated with a layer -- A23L 7/1963 covers cereal-derived food items that are provided with a coating layer, such as a film, glaze, or edible shell, applied to improve texture, protect against moisture, or add flavor or nutritional additives. The coating may be applied before or after cooking or baking.
[A23L 7/1965] Cooked; Precooked; Fried or pre-fried in a non-aqueous liquid frying medium, e.g. oil -- A23L 7/1965 covers cereal-derived products that are cooked, precooked, fried or pre-fried using a non-aqueous liquid frying medium such as oil. It includes methods and apparatus for preparing these items by heat treatment in oil rather than water.
[A23L 7/197] Treatment of whole grains not provided for in groups  A23L7/117  -  A23L7/196   preservation  A23B9/00 -- A23L 7/197 covers methods for treating whole grains that are not covered in earlier groups A23L 7/117 to A23L 7/196, such as cleaning, drying, heating, cooling or other preservation techniques applied to intact cereal kernels.
[A23L 7/1975] Cooking or roasting -- A23L 7/1975 covers methods and apparatus for cooking or roasting cereal-derived foods such as grains, flours, breads and similar products. It includes processes like baking, toasting, drying or heating that convert raw cereal material into edible forms, and patents for ovens, roasters and related control systems.
[A23L 7/198] Dry unshaped finely divided cereal products, not provided for in groups  A23L7/117  -  A23L7/196  and  A23L29/00 , e.g. meal, flour, powder, dried cereal creams or extracts -- A23L 7/198 covers dry, unshaped, finely divided cereal products that are not classified elsewhere, such as meals, flours, powders, dried cereal creams and extracts. These materials are intended as food ingredients or for further processing.
[A23L 7/20] Malt products -- A23L 7/20 covers malt products, i.e., foodstuffs obtained by germinating cereal grains such as barley and processing them into malted flours, syrups, extracts, or other derived items. It falls under CPC section A, class A23L for cereal-derived products within the broader human necessities hierarchy.
[A23L 7/25] Fermentation of cereal malt or of cereal by malting -- A23L 7/25 relates to the fermentation of cereal malt or cereals that have been malted, covering methods that convert these grains into fermented products such as breads, beers, or other food and beverage items derived from malted cereals. It is part of the CPC hierarchy for cereal-derived products within human necessities.
[A23L 9/00] Puddings -- A23L 9/00 covers puddings, including their preparation, composition and packaging, as a subcategory of foods and non-alcoholic beverages within the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[A23L 9/10] Puddings -- A23L 9/10 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers the preparation and composition of puddings, a category of foods that includes various sweet or savory dishes typically made with milk or cream and thickened with ingredients like flour, cornstarch, or eggs.
[A23L 9/12] Ready-to-eat liquid or semi-liquid desserts, e.g. puddings, not to be mixed with liquids, e.g. water, milk -- A23L 9/12 covers ready-to-eat liquid or semi-liquid desserts such as puddings that are sold in a consumable form and do not require mixing with additional liquids like water or milk before eating. It pertains to products that are formulated, packaged and marketed as complete, single-serve desserts.
[A23L 9/20] Cream substitutes -- A23L 9/20 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers cream substitutes used in foods or foodstuffs, specifically focusing on alternatives that mimic the properties and functions of traditional dairy cream. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with puddings and similar dessert items.
[A23L 9/22] containing non-milk fats but no proteins other than milk proteins -- A23L 9/22 covers puddings in which the fat component comes from non-milk sources while the only proteins present are milk proteins, such as custards or gelatin desserts that use vegetable or animal fats but rely solely on milk-derived proteins for structure and texture. This subclass falls under foods, specifically puddings, in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[A23L 9/24] containing non-milk fats and non-milk proteins, e.g. eggs or soybeans -- A23L 9/24 relates to puddings that are formulated with non-milk fats and non-milk proteins, such as those derived from eggs, soybeans or similar plant sources, rather than dairy ingredients.
[A23N] MACHINES OR APPARATUS FOR TREATING HARVESTED FRUIT, VEGETABLES OR FLOWER BULBS IN BULK, NOT OTHERWISE PROVIDED FOR -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for post-harvest processing of fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs in bulk, or on an industrial scale ( A23N1/00 - A23N15/00 ); Apparatus for preparing fodder for feeding animals ( A23N17/00 )  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Agricultural machinery, such as harvesters -> A01D - Household devices -> A47J - Treatment of grain -> B02B - Processing sugar containing produce -> C13B
[A23N 1/00] Machines or apparatus for extracting juice -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Extraction of juice (processes) -> A23L2/04 - Household devices for extracting juice -> A47J19/00 - Production of sugar juices -> C13B10/00
[A23N 1/003] especially for citrus fruits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines or apparatus for extracting juice in bulk, or on an industrial scale, dedicated for citrus fruit  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Extraction processes of juice from citrus fruit -> A23L2/06 - Household devices -> A47J19/00
[A23N 1/006] by electroplasmolysis  preserving of fruit or vegetables by electroplasmolytic treatment  A23B7/015 -- A23N 1/006 pertains to the extraction of juice from fruits or vegetables using machines that employ electroplasmolysis, a method also used for preserving produce through electroplasmolytic treatment. This technique involves applying an electric field to alter cell membranes, facilitating juice extraction and preservation.
[A23N 1/02] combined with disintegrating or cutting -- A23N 1/02 covers machines or apparatus that extract juice while also performing a disintegration or cutting step on bulk harvested fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs. It falls under the human necessities group, specifically foods or foodstuffs, and relates to equipment for treating harvested produce in bulk that is not otherwise specified.
[A23N 11/00] Removing pith from stems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for removing pith from stems  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Coring of fruit or vegetables -> A23N4/12  **Glossary:** - Pith: The soft internal tissue of a plant part; esp. a central cylinder of parenchyma in a stem or root
[A23N 12/00] Machines for cleaning, blanching, drying or roasting fruits or vegetables, e.g. coffee, cocoa, nuts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines for cleaning, blanching, drying or roasting fruits or vegetables, e.g. coffee, cocoa, nuts in bulk, or on an industrial scale
[A23N 12/005] for dry-cleaning -- A23N 12/005 pertains to machines or apparatus for bulk dry-cleaning harvested fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs, employing methods such as air flow, brushes or vibration to remove dirt and residues without the use of water.
[A23N 12/02] for washing or blanching -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines for washing or blanching fruits or vegetables on an industrial scale; with or without washing aids, such as brushes etc.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combined with subsequent drying -> A23N12/06 - Domestic devices for washing vegetables or the like -> A47J43/24 - Washing grain -> B02B1/04
[A23N 12/023] for washing potatoes, apples or similarly shaped vegetables or fruit  washing grain  B02B1/04 -- A23N 12/023 pertains to machines designed for washing potatoes, apples, and similarly shaped fruits or vegetables, as well as washing grain, with a specific reference to the mechanical aspect of cleaning processes.
[A23N 12/026] Stone-gatherers or cleaning devices for the washing machines -- A23N 12/026 pertains to specialized equipment designed to remove stones and clean fruits or vegetables during processing. This classification covers mechanisms integrated into washing systems that ensure the removal of unwanted debris, enhancing product quality and safety.
[A23N 12/04] for blanching -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines for blanching (exclusively)  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Blanching methods as such -> A23B7/06  **Glossary:** - Blanching: short term heating for inactivating enzymes, removing gas inclusions, etc.
[A23N 12/06] for washing or blanching, combined with subsequent drying -- A23N 12/06 is a CPC subclass for machines that wash or blanch harvested fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs and then dry them in a single integrated process. It falls under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Machines for treating harvested produce in bulk.
[A23N 12/08] for drying or roasting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines for drying or roasting of fruit and vegetables in bulk, or on an industrial scale  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Machines for washing or blanching, combined with subsequent drying -> A23N12/06 - Beverage-making apparatus with incorporated grinding or roasting means for coffee -> A47J31/42 - Coffee mills combined with roasting devices -> A47J42/52
[A23N 12/083] with stirring, vibrating or grinding devices -- A23N 12/083 covers machines for bulk treatment of harvested fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs that use stirring, vibrating or grinding devices, such as equipment that cleans, blanches, dries or roasts the material while it is agitated or ground.
[A23N 12/086] with centrifuging devices -- A23N 12/086 covers bulk processing machines for fruits, vegetables or flower bulbs that incorporate a centrifuge, used to separate water or other liquids during cleaning, blanching, drying or roasting operations such as coffee, cocoa or nuts.
[A23N 12/10] Rotary roasters -- Rotary roasters are machines arranged so that harvested fruit, vegetables, nuts or similar bulk foodstuffs are continuously tumbled within a heated chamber, allowing even roasting by convection and direct contact with hot surfaces. The rotating motion promotes uniform heat distribution and prevents scorching, making them suitable for processing coffee beans, cocoa beans, peanuts and other items that require controlled roasting in large quantities.
[A23N 12/12] Auxiliary devices for roasting machines -- A23N 12/12 concerns auxiliary devices that assist or improve bulk roasting machines for fruit, vegetables, coffee, cocoa, nuts and similar products, including feeders, agitators, temperature regulators and other supporting mechanisms. It falls under the CPC section for apparatus treating harvested produce.
[A23N 12/125] Accessories or details -- A23N 12/125 covers accessories or details for bulk-processing machines that treat harvested fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs, such as equipment used for cleaning, blanching, drying or roasting items like coffee, cocoa and nuts. These components are intended to be added to or replace parts of the primary apparatus to improve its operation.
[A23N 15/00] Machines or apparatus for other treatment of fruits or vegetables for human purposes -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Machines for processing fruit or vegetables, as defined therein -> A23N12/00 - Machines for treatment of cocoa -> A23G1/02
[A23N 15/001] for trimming artichokes -- Machines or apparatus designed to trim artichokes in bulk as part of treating harvested vegetables for human use. They remove the outer leaves and prepare the edible portions for subsequent processing or consumption.
[A23N 15/003] for trimming cauliflowers, broccoli or cabbages -- A23N 15/003 relates to machines or apparatus used to trim cauliflower, broccoli or cabbage, typically in bulk processing operations. The equipment removes outer leaves and excess material to produce uniformly sized heads suitable for human consumption.
[A23N 15/005] for pricking peas or beans -- A23N 15/005 covers machines or apparatus designed to prick peas or beans, used in bulk post-harvest treatment of harvested legumes. These devices facilitate subsequent processing such as drying, cleaning or further handling.
[A23N 15/01] for stripping Brussels' sprouts from stalks -- A23N 15/01 covers machines or apparatus specifically designed to strip Brussels sprouts from their stalks, falling within the broader group for other treatments of harvested fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs in bulk. This subclass is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification for devices used in processing vegetables for human consumption.
[A23N 15/02] for stemming, piercing, or stripping fruit -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines for stemming, piercing, or stripping fruit in bulk, or on an industrial scale; Removing sprouts of potatoes  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Vintaging machines -> A01D46/28 - Household appliances for removing stalks -> A47J21/00
[A23N 15/025] for stemming grapes -- A23N 15/025 covers machines or apparatus designed to remove stems from harvested grapes, a specific operation within bulk processing of fruit for human consumption. The device typically grips grape clusters and strips or cuts away stems to prepare the fruit for further handling, such as sorting, packaging or winemaking. It falls under the broader class for equipment treating harvested fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs in bulk.
[A23N 15/04] Devices for topping fruit or vegetables -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for topping fruit or vegetables in bulk, or on an industrial scale  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Machines for topping onions -> A23N15/08
[A23N 15/045] Devices for topping mushrooms -- A23N 15/045 covers machines and apparatus that remove or trim the caps of harvested mushrooms, a process known as topping, to prepare the fruiting bodies for further handling or sale. These devices are used in bulk post-harvest treatment of mushrooms as part of food-processing equipment.

[A23N 15/08] Devices for topping or skinning onions or flower bulbs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for topping or skinning onions or flower bulbs in bulk, or on an industrial scale
[A23N 15/10] for shelling peas or beans -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for shelling peas or beans  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Apparatus for shelling peanuts -> A23N5/01
[A23N 15/12] for snipping or stringing beans -- A23N 15/12 covers machines or apparatus designed to snip or string beans during bulk processing of harvested beans, facilitating handling and preparation for market or further processing. It belongs to the subclass for other treatments of fruits or vegetables in the CPC hierarchy.

This classification falls under machines or apparatus for treating harvested fruit, vegetables, or flower bulbs in bulk, not otherwise provided for, and is part of the broader category of foods or foodstuffs within human necessities.

[A23N 2015/006] Singularising cherries or other clustered fruit and vegetables -- This CPC subgroup concerns apparatus and methods for separating individual cherries or other fruit and vegetable clusters into single items. It includes mechanical or manual techniques that detach the produce without damage, facilitating subsequent handling, packaging or processing.
[A23N 2015/008] Sorting of fruit and vegetables -- A23N 2015/008 covers the sorting of fruit and vegetables, including machines and methods that separate produce by size, shape, colour, ripeness or defect. The classification applies to automatic, semi-automatic and manual sorting equipment used after harvest to prepare items for packaging or market distribution. It is part of the CPC subgroup dealing with post-harvest handling of horticultural products.
[A23N 3/00] Machines for coring or stoning fruit, characterised by their feeding device -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines for coring or stoning fruit in bulk, or on an industrial scale, characterised by their feeding device  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  (source: Fig. 4 of EP0056790 A1)  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Machines for coring or stoning characterised by their stoning or seed removing device -> A23N4/00 - Kitchen devices for stoning fruit -> A47J25/00
[A23N 3/02] with feeder-drums -- A23N 3/02 classifies machines for coring or stoning bulk-harvested fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs that employ feeder drums as the primary feeding device, situated within the foods-or-foodstuffs subgroup of Human Necessities.
[A23N 3/04] with endless feeder-belts -- A23N 3/04 covers machines for coring or stoning fruit that employ continuous, endless feeder-belts to transport the produce through the apparatus.
[A23N 3/06] with feeder-tables -- A23N 3/06 pertains to machines designed for the specific task of coring or stoning fruits, featuring a feeder-table mechanism that facilitates the efficient processing of fruit in bulk. This classification is part of the broader category addressing machinery for treating harvested produce not covered elsewhere.

[A23N 4/08] for dates, olives or the like oblong fruits -- It covers equipment designed to pit or de-seed oblong fruits such as dates and olives in bulk, using specialized stoning or seed-removal mechanisms.
[A23N 4/085] for olives -- A23N 4/085 covers machines designed to stone or remove seed-containing sections from harvested fruit, specifically adapted for processing olives. The classification includes apparatuses whose main function is the extraction of pits or cores from olives in bulk.
[A23N 4/10] for fruit with very small pips, e.g. raisins -- A23N 4/10 covers machines for treating harvested fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs in bulk that are designed to stone or remove seed-containing sections, specifically adapted for fruit with very small pips such as raisins.

[A23N 4/14] for apples, pears or the like -- A23N 4/14 refers to machines that stone or remove seed-containing sections from harvested fruit such as apples, pears or similar varieties, operating in bulk. The devices are specialised for eliminating pits or cores as part of post-harvest processing.
[A23N 4/16] for tomatoes or the like -- A23N 4/16 pertains to machines for bulk processing of harvested fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs that are designed to stone or remove seed-containing sections, specifically for tomatoes and similar produce. Such equipment extracts pits or cores from the fruit without providing additional processing functions.
[A23N 4/18] for citrus fruits -- A23N 4/18 covers machines designed to stone or remove seed-containing sections specifically from citrus fruits. The devices are intended for bulk processing of harvested fruit and are a sub-category of apparatus for treating fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs.
[A23N 4/20] for pineapples -- A23N 4/20 covers machines designed for processing pineapples, including equipment that removes the core, slices, or extracts seed-containing sections from the fruit in bulk. These devices belong to the broader class of apparatus for treating harvested fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs.
[A23N 4/22] for both splitting and stoning -- A23N 4/22 covers machines designed to process harvested fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs in bulk by performing both splitting and stoning operations in a single unit. These devices combine a splitting mechanism with a stoning or seed-removal system to prepare produce for further handling.
[A23N 4/24] for removing seed-containing sections from cut fruit -- A23N 4/24 covers machines that remove seed-containing sections from cut fruit in bulk, such as stoning or de-seeding devices used in processing lines for fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs. These apparatus are designed to separate or extract pits, stones or cores from pieces of fruit rather than whole fruit.

This entry falls under the broader category of machines for treating harvested fruits, vegetables, or flower bulbs in bulk.
[A23N 5/03] for coconuts -- A23N 5/03 covers machines designed specifically for processing coconuts, such as hulling, husking or cracking them, within the broader group of equipment for treating harvested fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs in bulk.

[A23N 7/00] Peeling vegetables or fruit -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines for peeling vegetables or fruit in bulk, or on an industrial scale  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for skinning onions -> A23N15/08 - Peeling by non-mechanical means -> A23P - Peeling machines of the household type -> A47J17/00
[A23N 7/002] Peeling pinaeapples  A23N7/005  and  A23N7/01  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines for peeling pineapples, in bulk, or on an industrial scale  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Peeling machines of the household type -> A47J17/00
[A23N 7/005] Peeling with steam -- The CPC subclass A23N 7/005 covers the use of steam to remove skins from harvested vegetable or fruit products. It includes apparatus and processes that direct steam onto the produce to loosen or detach the peel, allowing the outer layer to be stripped off in bulk handling operations. This method is applied where heat-based peeling is preferred over mechanical or chemical techniques.
[A23N 7/01] using chemical substances, e.g. lye -- A23N 7/01 covers methods and apparatus that employ chemical agents such as lye to remove the skins of harvested fruits or vegetables in bulk, typically as part of a peeling operation. This subclass falls under machines for treating harvested produce within the broader category of food-related equipment.
[A23N 7/02] Peeling potatoes, apples or similarly shaped vegetables or fruit -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines for peeling potatoes, apples or similarly shaped vegetables or fruit in bulk, or on an industrial scale  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Peeling machines of the household type -> A47J17/00
[A23N 7/023] one by one -- A23N 7/023 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to machines or apparatus designed for peeling vegetables or fruits individually, addressing specific needs not covered by broader categories. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office to organize patent applications and documents.
[A23N 7/026] Peeling machines therefor with rotary fruit holding spindles and fixed or movable peeler blades -- A23N 7/026 covers machines used to peel harvested fruit, vegetables or flower bulbs in bulk, featuring rotary spindles that hold the produce while fixed or movable peeler blades remove the skin. The design is intended for continuous, high-throughput processing in food-handling facilities.
[A23N 7/04] Peeling asparagus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines for peeling asparagus in bulk, or on an industrial scale  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Peeling with steam -> A23N7/005 - Peeling using chemical substances, e.g.lye -> A23N7/01 - Peeling machines of the household type -> A47J17/00
[A23N 7/08] for peeling fruit and removing seed-containing sections -- A23N 7/08 covers equipment designed to peel fruit or vegetables and to excise seed-containing portions, typically used in bulk processing lines for fruits, vegetables or flower bulbs. The machines automate removal of skins and seeds to improve efficiency in food preparation and industrial processing.
[A23N 7/10] Driving mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Driving mechanisms used in machines for peeling vegetables or fruit in bulk, or on an industrial scale  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Peeling machines of the household type -> A47J17/00
[A23P] SHAPING OR WORKING OF FOODSTUFFS, NOT FULLY COVERED BY A SINGLE OTHER SUBCLASS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus and processes for shaping and working of foodstuff in general, not particular to any special kind of food. Processes are concerned with apparatus or process parameters (equipment to be used, configuration of said equipment, parameters such as pressure, temperature, duration of progress by equipment setup), rather than referring to products characteristics, such as recipes, ingredients etc.  **Glossary:** - Food: In addition to the commonly accepted foods or foodstuffs, the term in this subclass also extends to cover food ingredients, such as thickening agents (e.g. starch), flavouring agents, artificial sweeteners, food colouring agents, etc.
[A23P 10/00] Shaping or working of foodstuffs characterised by the products -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus and process aspects of processing of food items in general, not related to specific food types
[A23P 10/10] Securing foodstuffs on a non-edible supporting member -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines and process for fixation of foodstuff on a non-edible supporting member, not specific to a particular type of food products
[A23P 10/20] Agglomerating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines and processes for agglomerating, granulating, tabletting of food products in general, not specific to a particular type of food products
[A23P 10/22] Agglomeration or granulation with pulverisation of solid particles, e.g. in a free-falling curtain -- A23P 10/22 covers processes that produce agglomerates or granules by pulverising solid particles while they are shaped, most commonly using a free-falling curtain technique. The method is applied in food-stuff preparation to create uniform granulated products, such as instant powders or reconstitutable meals, by combining grinding and granulation in a single step.
[A23P 10/25] Agglomeration or granulation by extrusion or by pressing, e.g. through small holes, through sieves or between surfaces -- A23P 10/25 covers processes that form agglomerates or granules of food materials by forcing the product through small openings, sieves or between closely spaced surfaces, using extrusion or pressing techniques. It applies to shaping or working of foodstuffs where the product's form is created by these mechanical agglomeration methods.
[A23P 10/28] Tabletting -- A23P 10/28 covers the shaping or working of foodstuffs that result in solid tablet forms. It includes the processes, equipment and parameters used to compress, press or mould powders, granules or other food materials into tablets such as nutraceutical, snack or medicinal-food tablets. This subclass is part of the broader category for shaping or working of foodstuffs not fully covered by a single other subclass.
[A23P 10/30] Encapsulation of particles, e.g. foodstuff additives -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines and processes for encapsulation of particles, e.g. foodstuff additives in general, not specific to a particular type of food products
[A23P 10/35] with oils, lipids, monoglycerides or diglycerides -- A23P 10/35 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system pertains to the shaping or working of foodstuffs characterized by products that include oils, lipids, monoglycerides, or diglycerides. This classification is used for inventions involving the processing and formation of food items where these fat components play a significant role.
[A23P 10/40] free-flowing powder or instant powder, i.e. powder which is reconstituted rapidly when liquid is added -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines and processes for making free-flowing or instant food powders in general, not specific to a particular type of food products  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Agglomerating; Granulating; Tabletting -> A23P10/20 - Encapsulation of particles, e.g. foodstuff additives -> A23P10/30
[A23P 10/43] using anti-caking agents or agents improving flowability, added during or after formation of the powder -- This subclass covers processes in which anti-caking or flow-enhancing agents are incorporated into powdered food materials either during the shaping step or after the powder has been formed. The agents improve handling, prevent lumping and facilitate further processing of the product.
[A23P 10/47] using additives, e.g. emulsifiers, wetting agents or dust-binding agents -- A23P 10/47 covers methods of shaping or working foodstuffs that involve the use of additives such as emulsifiers, wetting agents or dust-binding agents to improve handling, texture or stability. The classification is part of the CPC group dealing with food preparation processes not fully covered by other subclasses.
[A23P 20/00] Coating of foodstuffs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines and processes for coating of foodstuffs, coatings therefore, making of laminated, multilayered or stuffed foodstuffs in general, not specific to a particular type of food products
[A23P 20/10] Coating with edible coatings, e.g. with oils or fats -- This subclass concerns the application of edible films or layers to food items using substances such as oils, fats, or other edible coating materials. It includes processes for forming, applying, and drying such coatings to improve texture, preservation, or appearance. It falls under shaping or working of foodstuffs within the human necessities category.
[A23P 20/105] Coating with compositions containing vegetable or microbial fermentation gums, e.g. cellulose or derivatives; Coating with edible polymers, e.g. polyvinyalcohol -- A23P 20/105 relates to coating foodstuffs with compositions that contain gums derived from vegetable or microbial fermentation, such as cellulose and its derivatives, as well as edible polymers like polyvinyl alcohol. These coatings are used to modify texture, stability or appearance of the product.
[A23P 20/11] Coating with compositions containing a majority of oils, fats, mono/diglycerides, fatty acids, mineral oils, waxes or paraffins -- A23P 20/11 relates to coating foodstuffs with compositions in which oils, fats, mono- or diglycerides, fatty acids, mineral oils, waxes or paraffins form the majority of the material. The subclass covers methods for applying such lipid-rich layers to improve texture, appearance or preservation.
[A23P 20/12] Apparatus or processes for applying powders or particles to foodstuffs, e.g. for breading -- A23P 20/12 covers equipment and methods for applying powders or particulate materials to food items, such as breading, battering, seasoning or coating processes. It includes devices that spray, dust, or otherwise deposit dry particles onto items like meat, fish, vegetables or bakery products, facilitating uniform coverage in food preparation.
[A23P 20/13] involving agitation of the foodstuff inside a rotating apparatus, e.g. a drum -- A23P 20/13 covers coating of foodstuffs by agitating them inside a rotating apparatus such as a drum. The subclass deals with processes that continuously move and turn the food material to apply an even layer of coating during cooking, drying or seasoning.
[A23P 20/15] Apparatus or processes for coating with liquid or semi-liquid products -- A23P 20/15 covers devices and methods used to apply liquid or semi-liquid coatings to food items. It includes techniques such as spraying, dipping, brushing or tumbling that uniformly deposit sauces, batters, glazes or protective films onto the surface of the product, often to improve flavor, appearance or shelf-life.
[A23P 20/17] by dipping in a bath -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus or processes for dip-coating in a liquid or semi-liquid bath
[A23P 20/18] by spray-coating, fluidised-bed coating or coating by casting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Other apparatus or processes for coating of food products with liquid or semi-liquid products, e.g. spray-coating, fluidised-bed coating, coating by casting, not provided for in other classes  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Apparatus for applying powders or particles to foodstuffs, e.g. for breading; Such apparatus combined with means for pre-moistening or battering -> A23P20/12
[A23P 20/19] Coating with non-edible coatings -- The subclass A23P 20/19 covers processes for applying non-edible coatings to foodstuffs, such as protective films, waxes, polymers or other materials that are not intended for consumption. It is part of the broader group concerning shaping or working of foodstuffs.
[A23P 20/20] Making of laminated, multi-layered, stuffed or hollow foodstuffs, e.g. by wrapping in preformed edible dough sheets or in edible food containers -- A23P 20/20 covers processes for producing laminated, multi-layered, stuffed or hollow food items, such as wrapping fillings in pre-formed edible dough sheets or edible containers. The subclass concerns shaping or working foods by assembling layers or cavities, typical of pastries, pies and similar products.
[A23P 2020/251] Tempura batter; Leavened or other aerate batter or coating -- The CPC sub-group A23P 2020/251 covers inventions relating to the formulation, preparation or use of tempura batter and other aerated or leavened batters and coatings applied to foods before cooking, particularly for deep-frying or similar processes. It includes compositions that create a light, crisp crust and methods for improving texture, adhesion or stability of such batters. This classification is used for patents on culinary technology involving these specialized coating mixtures.
[A23P 2020/253] Coating food items by printing onto them; Printing layers of food products -- A23P 2020/253 covers the process of applying edible coatings to food by printing them directly onto the product, as well as the sequential printing of multiple food layers to create patterned or functional surfaces. The classification includes techniques that use food-based inks or pastes to form decorative, protective or nutritional coatings.
[A23P 20/25] Filling or stuffing cored food pieces, e.g. combined with coring or making cavities -- This group deals with methods and apparatuses for filling or stuffing food items that have been cored or have cavities, including combined coring and stuffing steps. It covers processes for inserting pastes, gels, powders or other fillings into vegetables, fruits, meat or bakery pieces after a cavity is created.
[A23P 2030/405] Forming foamed food products by sonication -- The CPC subclass A23P 2030/405 pertains to processes and apparatus for producing foamed food products by applying ultrasonic energy, or sonication, to a food mass in order to generate a stable foam structure. It includes methods that use high-frequency sound waves to introduce and disperse gas bubbles in doughs, batters, dairy or meat formulations, enhancing texture and volume without mechanical whipping.
[A23P 30/00] Shaping or working of foodstuffs characterised by the process or apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Shaping methods for food products, e.g. moulding, extruding etc., not specific to a particular type of food products, and their respective apparatus  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Shaping or working of foodstuffs characterised by the products -> A23P10/00 - Coating of foodstuffs; Coating therefor; Making laminated, multi-layered, stuffed or hollow foodstuffs -> A23P20/00
[A23P 30/10] Moulding -- A23P 30/10 moulding covers the shaping of foodstuffs by forcing a mass into a mould or cavity using pressure, heat or other means, and includes the processes and apparatus for producing molded food products such as gels, confections or meat analogues.
[A23P 30/20] Extruding -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Shaping of food products by extrusion in general, not specific to a particular type of food products
[A23P 30/25] Co-extrusion of different foodstuffs -- A23P 30/25 covers the shaping or working of foodstuffs by co-extrusion, where two or more different food materials are simultaneously extruded through a common die to form layered, combined or multi-component products. The subclass includes processes and apparatus designed for producing items such as multi-flavor snacks, filled pasta, or composite bakery goods by merging distinct food streams in a single extrusion step.
[A23P 30/30] Puffing or expanding -- A23P 30/30 covers processes and apparatus for puffing or expanding foodstuffs, including cereals, snack pieces and similar products, by rapid heating, pressure release or mechanical means that cause the material to inflate or increase in volume. It pertains to shaping or working of foodstuffs not otherwise classified, encompassing methods such as extrusion, steam-inflation, microwave expansion and related equipment.
[A23P 30/32] by pressure release, e.g. explosion puffing -- A23P 30/32 covers methods and apparatus for shaping or working foodstuffs by rapid pressure release, such as explosion-puffing of dough or batter to create porous, expanded products. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs → Shaping or Working of Foodstuffs, not fully covered by other subclasses.
[A23P 30/34] by extrusion-expansion -- A23P 30/34 covers shaping or working of foodstuffs by extrusion followed by expansion, such as puffed or expanded snack products. The subclass includes processes and apparatus where a material is forced through a die and then enlarged by pressure release, heating or other means, producing a porous structure. It falls under the broader category of shaping or working of foodstuffs that are not fully covered by other subclasses.
[A23P 30/36] in discontinuously working apparatus -- A23P 30/36 covers apparatus that shape or work foodstuffs in a discontinuous, batch-type mode rather than continuously. It includes machines where the processing steps occur intermittently, such as periodic loading, forming, or removal cycles, and is used for operations not fully captured by other more specific subclasses within the shaping of foodstuffs.
[A23P 30/38] by heating -- A23P 30/38 covers shaping or working foodstuffs by applying heat, such as baking, roasting, or other heat-based forming processes. It includes apparatuses and methods where temperature is used to achieve the desired shape or texture during food preparation.
[A23P 30/40] Foaming or whipping -- A23P 30/40 covers processes and apparatus for incorporating air or other gases into foodstuffs to produce a foam or whipped texture, such as in desserts, batters, or dairy products. It includes mechanical agitation, aeration methods and equipment like mixers, whippers and foaming devices.
[A23V] INDEXING SCHEME RELATING TO FOODS, FOODSTUFFS OR NON-ALCOHOLIC BEVERAGES AND LACTIC OR PROPIONIC ACID BACTERIA USED IN FOODSTUFFS OR FOOD PREPARATION -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Indexing scheme related to:
[A23V 2002/00] Food compositions, function of food ingredients or processes for food or foodstuffs -- A23V 2002/00 covers food compositions, the functional role of food ingredients and the processes used to produce foods or foodstuffs, especially those involving non-alcoholic beverages and lactic or propionic acid bacteria for preparation and preservation. It falls under the human necessities hierarchy, specifically within foods or foodstuffs and the indexing scheme for related microbiological applications.
[A23V 2200/00] Function of food ingredients -- The CPC subclass A23V 2200/00 covers the functional aspects of food ingredients, detailing how components such as additives, nutrients, or processing aids affect the properties, performance, or health benefits of foods, non-alcoholic beverages and related products. It is part of the indexing scheme for foods, foodstuffs and lactic or propionic acid bacteria used in food preparation, nested under Human Necessities → Foods or Foodstuffs.
[A23V 2200/02] Antioxidant -- A23V 2200/02 covers antioxidants used as functional food ingredients, indicating substances that prevent oxidation in foods, beverages or food preparations, often involving lactic or propionic acid bacteria. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for indexing schemes related to foods and non-alcoholic drinks.
[A23V 2200/03] Antigenicity -- A23V 2200/03 covers the antigenic properties of food ingredients, including substances that can trigger an immune response such as allergens or bacterial antigens used in foods. It addresses detection, reduction and labeling of such antigenicity in food preparation and products.
[A23V 2200/04] Colour -- A23V 2200/04 covers the function of colour as a food ingredient, encompassing the use of colourants in foods, non-alcoholic beverages and related preparations, including those derived from lactic or propionic acid bacteria.
[A23V 2200/042] Browning -- This CPC subclass A23V 2200/042 covers browning reactions in foods, including enzymatic and non-enzymatic processes that affect colour, flavour and preservation. It includes methods, additives and conditions used to induce, control or
[A23V 2200/044] Colouring -- A23V 2200/044 covers the use of colourants as functional food ingredients, including dyes added to foods, non-alcoholic drinks, or food preparations that involve lactic or propionic acid bacteria. It classifies inventions that provide or improve colouring agents for these applications.
[A23V 2200/046] Discolouring, bleaching -- A23V 2200/046 covers processes for discolouring or bleaching food ingredients, especially when lactic or propionic-acid bacteria are involved in the preparation of foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for functions of food ingredients within the broader categories of human necessities and foods or foodstuffs.

[A23V 2400/227] Ferrus -- A23V 2400/227 (Ferrus) pertains to the use of iron compounds in food-related applications involving lactic or propionic acid bacteria, such as fortifying fermented dairy, vegetable or beverage products with iron to enhance nutritional value or influence microbial activity.
[A23V 2400/229] Kefiri -- Kefiri refers to the fermented milk drink known as kefir, produced by culturing milk with a consortium of lactic and propionic acid bacteria often in association with yeasts. The entry covers the microorganisms, preparation methods, and product characteristics related to this dairy fermentation.
[A23V 2400/231] Lactis -- This CPC subclass A23V 2400/231 "Lactis" covers lactic or propionic acid bacteria of the genus Lactis used in food preparation, especially as starter cultures for dairy fermentation and related processes, within the broader indexing scheme for foods, foodstuffs and non-alcoholic beverages.
[A23V 2400/233] Macedonius -- A23V 2400/233 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system refers to the genus Macedonius, which is a type of lactic acid bacteria used in foodstuffs or food preparation. This classification helps in indexing patents related to the use of these specific bacteria in the production of foods or beverages.
[A23V 2400/235] Macacae -- A23V 2400/235 - Macacae covers the use of lactic or propionic acid bacteria of the genus Macacae in the preparation of foods, foodstuffs or non-alcoholic beverages. It belongs to the CPC hierarchy for human necessities, foods, and indexing schemes relating to bacterial cultures applied in food processing.
[A23V 2400/237] Mutans -- The A23V 2400/237 entry covers mutans, a group of lactic-acid bacteria that can be present in foodstuffs or used in food preparation. It includes methods for detecting, inhibiting, or employing these bacteria in processing, preservation, or fermentation of foods and non-alcoholic beverages.
[A23V 2400/239] Oralis -- A23V 2400/239 Oralis classifies inventions that employ lactic or propionic acid bacteria for oral use, such as probiotic preparations, food additives, or pharmaceutical compositions intended to be taken by mouth to provide health benefits or assist in food processing.
[A23V 2400/241] Paracitroorarus -- A23V 2400/241 falls under the CPC group for foods and non-alcoholic beverages that involve lactic or propionic acid bacteria, specifically covering the genus Paracitroorarus. It classifies inventions or processes that use these bacteria in food preparation, preservation, or fermentation. This entry is part of the broader hierarchy for human necessities and foodstuff indexing.
[A23V 2400/243] Rattus -- A23V 2400/243 designates the genus Rattus within the CPC class for lactic or propionic acid bacteria. It covers inventions that use rats as hosts, models or sources in the isolation, testing or application of these bacteria for food or non-alcoholic beverage preparation.

[A23V 2400/247] Sobrinus -- A23V 2400/247 - Sobrinus covers the genus Sobrinus, a group of lactic or propionic acid bacteria used in the manufacture and preservation of foods and non-alcoholic beverages, especially dairy products where they contribute acidification and flavor development.

[A23V 2400/251] Uberis -- Uberis designates a lactic or propionic acid bacterium, typically a strain of Streptococcus uberis, employed in food preparation such as dairy fermentations. It falls under CPC A23V 2400/251, which groups microorganisms used

It covers applications of Leuconostoc strains in fermentation, flavor development, and safety enhancement of consumable products.
[A23V 2400/313] Carnosum -- Carnosum denotes a genus of lactic acid bacteria employed in food preparation and preservation, particularly in meat, dairy and fish products to inhibit spoilage and extend shelf life. These Gram-positive, non-spore-forming microbes produce lactic acid and are valued for their ability to compete with pathogenic and spoilage organisms.
[A23V 2400/315] Citreum -- Citreum is a genus of lactic or propionic acid bacteria employed in the fermentation of foodstuffs and non-alcoholic beverages. It contributes to flavor development, texture improvement and microbial stability in dairy, bakery and fermented vegetable products. This classification captures its role within food-related microbiological processes.
[A23V 2400/317] Cremoris -- A23V 2400/317 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system refers to specific lactic acid bacteria, particularly those of the Cremoris variety, used in the preparation or processing of foods and foodstuffs. This classification is part of a broader scheme that indexes various aspects of food technology, focusing on microorganisms that contribute to fermentation processes.
[A23V 2400/319] Dextranicum -- Dextranicum denotes a genus of lactic or propionic acid bacteria that are employed in the fermentation and preservation of foods, especially dairy and other fermented products. [A23V 2400/321] Mesenteroides -- A23V 2400/321 designates the genus Mesenteroides, a group of lactic- or propionic-acid bacteria used in the preparation and fermentation of foods and non-alcoholic beverages. [A23V 2400/421] Halophilus -- Halophilus denotes a genus of salt-tolerant lactic acid bacteria that thrive in high-salinity environments and are employed in food fermentations such as cheese, cured meats and vegetable pickles. These microorganisms contribute to flavor development, acidification and preservation, making them valuable in processes where conventional lactic-acid bacteria are inhibited by salt. The code A23V 2400/421 classifies this category within the CPC scheme for foods, non-alcoholic beverages and the use of lactic or propionic acid bacteria.

[A23V 2400/427] Pentosaceus -- The CPC code A23V 2400/427 identifies the lactic or propionic acid bacterium Pentosaceus, a microorganism used in food preparation and fermentation processes. [A23V 2400/429] Urinaeequi -- A23V 2400/429 is a CPC subclass that indexes lactic or propionic acid bacteria employed in foodstuffs, non-alcoholic beverages and related preparations. [A23V 2400/513] Adolescentes -- A23V 2400/513 covers the use of lactic or propionic acid bacteria in food products intended for adolescents, including methods of preparation, formulation and functional benefits aimed at this age group.
[A23V 2400/515] Animalis -- A23V 2400/515 Animalis refers to lactic or propionic acid bacteria of animal origin used in food preparation, encompassing strains employed in the fermentation of dairy, meat or other animal-derived products.

[A23V 2400/529] Infantis -- A23V 2400/529 covers lactic or propionic acid bacteria designated "infantis", i.e., bacterial strains such as Bifidobacterium or Lactobacillus infantis employed in foods and food-preparation processes, particularly in products intended for infants or in probiotic formulations.
