It is part of the human necessities field, specifically under tobacco and machines for making cigarettes, focusing on the step of moving the raw product from storage to the forming unit.
[A24C 5/326] with lateral transferring means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for changing the conveyance direction of rods from axial to a direction perpendicular to the axis of the rod, or vice versa.
[A24C 5/327] Construction details of the cigarette transport drum -- A24C 5/327 in the Cooperative Patent Classification focuses on specific construction aspects of the cigarette transport drum, a component used in machines designed for making cigarettes. This classification is part of a detailed system covering various aspects of tobacco processing machinery.
[A24C 5/328] Controlling means -- The A24C 5/328 subclass defines controlling means for cigarette-making machines, covering devices and mechanisms that regulate the machine's operation such as sensors, feedback loops, speed regulators and material-flow controls to maintain consistent production and product quality.
[A24C 5/33] Catching or ordering devices -- A24C 5/33 covers devices that capture cigarettes as they exit a forming station or arrange them in a predetermined order for further handling. Such catching or ordering mechanisms are used in cigarette-making machines to separate, align, or transfer the products to packaging or other downstream processes.
[A24C 5/333] for combining several rows into one -- A24C 5/333 covers apparatus or methods that merge multiple parallel rows of cigarettes into a single row during the manufacturing process, typically integrated into machines that form, move, or handle cigarettes. This sub-class relates to the step of combining several rows into one to streamline downstream processing and packaging.
[A24C 5/336] Turning means -- Turning means refers to mechanisms in cigarette-making machines that rotate or re-orient the tobacco rod or finished cigarettes to enable subsequent processing steps such as cutting, drying or packaging. These devices include rotors, turntables or similar equipment that move the product along the production line.
[A24C 5/34] Examining cigarettes or the rod, e.g. for regulating the feeding of tobacco -- A24C 5/34 covers the inspection of cigarettes or the tobacco rod during production, such as mechanisms that monitor and regulate the feeding of tobacco into the making machine. It includes sensors, optical devices or control systems used to ensure proper positioning and consistency of the cigarette rod.
[A24C 5/3406] Controlling cigarette combustion -- A24C 5/3406 covers technologies that regulate the burning of a cigarette to achieve a consistent flame, reduce excess smoke, or modify temperature, including ventilation systems, heat-control devices, and specialized filters. The classification applies to any apparatus or method designed to manage combustion rate or intensity during smoking.
[A24C 5/3412] by means of light, radiation or electrostatic fields  A24C5/3406  takes precedence -- A24C 5/3412 relates to methods and apparatus for making cigarettes that employ light, radiation or electrostatic fields to form, treat or manipulate the tobacco, such as laser-based or plasma-based techniques. It falls under the broader A24C 5/3406 class for cigarette-making processes.
[A24C 5/3418] by pneumatic means  A24C5/3406  takes precedence -- A24C 5/3418 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to methods of making cigarettes using pneumatic means, with a note that A24C5/3406 takes precedence. This classification is part of a detailed system for tobacco machinery, specifically focusing on cigarette manufacturing techniques.
[A24C 5/3424] by weighing  A24C5/3406  takes precedence -- A24C 5/3424 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to the process of making cigarettes, specifically focusing on methods that involve weighing. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with machines for making cigars or cigarettes and is subject to a precedence rule where A24C5/3406 takes priority if applicable.
[A24C 5/343] by mechanical means, e.g. feelers -- A24C 5/343 covers cigarette-making machines that employ mechanical devices such as feelers or other tactile sensors to control or adjust the production process. These mechanisms detect the presence, position or amount of tobacco or paper and trigger actions like cutting, feeding or sealing, enabling fully automated cigarette fabrication.
[A24C 5/345] Removing defective cigarettes -- This CPC subclass A24C 5/345 covers methods and apparatus for removing defective cigarettes from a production line, ensuring that only acceptable products continue to packaging. It is situated under machines for making cigarettes in the tobacco sector.
[A24C 5/35] Adaptations of conveying apparatus for transporting cigarettes from making machine to packaging machine -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Conveying apparatus specially designed for transporting cigarettes from the making machine to the packaging machine.
[A24C 5/352] using containers, i.e. boats -- A24C 5/352 covers cigarette-making processes that employ containers such as boats for handling, transporting, or storing tobacco products within the machine. The classification pertains to the use of these boat-type vessels as part of the apparatus that forms, fills, or moves cigarettes during production. It falls under the broader categories of human necessities, tobacco, and machines for making cigars or cigarettes.
[A24C 5/354] Filling the boats at the making machine -- A24C 5/354 concerns the portion of a cigarette-making machine that supplies tobacco to the forming chamber, often called the "boat." It covers devices and methods for loading or feeding the tobacco charge into the machine's forming zone during the cigarette-making process.
[A24C 5/356] Emptying the boats into the hopper of the packaging machine -- The A24C 5/356 subclass covers the process of discharging filled boats-trays or containers that hold tobacco or cigarettes-directly into the hopper of a cigarette-packaging machine. This step links the manufacturing line with packaging, ensuring a continuous supply of product for subsequent wrapping and sealing.
[A24C 5/358] Boat constructions -- A24C 5/358 pertains to cigarette-making machinery and defines the design of boat-shaped constructions used in those machines, such as carriers, reservoirs or transport elements that have a boat-like form to guide, hold or process tobacco during production.
[A24C 5/36] Removing papers or other parts from defective cigarettes -- This subclass covers processes for extracting the paper wrapper or other components from cigarettes that are flawed or incomplete, usually as part of quality-control or rework operations. It includes manual or mechanical methods to separate the tobacco rod from its covering without damaging the remaining material.
[A24C 5/38] Machines combined with printing devices -- A24C 5/38 refers to machines that integrate cigarette-making functions with printing devices, allowing the formation of tobacco rods and the application of printed markings such as branding, tar-level information or health warnings in a single apparatus. It lies within the CPC hierarchy under Human Necessities → Tobacco → Machines for Making Cigars or Cigarettes → Making Cigarettes.
[A24C 5/39] Tobacco feeding devices -- This CPC subclass A24C 5/39 deals with devices that feed tobacco into cigarette-making machines. It covers mechanisms that transport, supply, or position tobacco material for the forming of cigarettes, including conveyors, hoppers and dosing apparatus.
[A24C 5/391] feeding to several cigarette making machines -- A24C 5/391 covers systems that distribute tobacco material to more than one cigarette-making machine at the same time, providing synchronized feeding to keep each machine supplied continuously. It includes mechanisms for dividing, directing and delivering the rod or filler so that several machines can operate in parallel.
[A24C 5/392] feeding pneumatically -- A24C 5/392 covers a pneumatic feeding arrangement used in cigarette-making machines, where air pressure conveys tobacco, paper or other components into the forming zone. The system replaces mechanical conveyance, improving speed and reducing mechanical wear while ensuring precise placement of material during the making of cigarettes.
[A24C 5/393] with a vibrating conveyor -- A24C 5/393 refers to machines for making cigarettes that incorporate a vibrating conveyor to transport tobacco, paper or finished cigarettes within the device, improving material handling and feeding accuracy during the manufacturing process.
[A24C 5/394] with an auxiliary feed unit -- A24C 5/394 covers cigarette-making machines that incorporate an auxiliary feed unit, which supplies additional material such as tobacco plugs or filter elements alongside the main feed. The auxiliary unit enables continuous production or the insertion of different components during the manufacturing process.
[A24C 5/395] with arrangements in the hopper, e.g. for spreading, tamping -- A24C 5/395 covers cigarette-making machines that include particular hopper configurations, such as devices for spreading or tamping the tobacco, to ensure uniform feeding of the material during the manufacturing process.
[A24C 5/396] with separating means, e.g. winnowing, removing impurities -- A24C 5/396 concerns cigarette-making equipment that incorporates a separating stage, such as winnowing or other means for removing impurities from the tobacco before it is formed into a cigarette. It is a subclass of machines for making cigars or cigarettes that specifically address the cleaning or grading of the tobacco feed. This sub-group is part of the broader Human Necessities - Tobacco classification.
[A24C 5/397] with means for regulating the tobacco quantity -- A24C 5/397 covers cigarette-making machines that include mechanisms to control the amount of tobacco placed in each rod. The subclass specifies devices such as adjustable feeders, weigh-sensors or dosing chambers that regulate the tobacco quantity before it is packed, ensuring consistent product weight and size. It is a narrow category within the broader group of machines for making cigars or cigarettes.
[A24C 5/398] Driving arrangements -- A24C 5/398 concerns the driving arrangements used in machines that make cigarettes, covering mechanisms such as rollers, conveyors, pneumatic or mechanical drives that transport tobacco, paper or cigarette blanks through the forming and shaping sections of the equipment. This subclass falls under the broader group for machines for making cigars or cigarettes and focuses specifically on the systems that move material within the device.
[A24C 5/399] Component parts or details, e.g. feed roller, feed belt -- The subclass A24C 5/399 covers individual components and detailed parts of machines used for making cigarettes, such as feed rollers, feed belts and similar elements that convey tobacco material through the device. It pertains to the mechanical elements that control the movement and positioning of the workpiece within the cigarette-making process.
[A24C 5/40] Hand-driven apparatus for making cigarettes -- A hand-driven device for producing cigarettes, classified under CPC A24C 5/40, is a manually operated machine that shapes, compacts and assembles tobacco into finished cigarettes. It belongs to the category of machines for making cigars or cigarettes within the broader human necessities-tobacco group.
[A24C 5/42] Pocket cigarette-fillers -- Pocket cigarette-fillers are small portable devices that hold loose tobacco and dispense the appropriate amount for hand-rolling cigarettes. They are used in conjunction with manual cigarette-making machines to simplify the filling process and improve consistency. This entry falls under CPC A24C 5/42, covering equipment for making cigarettes within the broader categories of human necessities and tobacco.
[A24C 5/425] for obtaining cigarettes of various lengths -- A24C 5/425 covers features of cigarette-making machines that enable the production of cigarettes in a range of lengths. It includes mechanisms for adjusting paper feed, filler placement or cutting that allow operators to obtain shorter or longer cigarettes without changing the main machine. This subclass is part of the broader group for machines that make cigars or cigarettes.
[A24C 5/44] Pocket cigarette-rollers -- A24C 5/44 covers pocket-sized cigarette-rolling devices that manually or semi-automatically form tobacco into ready-to-smoke cigarettes for personal use. These compact machines are designed to be portable and operate without external power.
[A24C 5/46] Making paper tubes for cigarettes -- The A24C 5/46 subclass covers processes for producing the paper tubes that form the bodies of cigarettes. It includes methods of shaping, winding, and cutting paper or paper-like material into cylindrical tubes suitable for filling with tobacco, and addresses related apparatus and quality-control steps.
[A24C 5/465] the paper tubes partially containing a filter element -- A24C 5/465 covers paper cigarette tubes that incorporate a filter element only partially along the tube length, such as a filter inserted into the tube but not extending through its entire length, used in the manufacture of filter-tipped cigarettes.
[A24C 5/47] Attaching filters or mouthpieces to cigars or cigarettes, e.g. inserting filters into cigarettes or their mouthpieces -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Incorporating filters or mouthpieces into a cigarette rod or a tobacco rod -> A24C5/52
[A24C 5/471] by means of a connecting band -- A24C 5/471 refers to cigarette-making machines that employ a connecting band to join parts of the product, such as linking the tobacco rod to the filter or paper. The band is applied during the assembly process to hold components together before final sealing. This subclass falls under machines for making cigars or cigarettes within the human necessities field.
[A24C 5/472] Applying adhesives to the connecting band -- This subclass relates to the step in cigarette production where adhesive is applied to the connecting band that seals the ends of the tobacco rod. It covers devices or procedures that deposit glue onto the band during the assembling of cigarettes.
[A24C 5/473] Cutting the connecting band -- A24C 5/473 covers the step in cigarette-making equipment where the connecting band that holds the tobacco rod together is cut. The process removes or separates the band after the rod is formed, preparing the cigarette for final shaping or packaging. This sub-class falls under machines for making cigars or cigarettes within the human necessities field.
[A24C 5/474] by inserting filters into cigarettes or their mouthpieces -- A24C 5/474 covers techniques for adding a filter or filter tip to a finished cigarette or its mouthpiece, including inserting pre-formed filter rods, plug-type filters or applying filter material during the final assembly step.
[A24C 5/475] adapted for composite filters -- A24C 5/475 refers to cigarette-making machines that are specially configured to handle composite filters, such as multi-layer or mixed-material filters, allowing the filter to be attached during the manufacturing process.
[A24C 5/476] adapted for cigars or cigarettes with a hollow mouthpiece, e.g. recess filter cigarettes -- A24C 5/476 refers to machines for making cigars or cigarettes that are specifically adapted to produce items with a hollow mouthpiece, such as recessed-filter cigarettes. The subclass covers equipment modifications that accommodate the formation of the mouthpiece cavity during the manufacturing process.
[A24C 5/477] with a mouthpiece made from plastic, wood or the like -- A24C 5/477 covers cigarette-making machines that include a mouthpiece constructed from materials such as plastic, wood or similar substances, indicating the device is designed for user-handed operation with a detachable or integral mouthpiece of those types. This subclass falls under machines for making cigars or cigarettes within the broader human necessities and tobacco categories.
[A24C 5/478] Transport means for filter- or cigarette-rods in view of their assembling -- A transport system designed to move filter rods or cigarette rods during the assembly process, enabling precise positioning and feeding of the components for joining into finished cigarettes. It is used in machines that produce cigarettes by aligning and delivering the rods to the point where filters are attached.
[A24C 5/52] Incorporating filters or mouthpieces into a cigarette rod or a tobacco rod -- A24C 5/52 concerns the incorporation of filters or mouthpieces into a cigarette or tobacco rod, covering designs that attach or integrate filtration elements directly
[A24C 5/54] Folding the ends of cigarette paper tubes after filling them with tobacco -- It covers the step of sealing a cigarette rod by folding the paper at both ends after the tobacco has been packed, including the methods and apparatus used to form those folds and produce a finished cigarette.
[A24C 5/56] Making tipping materials, e.g. sheet cork for mouthpieces of cigars or cigarettes, by mechanical means -- This CPC subgroup (A24C 5/56) relates to the mechanical production of tipping materials, such as sheet cork, that serve as mouthpieces for cigars or cigarettes. It is classified under machines for making cigarettes, within the broader tobacco category of human necessities.
[A24C 5/565] by treating the tipping material with a liquid or viscous solution, e.g. paraffine  final treatment  A24C5/608 -- A24C 5/565 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to the process of making cigarettes, specifically focusing on the treatment of tipping material with liquids or viscous solutions like paraffin during the final stages of production. This entry is part of a broader category that includes machines for manufacturing cigars and cigarettes under human necessities and tobacco.
[A24C 5/58] Applying the tipping materials -- A24C 5/58 relates to the step in cigarette production where a machine applies tipping material-such as filter plugs, flavored segments or other end pieces-to the ends of the tobacco rod. The classification covers apparatus and methods for positioning, attaching and securing this material during the making of cigarettes.
[A24C 5/583] to a continuous web of cigarette paper -- This subclass covers cigarette-making machines that supply paper as a continuous roll rather than individual sheets, unwinding the web and feeding it through the forming process to produce the finished cigarette.
[A24C 5/586] to a cigarette -- A24C 5/586 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to the process of making cigarettes, specifically detailing methods or apparatus for forming the cigarette itself.
[A24C 5/60] Final treatment of cigarettes, e.g. marking, printing, branding, decorating -- A24C 5/60 relates to post-manufacture processes applied to completed cigarettes, including marking, printing, branding or other decorative treatments that add visual identifiers, information or aesthetic features to the product.
[A24C 5/601] Marking, printing or decorating cigarettes -- A24C 5/601 covers techniques for applying markings, labels, graphics or other decorative elements to finished cigarettes, including printing of brand names, logos, health warnings, color patterns or texture modifications. The classification includes processes, equipment and materials used to imprint or adorn tobacco products after they are formed.
[A24C 5/602] Bronzing -- A24C 5/602, titled "Bronzing," covers methods or apparatus used in cigarette-making machines to apply a brown or bronzed finish to tobacco, paper or filter components during production, giving the product a darker appearance. This subclass falls under machines for making cigarettes, specifically the step of bronzing within the overall manufacturing process.
[A24C 5/603] Final drying of cigarettes -- A24C 5/603 covers the stage in cigarette production where the finished products are subjected to a final drying process to reduce moisture content and stabilize the tobacco before packaging. This step follows earlier forming, drying, and cutting operations and is performed by equipment integrated into or attached to cigarette-making machines. The classification is part of the CPC hierarchy for machines used in making cigars or cigarettes.
[A24C 5/605] Pressing of cigarettes, e.g. for obtaining an oval form -- The CPC subclass A24C 5/605 covers processes that press finished tobacco rods to alter their shape, such as flattening or forming an oval cross-section after the cigarettes have been assembled. It pertains to post-manufacture shaping operations that improve handling, packaging or aesthetic characteristics of the final product.
[A24C 5/606] Perforating cigarettes -- A24C 5/606 is a CPC subclass under Human Necessities → Tobacco → Machines for making cigars or cigarettes → Making cigarettes, covering inventions that perforate cigarettes. It pertains to devices or methods that create holes, vents or channels in the tobacco rod to modify draw resistance, dilution or flavor delivery.
[A24C 5/607] by mechanical means -- The subclass A24C 5/607 covers the mechanical production of cigarettes, describing apparatus and methods that automatically shape, fill, compress and finish tobacco into complete cigarettes. It includes machines that perform these steps without manual intervention, such as automated cigarette-making lines that handle rod formation, filter attachment and packaging.
[A24C 5/608] Treating cigarettes with a liquid or viscous solution -- A24C 5/608 covers processes in which finished cigarettes are subjected to a liquid or viscous coating, such as flavoring, moisture, preservatives, or other additive solutions, after manufacture. The treatment aims to modify the taste, burn characteristics, or shelf-life of the tobacco product.
[A24D] CIGARS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cigars, Cigarettes  Tobacco smoke filters  Manufacture of tobacco smoke filters or mouthpieces  **Glossary:** - Cigarette: "cigarette" means: (1) a small tube of wrapper containing tobacco or tobacco substitutes which people smoke by igniting it directly and where the tobacco (or the tobacco substitute) is burned; (2) in a broader sense it also means a small tube of wrapper containing tobacco or tobacco substitutes which is associated with a heat source at manufacturing or inserted by the user in a device containing a heat source, and where the tobacco (or the tobacco substitute) is heated without being burned. In this broader sense, the word "rod" is also often used in place of cigarette. - Simulated smoking device: "simulated smoking devices" means devices simulating the act of smoking without burning tobacco, e.g. so called electronic cigarettes
[A24D 1/00] Cigars -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cigars, Cigarettes as products per se
[A24D 1/002] with additives, e.g. for flavouring -- A24D 1/002 covers cigars that incorporate additives, such as flavoring agents, to modify or enhance their taste, aroma or smoking characteristics. The classification is applied to inventions where the additive is mixed with the tobacco or applied to the cigar during manufacture. It falls under the Human Necessities-Tobacco-Cigars hierarchy.
[A24D 1/004] with a filter located between tobacco rods -- A24D 1/004 covers cigars that include a filter placed between the tobacco rods, separating
[A24D 1/006] with reinforced extremities -- This subclass covers cigars whose ends are reinforced, for example by an extra wrapper or embedded material that strengthens the foot or tip to improve structural integrity and draw control.
[A24D 1/008] dividable -- A24D 1/008 covers cigars that are constructed to be divided into separate portions, allowing the product to be split for individual use or sharing. The classification applies to designs, methods of manufacture or features that enable the cigar to be broken apart without damaging the remaining sections.
[A24D 1/02] with special covers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cigars, Cigarettes in which the wrapper has a particular structure or composition.
[A24D 1/022] Papers for roll-your-own cigarettes -- A24D 1/022 classifies inventions related to papers specifically designed for hand-rolled cigarettes, covering their composition, dimensions, moisture content and burning characteristics to improve user experience and safety. The code is used in patent documents to identify technologies for disposable rolling papers within the broader tobacco and human necessities categories.
[A24D 1/025] the covers having material applied to defined areas, e.g. bands for reducing the ignition propensity -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a tobacco column (12) within a wrapper (14) comprising treated areas (24).  **Glossary:** - ignition propensity: capacity of a cigarette of burning through when left resting on a substrate without puffing
[A24D 1/027] with ventilating means, e.g. perforations -- A24D 1/027 covers cigar designs that incorporate ventilating features such as perforations or other openings to allow airflow through the tobacco, improving draw and reducing harshness. It is a subcategory of cigar constructions within the broader tobacco classification.
[A24D 1/04] with mouthpieces or filter-tips -- A24D 1/04 is a CPC subclass covering cigars that incorporate a mouthpiece or a filter-tip. It falls under the hierarchy of Human Necessities → Tobacco → Cigars and specifies designs where a detachable or integral component guides smoke away from the lip. This subclass is used by examiners to index patents dealing with such modified cigar constructions.
[A24D 1/042] with mouthpieces  A24D1/047  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With extensible mouthpieces -> A24D1/047
[A24D 1/045] with smoke filter means  A24D1/047  takes precedence -- A24D 1/045 covers cigars that incorporate a smoke-filter device, such as a built-in filter rod, tip or other means to reduce the amount of smoke inhaled by the user. This subclass is subordinate to A24D 1/047, which has priority over it.
[A24D 1/047] extensible -- The CPC entry A24D 1/047, titled "extensible," classifies cigars that are designed or manufactured with features allowing their length or size to be increased, such as removable or insertable sections that can be added to the tobacco rod. This includes cigars equipped with extensions, sleeves or modular components that enable the smoker to adjust the overall dimensions while maintaining a single, continuous smoking experience.
[A24D 1/08] with lighting means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Smoking products combined with pyrophoric or easily ignitable material and products combined with match scratchers or analogous means
[A24D 1/10] with extinguishers -- A24D 1/10 classifies cigars that include a built-in or attached means for extinguishing the ember, such as an internal water, chemical or mechanical device. The entry covers any cigar design where an extinguishing feature is integrated for safety or convenience.
[A24D 1/12] with ash-retaining attachments, holders, or other equipment -- The A24D 1/12 subclass covers cigars that incorporate devices such as ash-retaining attachments, holders, or other auxiliary equipment designed to collect or manage ash during smoking. It pertains to accessories integrated with or added to cigars to improve handling or reduce ash spillage.
[A24D 1/14] Tobacco cartridges for pipes -- The CPC subclass A24D 1/14 covers tobacco cartridges designed for use in smoking pipes, including pre-filled, replaceable inserts that hold processed tobacco for convenient pipe smoking. It falls under the hierarchy Human Necessities → Tobacco → Cigars → Cigars, specifying pipe-compatible cartridge formats.
[A24D 1/16] Bands for cigars or cigarettes -- The CPC entry A24D 1/16 relates to accessories for tobacco products, specifically bands that are applied to cigars or cigarettes. These bands may be decorative, functional, or used for branding, sealing, or identifying the product. They fall under the "Cigars" subclass within the broader "Tobacco" section of Human Necessities.
[A24D 1/18] Selection of materials, other than tobacco, suitable for smoking -- A24D 1/18 covers the choice and preparation of non-tobacco substances intended for use in smoking devices, including the selection of plant, mineral or synthetic materials that can be burned or vaporised. It addresses their suitability, properties, and treatment methods for producing a consumable smoking product.
[A24D 1/20] Cigarettes specially adapted for simulated smoking devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cigarettes to be inserted into simulated smoking devices where the cigarettes are heated by a heat source of the device.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates cigarette (40), tobacco or tobacco substitute segment (42), hollow tube (44), filter (44), mouthpiece (48) and wrapper (50).
[A24D 1/22] Cigarettes with integrated combustible heat sources, e.g. with carbonaceous heat sources -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cigarettes with an integrated combustible heat source which heats the tobacco (substitute) without burning it (to produce an aerosol) in the act of simulated smoking.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates cigarette (2), heat source (4), tobacco or tobacco substitute segment (10), hollow tube (12), aerosol cooling element (14), spacer (16), mouthpiece, filter (18) and wrapper (20).
[A24D 3/00] Tobacco smoke filters, e.g. filter tips or filtering inserts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Filters for tobacco smoke, products per se and their manufacture.  Mouthpieces for cigars or cigarettes.
[A24D 3/02] Manufacture of tobacco smoke filters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The production of tobacco smoke filters, e.g. from the filter tow bale to the final product.
[A24D 3/0204] Preliminary operations before the filter rod forming process, e.g. crimping, blooming  A24D3/0275  takes precedence -- A24D 3/0204 covers the preparatory steps carried out before forming the filter rod of a tobacco smoke filter, such as crimping the filter material or blooming it to set its shape. These operations are performed on filter tips or inserts prior to the final assembly of the cigarette filter. This entry falls under HUMAN NECESSITIES → TOBACCO → CIGARS → Tobacco smoke filters.
[A24D 3/0208] Cutting filter materials -- The A24D 3/0208 entry covers techniques and devices for cutting filter materials used in tobacco smoke filters, such as cutting cellulose acetate rods, foam plugs or other filter inserts to the required length or shape for incorporation into cigarettes.
[A24D 3/0212] Applying additives to filter materials -- A24D 3/0212 relates to processes for treating cigarette filter materials with additives such as flavorings, antimicrobial agents, moisture regulators or other functional compounds, thereby altering the filter's performance, taste or physical properties. The classification addresses the application step, including coating, impregnation or incorporation of these substances into filter tips or inserts.
[A24D 3/0216] the additive being in the form of capsules, beads or the like -- A24D 3/0216 covers tobacco-smoke filter additives that are provided as discrete capsules, beads, or similar particulate forms, which are incorporated into filter tips or inserts to modify filtration or flavor. It falls within the CPC hierarchy under Human Necessities → Tobacco → Cigars → Tobacco smoke filters.
[A24D 3/022] with liquid additives, e.g. application of plasticisers -- A24D 3/022 covers tobacco smoke filters, such as filter tips or inserts, that incorporate liquid additives. The entry specifically relates to the use of substances like plasticisers to modify the filter's flexibility, durability or filtration performance.
[A24D 3/0225] with solid additives, e.g. incorporation of a granular product  A24D3/0216  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass A24D 3/0225 covers tobacco-smoke filters that incorporate solid additives, such as granular products mixed into the filter material or inserted as a separate element. It relates to filters like filter tips or inserts that contain these solid additives, and it is subordinate to the more specific subclass A24D 3/0216.
[A24D 3/0229] Filter rod forming processes  A24D3/0275  takes precedence -- A24D 3/0229 covers processes for forming the filter rod of tobacco‐smoke filters, such as extrusion, shaping, cutting and assembling the filter material into a rod or tip. The class includes methods that produce a continuous or segmented filter element before it is attached to a cigarette. It is subordinated to A24D 3/0275, which takes precedence over it.
[A24D 3/0233] by means of a garniture -- This subclass covers cigar smoke-filtering arrangements where the filter is provided by means of a garniture-a decorative or functional accessory such as a holder, band, case or similar item that incorporates or carries the filtering element as part of the cigar.
[A24D 3/0237] by extrusion -- This subclass covers extrusion-formed tobacco smoke filters such as filter tips or inserts used in cigars. It includes methods and apparatus for extruding filter material, typically cellulose acetate, to produce continuous or segmented filter elements.
[A24D 3/0241] by compacting particulated materials -- A24D 3/0241 relates to tobacco‐smoke filters, such as filter tips or inserts, that are produced by compacting particulate materials to create a filtration medium. The classification covers the manufacturing method and resulting structure of these compacted-particle filters.
[A24D 3/0245] by winding, e.g. spirally -- A24D 3/0245 covers tobacco smoke filters, such as filter tips or inserts, that are produced by winding the filter material into a spiral configuration. This method creates a compact, tortuous path for smoke, enhancing filtration efficiency in cigars.
[A24D 3/025] Final operations, i.e. after the filter rod forming process  A24D3/0275  takes precedence -- A24D 3/025 pertains to the post-formation operations applied to tobacco-smoke filters, such as trimming, attaching filter tips, capping, and final quality checks that occur after the filter rod has been formed. This subclass falls under Human Necessities → Tobacco → Cigars → Tobacco smoke filters.
[A24D 3/0254] Cutting means -- Cutting means refer to the tools or mechanisms-such as blades, knives or cutting stations-used to trim, separate or shape filter components during the manufacture of tobacco smoke filters, including filter tips and internal filtering inserts.
[A24D 3/0258] Means for making grooves -- A24D 3/0258 covers devices or methods used to form longitudinal or transverse grooves in tobacco-smoke filter elements such as filter tips or inserts, enabling controlled airflow or filtration characteristics. The classification falls under Human Necessities → Tobacco → Cigars → Tobacco smoke filters.
[A24D 3/0262] Filter extremity shaping and compacting means -- Technological measures that shape and compact the terminal portion of a cigar's filter element, such as molding, compressing or otherwise forming the filter tip or insert to achieve a defined geometry, improve fit, stability and filtration performance.
[A24D 3/0266] Rolling means -- A24D 3/0266 relates to apparatuses or devices used to form tobacco or filter material into a roll for cigars, such as manual rollers, rolling tubes, molds or automated rolling mechanisms. It falls within the CPC group for tobacco smoke filters and covers the means of rolling the filter or tobacco element before insertion into a cigar.
[A24D 3/027] Multiple line manufacturing devices -- Multiple line manufacturing devices are apparatuses that produce tobacco smoke filter components, such as filter tips or inserts, on several parallel production lines at once, increasing output and synchronising the fabrication of filter elements for cigarettes. This technology falls under CPC A24D 3/027 within the tobacco-related human necessities classification.
[A24D 3/0275] for filters with special features -- A24D 3/0275 covers cigar tobacco-smoke filters that incorporate special features beyond standard filtering, such as unique shapes, materials, or functional additions designed to modify filtration performance, handling, or user experience. It applies to filter tips, inserts or similar components.
[A24D 3/0279] with tubes -- Tobacco smoke filters that incorporate a tubular element, such as a hollow or solid tube integrated into a filter tip or insert, to modify airflow, filtration efficiency or flavor delivery.
[A24D 3/0283] with means for a non-axial smoke flow -- The subclass A24D 3/0283 pertains to cigar smoke filters that incorporate features causing the smoke to flow non-axial, such as baffles, angled passages or labyrinthine structures that redirect the stream before it reaches the smoker. This design aims to improve filtration efficiency or modify draw characteristics.
[A24D 3/0287] for composite filters -- A24D 3/0287 relates to composite tobacco-smoke filters that combine two or more filtering materials-such as cellulose acetate, activated carbon, or other polymers-to improve removal of tar, nicotine and other constituents. The code covers filter tips, inserts or other filter structures designed for cigars or cigarettes that use layered or mixed-material constructions.
[A24D 3/0291] for hollow tipped filters, e.g. recess filters -- A24D 3/0291 covers tobacco smoke filters that incorporate a hollow tip, such as recess filters designed to receive a filter tip within a cavity. These filters are used in cigars and provide a sealed passage for smoke while allowing the tip to be inserted or removed. This subclass falls under the broader category of tobacco smoke filters in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[A24D 3/0295] Process control means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control means for the filter manufacturing process.
[A24D 3/04] Tobacco smoke filters characterised by their shape or structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The tobacco smoke filter as a product, eventually incorporated in a smoking article  Mechanical or structural aspects of the finished filter product
[A24D 3/041] with adjustable means for modifying the degree of filtration of the filter -- A24D 3/041 covers tobacco smoke filters such as filter tips or inserts for cigars that incorporate adjustable mechanisms to vary the amount of filtration. The adjustment may be achieved by movable sections, variable-density material, or other means to control smoke flow.
[A24D 3/043] with ventilation means, e.g. air dilution  A24D3/041  takes precedence -- A24D 3/043 covers tobacco smoke filters that incorporate ventilation features such as air-dilution openings or inserts
[A24D 3/045] with smoke acceleration means, e.g. impact-filters -- A24D 3/045 covers tobacco smoke filters that incorporate mechanisms to speed up the flow of smoke, such as impact-filters or other acceleration means, typically used in filter tips or inserts for cigars. This subclass falls under Human Necessities → Tobacco → Cigars.
[A24D 3/046] with electrical or magnetical filtering means -- A24D 3/046 covers tobacco-smoke filters that incorporate electrical or magnetic components to enhance filtration. The entry specifies devices such as filter tips or inserts that use an electric field, magnetic field, or electro-static mechanisms to capture or neutralise particles and gases in the smoke. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for human necessities, tobacco, cigars, and tobacco-smoke filters.
[A24D 3/048] containing additives -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Filters characterized by the presence of an additive in its constructional features.
[A24D 3/06] Use of materials for tobacco smoke filters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Material used in the tobacco smoke filter.  Chemical, structural or functional aspects of the material or additive.
[A24D 3/061] containing additives entrapped within capsules, sponge-like material or the like, for further release upon smoking -- This CPC subclass A24D 3/061 covers tobacco smoke filters for cigars that contain additives entrapped
[A24D 3/062] characterised by structural features -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structural features of the material itself or the additive (other than fibers), e.g. foam like material, particle size or shape of granular additives.
[A24D 3/063] of the fibers -- A24D 3/063 pertains to tobacco smoke filters made from specific types of fibers, such as those used in cigar filter tips or inserts, enhancing filtration properties.
[A24D 3/064] having non-circular cross-section -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. Y or C cross-section
[A24D 3/065] with sheath/core of bi-component type structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fibers with any composite structure, i.e. at least two distinctive regions, e.g. sheath/core.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1. Sheath/core or hollow fibers  2. Bi-component fibers  Figure 2 illustrates bicomponent fibers distributed in striped (2A), segmented pie (2B, 2C), trilobal (2D), sheath-core (2E), islands in the sea (2F, 2G).
[A24D 3/066] in the form of foam or having cellular structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  foam-like, cellular or porous material
[A24D 3/067] characterised by functional properties -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Functional properties of the material, additive or the filter itself, e.g. biodegradable filter, disintegrative paper or disintegration enhancers.
[A24D 3/068] Biodegradable or disintegrable -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Biodegradability or ease of disintegration in the environment is an important feature of the material or filter itself.
[A24D 3/08] of organic materials as carrier or major constituent -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Of ion exchange materials -> A24D3/12
[A24D 3/10] of cellulose or cellulose derivatives -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cellulose, e.g. paper or derivative e.g. cellulose acetate
[A24D 3/12] of ion exchange materials -- A24D 3/12 relates to tobacco smoke filters that employ ion-exchange materials, such as resins or zeolites, to capture and neutralise charged particles, acidic gases or other constituents in cigarette smoke, thereby improving filtration performance. This sub-class falls within the broader category of cigar filter tips and inserts.
[A24D 3/14] of organic materials as additive -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Organic additive, e.g. flavouring agent,  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Of ion exchange materials -> A24D3/12
[A24D 3/145] Microbial metabolite, e.g. enzyme -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Enzymes or other microbial metabolite or extract thereof, e.g. bacterial extract.
[A24D 3/16] of inorganic materials -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Comprising inorganic material as main constituent or as additive.
[A24D 3/163] Carbon -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Carbon, generally active carbon.
[A24D 3/166] Silicic acid or silicates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  includes also silica, zeolites, aluminosilicates
[A24D 3/17] Filters specially adapted for simulated smoking devices -- A24D 3/17 covers filters that are specifically designed for use in simulated smoking devices, such as training rigs or testing apparatuses, including specially shaped tips or inserts that replicate cigarette filters but are not intended for real tobacco consumption.
[A24D 3/18] Mouthpieces of cigars or cigarettes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  elements adapted to be inserted at the mouth end of cigar or cigarettes, usually but not necessarily having filtering action, e.g. elements for smoking Russian cigarettes.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Manufacture of tobacco smoke filters -> A24D3/02
[A24D 3/185] with a straw-like insert, e.g. Virginia cigars -- A tobacco smoke filter designed for Virginia cigars that incorporates a hollow, straw-like insert, often extending through the filter tip to provide a conduit for air or smoke. This configuration is used to modify draw resistance and flavor delivery in the cigar.
[A24F] SMOKERS' REQUISITES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Smokers' requisites:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Inhaling appliances for medical purposes, shaped like cigars, cigarettes or pipes -> A61M15/06
[A24F 1/00] Tobacco pipes -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[A24F 1/02] with arrangements for cleaning or cooling the smoke -- A24F 1/02 designates tobacco-pipe inventions that incorporate features for cleaning or cooling the smoke, such as filters, water or air chambers, cooling fins, or removable cleaning elements, aimed at improving the smoking experience. This subclass falls within the broader category of smokers' requisites for tobacco pipes.
[A24F 1/04] with smoke chamber or slobber traps -- A24F 1/04 pertains to tobacco pipes that incorporate a smoke chamber or a slobber-trap device designed to collect and retain moisture or condensate from the smoker's breath, enhancing the pipe's functionality and cleanliness. This subgroup falls under HUMAN NECESSITIES → TOBACCO → SMOKERS' REQUISITES → Tobacco pipes.
[A24F 1/06] inside the pipe -- A24F 1/06 covers elements situated inside a tobacco pipe, such as the bore, stem, filter, moisture chamber or any internal component that influences airflow, heat or flavor. The subclass includes designs, materials and modifications of these interior parts to improve smoking performance or user comfort.
[A24F 1/08] inside the stem -- A24F 1/08 pertains to the interior of the pipe stem, covering cavities, channels, or other internal structures that influence airflow, cooling, or moisture management in tobacco pipes. This subclass falls under the CPC hierarchy for smokers' requisites within human necessities.
[A24F 1/10] inside the bowl -- A24F 1/10 covers the interior of a tobacco-pipe bowl, including the shape, surface treatment, coating, cleaning or other modifications of the space where tobacco is placed and burned.
[A24F 11/00] Seasoning of tobacco pipes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices or method for smoking a pipe the first time or conditioning it again after a long period of non-use
[A24F 1/12] outside the pipe -- A24F 1/12 pertains to the external parts of tobacco pipes, such as the body, stem, bowl rim and any decorative or protective coverings that form the pipe's outer surface. It includes the materials, shapes and finishes applied to the exterior, but not the internal smoking chamber or airflow components.
[A24F 1/14] with liquid-container -- A24F 1/14 covers tobacco pipes that incorporate a liquid container, such as water or other fluids, to filter or cool the smoke. The classification includes designs where the pipe body is connected to a reservoir that holds the liquid, affecting the inhaled smoke's temperature and composition. It falls under the hierarchy of human necessities, tobacco, smokers' requisites, and tobacco pipes.
[A24F 1/16] with zigzag or like passages for the smoke -- A24F 1/16 designates tobacco pipes that incorporate zigzag or serpentine passages for the smoke, a feature intended to lengthen the airflow path, cool the smoke and enhance filtration. It is a sub-category of pipe designs within the smokers' requisites group.
[A24F 1/18] with non-absorbent linings -- A24F 1/18 in the Cooperative Patent Classification, used by the EPO and USPTO, specifies tobacco pipes that feature non-absorbent linings. This classification helps in identifying patents related to tobacco pipes designed to prevent moisture absorption, enhancing smoking experience or pipe maintenance.
[A24F 1/20] with absorbent linings -- A24F 1/20 refers to tobacco pipes that incorporate an absorbent lining, such as ceramic, felt or other porous materials, to soak up moisture, tar or condensate from the smoke. This sub-class covers designs where the lining is integral to the pipe's bowl or shank, improving taste and reducing clogging, and is distinguished from plain-bore pipes that lack such moisture-absorbing features.
[A24F 1/22] with arrangements for cooling by air, e.g. pipes with double walls -- A24F 1/22 covers tobacco pipes that provide cooling by passing air around the smoke, typically through double-walled or similar constructions that create a flow of cooling air between the walls of the pipe. This sub-class is part of the hierarchy Human Necessities → Tobacco → Smokers' Requisites → Tobacco Pipes.
[A24F 1/24] for burning the tobacco from below -- The subclass A24F 1/24 covers tobacco-pipe designs in which the combustion zone is located beneath the tobacco, using a heat source or flame directed upward to ignite the material. It includes mechanisms that generate heat below the bowl and channel it to burn the tobacco from below.
[A24F 1/26] with filling devices -- A24F 1/26 covers tobacco pipes that incorporate a filling device, meaning any built-in or removable mechanism-such as a chamber, cartridge, or loading tube-designed to receive and hold tobacco before the pipe is smoked.
[A24F 1/28] Tubular pipes, e.g. in the form of cigars -- A24F 1/28 covers tubular smoking pipes that are shaped like cigars, including those made from a single elongated tube of material. The classification falls under human necessities → tobacco → smokers' requisites → tobacco pipes, and pertains specifically to pipe designs that resemble a cigar form for delivering tobacco smoke.
[A24F 1/30] Hookahs -- A24F 1/30 designates hookahs, also called water pipes, which heat tobacco in a bowl and draw the smoke through a water-filled chamber before inhalation. The entry covers the overall device and its principal components such as the bowl, stem, hose and water reservoir, classifying it among tobacco-pipe products.
[A24F 13/00] Appliances for smoking cigars or cigarettes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Appliances useful during the smoking experience
[A24F 13/02] Cigar or cigarette holders -- A24F 13/02 covers devices designed to hold cigars or cigarettes while being smoked, such as pipes, holders, or clamps that support the tobacco article and keep it steady in the hand. These items are classified under appliances for smoking cigars or cigarettes, part of the broader categories of smokers' requisites and human necessities. They facilitate handling, positioning, and sometimes combustion control of the tobacco product.
[A24F 13/04] with arrangements for cleaning or cooling the smoke -- A24F 13/04 covers smoking devices such as cigars or cigarette appliances that incorporate mechanisms to clean or cool the smoke before inhalation, for example filters, water chambers or cooling passages. This subclass falls under the Cooperative Patent Classification hierarchy for human necessities, tobacco, and smokers' requisites, detailing designs that improve smoke quality and user comfort.
[A24F 13/06] with smoke filters -- This subclass covers smoking appliances that incorporate a smoke filter, such as cigarettes, cigars or related devices equipped with filter tips or built-in filtering elements. It falls under the broader category of appliances for smoking cigars or cigarettes within the tobacco group.
[A24F 13/08] with special devices, e.g. spikes or grippers, for holding the cigars or cigarettes -- This subclass covers smoking appliances that incorporate special devices such as spikes or grippers to hold cigars or cigarettes in place, improving stability and handling during use.
[A24F 13/10] with end-ejectors -- The A24F 13/10 subclass covers smoking appliances that incorporate end-ejector mechanisms, which expel the burnt cigarette, cigar or similar product from the device after use. It includes mechanical, pneumatic or spring-loaded systems designed to remove the spent article automatically.
[A24F 13/12] combined with other objects, e.g. writing utensils -- A24F 13/12 refers to smoking appliances that are integrated with other items, for example a cigar or cigarette holder that also incorporates a writing utensil, combining a smoking device with an additional functional object.
[A24F 13/14] Protecting cases -- A24F 13/14 refers to protective cases for cigars, cigarettes or related smoking implements. These accessories shield the items from physical damage, moisture or contamination while permitting convenient transport and storage. They belong to the broader group of tobacco smoking appliances.
[A24F 13/16] Safety sleeves for cigars or cigarettes preventing damage by glowing ash -- A24F 13/16 covers protective sleeves that slide onto cigars or cigarettes to shield the holder and nearby surfaces from the heat and damage of glowing ash. These safety accessories are classified as smoking-related appliances under the CPC hierarchy for tobacco and smokers' requisites.
[A24F 13/18] Extinguishers for cigars or cigarettes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Extinguishers per se.
[A24F 1/32] Selection of materials for pipes -- A24F 1/32 deals with the selection of materials for tobacco pipes, focusing on properties such as heat-resistance, durability, moisture control and influence on flavor. It covers guidance on metals, ceramics, wood and synthetic composites used for pipe bowls, stems and mouthpieces.
[A24F 13/20] Means for cutting-off the glowing ash -- This subgroup covers devices designed to detach or remove the hot ash from cigars or cigarettes, preventing it from falling onto the user or surroundings. Such means include ash trays with cutting mechanisms, clip-on ash removers, or built-in cutting edges on smoking appliances.
[A24F 13/22] Supports for holding cigars or cigarettes while smoking -- A24F 13/22 covers devices that support cigars or cigarettes during use, such as holders, trays or stands that keep the product steady while being smoked. These accessories are part of the broader category of smoking appliances within the Human Necessities section of the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[A24F 13/24] Cigar cutters, slitters, or perforators, e.g. combined with lighters -- It covers devices designed to trim or puncture cigars or cigarettes, such as cutters, slitters, or perforators, often integrated with a lighter. These tools are used by smokers to prepare tobacco for lighting. The classification falls under appliances for smoking cigars or cigarettes within the human necessities category.
[A24F 13/26] formed as pocket devices -- A24F 13/26 covers portable smoking appliances that are shaped or sized to fit in a pocket, such as pocket-sized lighters, compact pipe-like devices, or miniature cigar holders designed for easy carriage and discreet use. These items are intended for personal consumption of cigars or cigarettes and fall under the broader category of smokers' requisites.
[A24F 13/28] Devices for blowing through cigars -- A24F 13/28 defines devices that actively draw air through a cigar, including mechanical blowers, forced-draw pipes and similar accessories that generate suction or airflow to enhance smoking without manual inhalation. These appliances may be handheld or mounted and are intended to improve draw ease or flavor.
[A24F 13/30] Arrangements for producing smoke images or rings -- A24F 13/30 covers arrangements that generate visual smoke patterns, such as rings or images, for decorative, signalling or entertainment purposes in smoking devices. It includes mechanisms that shape, direct or modulate the emitted smoke to form defined shapes.
[A24F 15/00] Receptacles or boxes specially adapted for cigars, cigarettes, simulated smoking devices or cigarettes therefor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Adaptations of receptacles or boxes for use in vehicles -> B60N3/12 - Packages specially adapted for cigarettes -> B65D85/10
[A24F 15/005] with means for limiting the frequency of smoking, e.g. with time-control, counting means -- A24F 15/005 refers to cigarette or cigar storage containers that incorporate devices to restrict how often they can be used, such as timers, counters, or other frequency-limiting mechanisms. It falls under the tobacco-related category of receptacles specially adapted for smokers' requisites.
[A24F 15/01] specially adapted for simulated smoking devices or cigarettes therefor -- A24F 15/01 pertains to receptacles or boxes that are specially adapted for simulated-smoking devices or the cigarettes used with them. [A24F 15/015] with means for refilling of liquid inhalable precursors -- A24F 15/015 pertains to receptacles or boxes designed for cigars, cigarettes or simulated smoking devices that incorporate a system for refilling liquid inhalable precursors, such as e-liquids or other vaporizable substances, enabling users to replenish the supply without removing the container.
[A24F 15/02] for domestic use -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With means for limiting the frequency of smoking -> A24F15/005
[A24F 15/04] with appliances for releasing a single cigar or cigarette -- A24F 15/04 covers specially designed receptacles or boxes for cigars, cigarettes or simulated smoking devices that incorporate a mechanism to dispense one individual cigar or cigarette at a time. [A24F 15/06] with means for offering -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With appliances for releasing a single cigar or cigarette -> A24F15/04

Typical examples include containers that incorporate lighters, pen holders, storage compartments or electronic components, providing multifunctional use.
[A24F 15/10] with lighters -- A24F 15/10 covers receptacles or boxes for cigars, cigarettes or simulated smoking devices that incorporate an integrated lighter. [A24F 19/00] Ash-trays -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ashtrays in any form.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arrangements in vehicle passenger accommodation -> B60N3/08
[A24F 19/0007] for chair arms -- This CPC subclass (A24F 19/0007) covers ash-tray devices specifically designed to be attached to or integrated within chair armrests, allowing smokers to deposit ash while seated. It pertains to furniture accessories that combine seating support with ash-collection functionality.
[A24F 19/0014] in one piece -- A24F 19/0014 designates ash-trays that are produced as a single, integral unit rather than assembled from separate parts. The classification covers any one-piece construction, such as molded, stamped or cast ash-trays, including built-in features that are formed together with the tray body.
[A24F 19/0021] comprising self-cleaning means -- A24F 19/0021 covers ash-tray designs intended for smokers that incorporate self-cleaning mechanisms, such as automatic ash removal, vibration, disposable liners or absorbent materials that reduce the need for manual cleaning. The subclass applies to any ashtray that integrates these cleaning functions within its structure.
[A24F 19/0028] of the disposable type  A24F19/025  takes precedence -- The subclass A24F 19/0028 refers to disposable ash-trays, such as single-use paper, cardboard or plastic receptacles designed to collect cigarette ash. It is superseded by the broader subclass A24F 19/025, which takes precedence for all disposable-type ash-tray designs.
[A24F 19/0035] with supporting means for the cigars or cigarettes -- A24F 19/0035 covers ash-trays that incorporate a support device for holding cigars or cigarettes while they are being smoked, such as a holder, cradle or inclined platform integrated into the ash-tray design.
[A24F 19/0042] with smoke filtering devices -- The CPC subclass A24F 19/0042 covers ash-trays that are equipped with smoke-filtering devices. It falls under the hierarchy Human Necessities → Tobacco → Smokers' Requisites → Ash-trays, and pertains to designs where the ash-tray incorporates a mechanism to filter or reduce smoke emitted from cigarettes.
[A24F 19/005] comprising a grid or grille -- A24F 19/005 refers to ash-trays that incorporate a grid or grille, a design element that separates ash and tobacco debris from the surface, facilitating cleaning and preventing the spread of ash. This subclass falls under the smokers' requisites category within the tobacco division of the Human Necessities section.
[A24F 19/0057] tiltable, e.g. for emptying  A24F19/06  takes precedence -- A24F 19/0057 refers to ash-trays that are designed to tilt, typically to facilitate the removal of accumulated ash or debris. This feature falls under the smokers' requisites section of the Cooperative Patent Classification, specifically within the broader category of tobacco-related human necessities. It is subordinate to the more general tilting ash-tray concept defined in A24F 19/06.
[A24F 19/0064] of the pocket type -- A24F 19/0064 covers ash-tray designs intended to be carried in a pocket, providing a compact, portable means for smokers to collect ash and cigarette remnants while on the move. These pocket-type ashtrays are typically small, lightweight, and often made of metal or plastic for easy handling.
[A24F 19/0071] Stands for ashtrays -- The code A24F 19/0071 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers accessories designed to hold ashtrays, specifically stands or supports that elevate an ashtray for convenience and stability. These items fall under the human necessities sector, tobacco subcategory, smokers' requisites.
[A24F 19/0078] comprising two separatable parts, e.g. coaxial  A24F19/02  takes precedence -- A24F 19/0078 relates to ash-trays that are made of two separable parts, for example a coaxial arrangement where an inner container fits inside an outer one and can be detached for cleaning, replacement or separate use.
[A24F 19/0085] one of the parts being covering or closing means -- A24F 19/0085 covers ash-tray components that act as a cover or closing element, such as lids, sliding or hinged covers, and other means that seal the receptacle. These features are intended to contain ash, reduce odor and prevent spillage while the ash-tray remains in use. The classification falls under Human Necessities → Tobacco → Smokers' Requisites → Ash-trays.
[A24F 19/0092] Fixing means for ashtrays on other objects -- A24F 19/0092 covers devices that attach ashtrays to other surfaces or objects, such as brackets, clamps, adhesives or mounting fixtures, allowing a stable placement on tables, walls, vehicles or furniture. The code includes any means specifically designed to fix an ashtray onto a host item.
[A24F 19/02] with removable insets -- Ash-tray designs that incorporate removable inserts, allowing the tray's interior to be taken out for cleaning, replacement, or customization. The removable inset can be a separate compartment, filter holder, or decorative element that can be detached without dismantling the whole ash-tray. This feature is classified under CPC A24F 19/02 for smokers' requisites.
[A24F 19/025] of the disposable type -- A24F 19/025 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the European and United States patent offices, specifies ash-trays that are of the disposable type. This classification is part of a broader category covering smokers' requisites under human necessities.
[A24F 19/04] with depressible false floor -- An ash-tray equipped with a false floor that can be pressed down to receive ash and debris, allowing the collected material to be removed easily for cleaning. This feature improves hygiene and convenience for smokers.
[A24F 19/06] with tiltable bowl or false floor -- A24F 19/06 covers ash-trays that incorporate a tiltable bowl or a false floor to aid ash removal and prevent spillage. These designs are intended for smokers to direct ash away and improve cleanliness.
[A24F 19/08] with slidably mounted false floor -- A24F 19/08 refers to ash-trays that incorporate a false floor which can be slid open or shifted, creating a separate compartment for ash and residue. The movable floor facilitates cleaning and can isolate spent material from the main tray, improving hygiene for smokers.
[A24F 19/09] with automatically-acting safety devices, e.g. heat-responsive -- A24F 19/09 covers ash-trays equipped with safety mechanisms that activate automatically, typically in response to heat from a cigarette or cigar, to prevent fire or injury.
[A24F 19/10] combined with other articles -- A24F 19/10 refers to ash-trays that are integrated with other objects, such as holders, lighters, or containers, forming a combined article. It falls under the smokers' requisites category of the Human Necessities section, specifically addressing designs where the ash-tray is incorporated into a multifunctional product.
[A24F 19/105] with ornamental objects -- The CPC subclass A24F 19/105 refers to ash-trays that incorporate decorative or ornamental objects, such as artistic inserts, figures or embellishments integrated into the tray's design. This entry falls under Human Necessities → Tobacco → Smokers' Requisites and addresses aesthetic enhancements of ash-tray items.
[A24F 19/12] with match-boxes -- A24F 19/12 pertains to ash-trays that include an integrated match-box or compartment for storing matches, enabling users to keep and retrieve matches directly from the ash-tray. This subcategory falls under the smokers' requisites segment of the tobacco group.
[A24F 19/14] with extinguishers -- Ash-trays that incorporate built-in extinguishing devices for safely putting out tobacco waste, such as cigarette butts and matches, are covered by this subclass. The feature is an integrated fire-suppressing element designed to reduce fire risk while handling smoking remnants. This entry falls under the smokers' requisites group of the CPC.
[A24F 19/145] using fluid or sand -- A24F 19/145 covers ash-tray designs that employ a fluid (such as water or oil) or sand to extinguish, dampen or contain cigarette ash and embers. The feature is intended to improve safety or cleanliness by quickly cooling or absorbing the ash after disposal.

[A24F 23/00] Cases for tobacco, snuff, or chewing tobacco -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cases or containers specially for tobacco, snuff or chewing tobacco.
[A24F 23/02] Tobacco pouches -- A24F 23/02 covers tobacco pouches, which are containers designed to hold and protect tobacco, snuff, or chewing tobacco for personal use. The classification includes all forms of pouch construction, materials, closure mechanisms and accessories intended specifically for storing tobacco products.
[A24F 23/04] combined with other objects, e.g. with filling devices for pipes -- The CPC class A24F 23/04 covers tobacco products that are integrated with additional items, such as filling devices for pipes, so the tobacco and the accessory form a combined unit. It addresses designs where the tobacco is pre-packed or attached to a mechanism that facilitates loading or use, typically for cigarettes, cigars, snuff or chewing tobacco. This subclass is a narrow branch under the "Smokers' requisites" group of the Human necessities section.
[A24F 25/00] Devices used by the smoker for controlling the moisture content of, or for scenting, cigars, cigarettes or tobacco -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cigar, cigarette or tobacco humidors.
[A24F 25/02] Moistening devices -- A24F 25/02 pertains to devices that add or retain moisture, or impart scent, to cigars, cigarettes or other tobacco products to improve the smoker's experience. It includes humidifiers, moisture packs, scented inserts and similar accessories used by smokers.
[A24F 27/00] Match receptacles or boxes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any kind of receptacles for matches.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Compositions of strike-surfaces, matches -> C06F
[A24F 2700/00] Tobacco pipes -- A24F 2700/00 denotes the class for tobacco pipes, encompassing devices used by smokers to hold and draw tobacco. The classification covers various pipe types, designs, materials and related accessories intended for the consumption of tobacco.
[A24F 2700/01] Collapsible pipes and pipes in general -- A24F 2700/01 covers inventions relating to tobacco pipes, including the overall design and construction of both standard and collapsible pipe forms. It is applied to devices that enable the pipe to be folded or reduced in size for storage or transport while maintaining functionality for smoking.
[A24F 2700/03] Pipes with cooling or zigzag circulation of the smoke -- A24F 2700/03 classifies tobacco pipes that incorporate a cooling system or a zig-zag smoke-circulation channel, designed to lower the temperature and smooth the delivery of smoke to the user. This subcategory falls under the hierarchy of Human Necessities → Tobacco → Smokers' Requisites → Tobacco pipes.
[A24F 2700/04] Pipes filled with absorbant materials, pipes with devices filtering the smoke -- The code covers tobacco pipes that incorporate internal absorbent or filtering elements, such as porous plugs, charcoal or other materials that retain moisture or remove particulates, and pipes equipped with built-in or removable filters. It applies to designs where the pipe's bore or mouthpiece contains a device specifically intended to modify the smoke before it reaches the user.
[A24F 2700/08] Bowl-covers or accessories for smokers' pipes -- A24F 2700/08 covers bowl-covers and other accessories designed for smokers' pipes. It falls under the CPC hierarchy Human Necessities > Tobacco > Smokers' Requisites > Tobacco pipes.
[A24F 27/02] with means for storing the matches separately -- The A24F 27/02 subclass covers match receptacles or boxes that include a dedicated compartment for storing matches apart from the striking surface. It specifies designs where the matches are kept separate, often in a built-in drawer or sealed chamber, to protect them from moisture and accidental ignition. This is used for tobacco-related accessories such as smokers' matchboxes.
[A24F 27/04] Protective coverings for match receptacles -- Protective coverings for match receptacles are accessories such as cases, sleeves or holders that shield matches from moisture, damage or accidental ignition, often used as part of tobacco-related smoking equipment. The CPC entry A24F 27/04 classifies these protective items for matchboxes or similar containers.
[A24F 27/06] with wind-guards -- A24F 27/06 covers match receptacles or boxes that incorporate wind-guards, features designed to protect matches from drafts that could cause premature ignition or extinguishing. The classification falls under the tobacco-related subgroup of smokers' requisites within the broader human necessities category.
[A24F 27/08] Stands for match-boxes -- A24F 27/08 classifies devices that support or hold match-boxes, such as portable stands, racks or holders used to keep the boxes upright and accessible for smokers. These items are considered accessories within the tobacco-related "smokers' requisites" subgroup.
[A24F 27/10] Receptacles for matches or match-boxes to be supported on the wall -- The entry A24F 27/10 refers to wall-mounted receptacles designed specifically to hold matches or match-boxes, typically used by smokers as part of their tobacco-related accessories. It covers the structural features that allow the container to be securely attached to a wall, provide easy access to the matches, and protect them from damage or accidental ignition.
[A24F 27/12] Match-books -- A24F 27/12 covers match-books, the small portable packages that hold a strip of matches for personal use. These receptacles are classified under smokers' requisites within the broader tobacco and human necessities categories.
[A24F 27/14] Match dispensers -- A24F 27/14 relates to devices that store and present matches, such as boxes or other receptacles that protect the matches from moisture and enable easy removal for lighting. These dispensers are intended for smokers to keep matches convenient and safe for use.
[A24F 27/16] for pocket use -- A24F 27/16 covers small, portable match containers intended for personal, pocket-size use by smokers. These devices are compact boxes or receptacles that hold matches for easy access while on the go.
[A24F 27/18] with means preventing spontaneous ignition -- A24F 27/18 covers match receptacles or boxes equipped with safety features that stop accidental ignition, such as protective covers, friction-free striking surfaces, or chemical inhibitors that prevent spontaneous flame.
[A24F 27/20] with means for igniting the match as it is dispensed -- A24F 27/20 covers match receptacles or boxes that incorporate an ignition mechanism which lights a match as it is withdrawn. The entry applies to combined storage-and-strike devices used by smokers, providing a convenient way to obtain a lit match directly from the container.
[A24F 27/22] for domestic use -- A24F 27/22 covers match receptacles or boxes intended for domestic use, such as household matchboxes, matchbooks and small match holders designed for home environments. The class groups containers that store and protect matches for everyday personal smoking or lighting needs within private residences.
[A24F 27/24] with means preventing spontaneous ignition -- A24F 27/24 covers match receptacles or boxes that incorporate mechanisms to prevent spontaneous ignition, such as protective covers, safety strikers or friction strips, ensuring matches remain safe until they are intentionally lit.
[A24F 27/26] with means for igniting the match as it is dispensed -- A matchbox that incorporates an ignition mechanism which automatically lights a match when it is released from the container. The device combines a storage compartment for matches with a built-in striker or spark source, allowing the user to light a match simply by pulling it out.
